TL;DR: Introduction of graph-based tools significantly improves accuracy and reduces hallucination in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems when tested on a challenging QA benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the deficiencies of large language models (LLMs) in accuracy and hallucination, proposing graph structures to enhance retrieval-augmented generation systems for precise information handling.

Method: The authors design an agentic system combining vector search and graph query tools on structured datasets derived from Wikipedia. Performance is evaluated using MoNaCo, a benchmark designed for complex Wikipedia-based queries.

Result: Graph-based tools in RAG systems improve precision, recall, and truthfulness while halving hallucinated answers, with only a modest increase in token usage.

Conclusion: Graph-enhanced RAG systems are promising for boosting factual correctness and minimizing hallucination in handling complex information queries.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have fundamentally transformed the landscape of Natural Language Processing. Despite these advances, LLMs and LLM-based systems remain prone to a variety of failure modes. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems have emerged as a common deployment scenario seeking to both avoid the well known risk of the LLM "hallucinating" information, and to enable reasoning and question answering over proprietary information that the LLM did not have access to during training without resorting to expensive model fine-tuning.
  In this work, we explore the idea of using a lightweight graph structure with a relatively simple graph schema, to support the RAG subsystem via a dedicated toolset. We design an agentic system with a variety of vector search and graph query tools operating over a structured dataset based on a curated subset of English Wikipedia articles, and evaluate its performance on questions from MoNaCo, a challenging Wikipedia QA benchmark of complex query answering tasks.
  Our results show that the introduction of graph-based tools can significantly increase the precision and recall of factual correctness, can halve the number of hallucinated answers, and achieves the highest fine-grained truthfulness score among the three evaluated scenarios. All this with a modest increase in token usage.

</details>


### [176] [ACE-SQL: Adaptive Co-Optimization via Empirical Credit Assignment for Text-to-SQL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05906)
*Xiaobing Chen,Ai Jian,Eryu Guo,Zhiqi Pang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces ACE-SQL, a reinforcement learning framework for Text-to-SQL tasks that jointly optimizes schema retrieval and SQL generation using execution feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for Text-to-SQL struggle with accurately linking complex database schemas to SQL generation, either leaving schema linking implicit or relying on static supervision that may not align with the generator's needs.

Method: ACE-SQL dynamically constructs column-set pools using generator rollouts and adjusts retrieval targets based on the frequency of execution-correct SQL queries, enabling adaptive co-optimization between schema retrieval and SQL generation via reinforcement learning.

Result: ACE-SQL achieves 65.3% execution accuracy on BIRD Dev with a token efficiency of 0.93k output tokens per query using a dataset of 3k synthetic Text-to-SQL pairs for training.

Conclusion: ACE-SQL enhances schema linking and SQL generation accuracy by implementing a reinforcement learning model that optimizes both processes through adaptive and bidirectional feedback mechanisms.

Abstract: Text-to-SQL maps natural language questions to executable SQL queries. Modern databases often contain large and complex schemas, making schema linking a critical step for accurate SQL generation. Existing methods either rely on full-schema generation, which leaves schema linking implicit within a large search space, or use a separate retriever trained with static gold-column supervision, whose targets may be suboptimal for the current generator policy. To address this issue, we propose Adaptive Co-optimization via Empirical Credit Assignment for Text-to-SQL (ACE-SQL), a reinforcement learning (RL) framework that jointly optimizes schema retrieval and SQL generation under execution feedback. ACE-SQL constructs an online column-set pool from generator rollouts and derives adaptive on-policy retrieval targets from the column set most frequently associated with execution-correct rollouts. This induces bidirectional adaptation, where the retriever adapts toward column sets that the generator can execute correctly, while the generator adapts to the retriever's evolving schema selections under execution feedback. With approximately 3k synthetic Text-to-SQL question-database pairs for RL training, ACE-SQL achieves 65.3% greedy execution accuracy on BIRD Dev while using 0.93k output tokens per query. The repository is available at https://github.com/xbchen1/ACE-SQL.

</details>


### [177] [Better Literary Translation: A Multi-Aspect Data Generation and LLM Training Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05924)
*Zhihao Lin,Ziqi Zhu,Hao Huang,Guanghui Wang,Peiyang He*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes an iterative refinement framework for literary translation using Large Language Models (LLMs) to balance fluency and literary effects, surpassing existing methods in quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address challenges in literary translation, particularly the lack of annotated data and the difficulty of aligning fluency with literary impact.

Method: A multi-aspect iterative refinement framework utilizing specialized LLMs, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), reinforcement learning, and metrics like CEA100 to assess progress.

Result: The proposed methods outperform traditional ground truths by 8.65 CEA100 points in SFT, with a further improvement of 1.51 points using GRPO in reinforcement learning. Developed models perform competitively with benchmarks in English-to-Chinese literary translation.

Conclusion: The LitMT models (8B and 14B) result in state-of-the-art performance and exhibit strong adaptability to out-of-domain literary translations, highlighting the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

Abstract: Literary translation poses unique challenges due to the scarcity of high-quality annotated data and the need to balance expression fluency with literary effect. We present a multi-aspect iterative refinement framework that generates high-quality translation references and preference data through specialized LLM translators, each targeting a distinct quality dimension. We leverage the generated data for supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning. Experiments show that our generated references outperform the original ground truth for SFT by 8.65 CEA100 points. For reinforcement learning, we find that DPO leads to performance degradation in this setting, while leveraging an explicit reward model for GRPO yields an additional 1.51 point improvement. We attribute this to the stability of two-stage training and GRPO's online exploration capability. Our resulting models, LitMT-8B and LitMT-14B, achieve 67.25 and 69.07 CEA100 respectively on the MetaphorTrans English-to-Chinese literary translation benchmark, competitive with Claude Sonnet 4.5 at 68.43, and demonstrate strong generalization to out-of-domain literary work (i.e., O. Henry).

</details>


### [178] [To Be Multimodal or Not to Be: Query-Adaptive Audio-Visual Person Retrieval via Active Modality Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05931)
*Erfan Loweimi,Mengjie Qian,Kate Knill,Guanfeng Wu,Chi-Ho Chan,Abbas Haider,Muhammad Awan,Josef Kittler,Hui Wang,Mark Gales*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a query-adaptive framework for person retrieval from video archives based on voice and face, achieving superior precision by distinguishing active modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of multimodal person retrieval in real-world videos where targets might not always be both seen and heard.

Method: The method involves detecting active modalities via cross-modal score consistency, which determines whether both voice and face are present and effective. This consistency drives classifiers to improve retrieval accuracy.

Result: The proposed adaptive system demonstrated 94.2% precision-at-1 (P@1) on the BBC Rewind corpus, outperforming unimodal and fixed fusion approaches. It showed substantial improvement over speaker-only systems and competed closely with the ground-truth modality label oracle.

Conclusion: Query-adaptive modality detection improves person retrieval systems in real-world scenarios, addressing limitations of noise in multimodal fusion and effectively recovering performance gaps.

Abstract: When retrieving a person from a video archive by voice and face, should the system be multimodal or not? In real-world broadcast archives, unlike curated benchmarks, a target may be heard but unseen, seen but unheard, or both. Fusing scores from an absent modality injects noise, degrading precision below the best unimodal system. We propose a query-adaptive framework that detects active modalities via cross-modal score consistency: when both modalities are active, files retrieved by one also score highly on the other; this agreement breaks down when a modality is absent. Classifiers driven by these cross-modal features achieve 89% detection accuracy. On the BBC Rewind corpus (with over 12,000 broadcast videos) the adaptive system attains 94.2% P@1, outperforming speaker-only (82.9%), face-only (93.4%), and fixed fusion (90.0%), recovering 64% of the gap to an oracle with ground-truth modality labels (96.6%).

</details>


### [179] [Epistemic Injustice in Language Models: An Audit of Pretraining Filters and Guardrails](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05936)
*Marco Antonio Stranisci,A Pranav,Rossana Damiano,Christian Hardmeier,Anne Lauscher*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper assesses how filtering in language models disproportionately erases content about marginalized groups while failing to address harmful content.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how existing filtering and moderation mechanisms in language models lead to biases, epistemic erasure, and conflicts with human judgment.

Method: The authors audited four pretraining filters and three inference-time guardrails using Common Crawl data and a set of 500 manually annotated sentences focused on mentions of gender and regional origin. They analyzed relationships with blocklist-based cues and human annotation judgments.

Result: The study found that filtering mechanisms often relied heavily on blocklist cues, leading to over-flagging of content related to marginalized groups like transgender people, women, and Central Americans. However, the filters often missed harmful content such as private information leaks and hate speech.

Conclusion: The research reveals significant challenges in current automated content filtering systems, highlighting their role in epistemic erasure and their inability to address nuanced harms recognized by human annotators.

Abstract: Modern language models rely on pretraining filters to remove undesirable content from training corpora and inference-time guardrails to suppress undesirable outputs during deployment. In this paper, we examine how these filtering and moderation decisions produce forms of epistemic erasure and reveal tensions both across automated systems and between these systems and human judgment. We audit four pretraining filters and three inference-time guardrails on Common Crawl sentences containing gender and regional-origin mentions, together with a manually annotated subset of 500 sentences. Our analysis shows that filtering and guardrail decisions are strongly associated with blocklist-based lexical cues, while frequently failing to flag content containing private information or explicit hate speech. At the same time, marginalized groups, particularly transgender people, women, and Central Americans, are significantly over-flagged across systems. Human annotators, by contrast, would retain 88.5\% of filter-flagged and 91.3\% of guardrail-flagged content, often recognizing representational harms arising from tensions of content removal that current systems fail to capture. Taken together, our findings document a form of epistemic erasure in which mentions of marginalized groups are disproportionately removed before pretraining and additionally suppressed again at inference time.

</details>


### [180] [Large Language Models are Perplexed by some Political Parties](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05937)
*Paul Lerner,François Yvon*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Large Language Models (LLMs) display political bias, being more perplexed by far-right and nationalist texts compared to social-democratic ones, across multiple models and languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate the political fairness of LLMs, which has been scarcely studied despite their increasing use in sensitive applications, including politics.

Method: The study measures the perplexity of LLMs across ten models and three datasets in 37 languages, testing whether they assign equal probabilities (fairness) to texts from different political orientations.

Result: LLMs exhibited greater perplexity with far-right and nationalist texts, indicating a bias, which is consistent with earlier studies on translation fairness. Additionally, this bias originates from pretraining and remains unaffected by instruction-tuning.

Conclusion: LLMs are not politically neutral; their pretraining processes embed political bias, which persists across tasks and is not significantly mitigated by instruction-tuning.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used, including in political applications, but their political fairness has been little studied. We assess it using perplexity, posing that a fair model should give equal probability to all political groups. However, we find, across ten LLMs and three datasets covering 37 languages, that LLMs are more perplexed by the texts of far right and nationalist parties than of social-democratic parties. We find this to be consistent with previous work on translation fairness, to the point that perplexity correlates with downstream translation metrics. Our method is applicable to both base LLMs as well as their instruction-tuned counterpart, and we find that both are highly correlated, suggesting that the political fairness of LLMs stems from their pretraining, and is hardly affected by instruction-tuning.

</details>


### [181] [Measuring the sensitivity of LLM-based structured extraction to prompt, model, and schema choices in clinical discharge summaries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05970)
*Martin Murin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates sensitivity in large language models used for clinical text extraction, without relying on human-annotated ground truth. Through controlled tests on schema design, model size, and prompt phrasing, findings highlight schema-induced disagreement and larger model effects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how structured extraction from clinical free-text notes depends on upstream configuration decisions, as such sensitivity measures are less explored compared to accuracy benchmarks.

Method: The authors fixed a clinical schema and tested sensitivity by alternately varying prompts and model sizes. They employed MIMIC-IV v3.1 discharge summaries, measuring cross-prompt agreement with Cohen's kappa and assessing schema impact using flagged categories and multi-class categorization.

Result: Sensitivity was observed largely tied to the schema’s absence-vs-silence distinction and model size's influence on output patterns. Larger models redistributed agreement across fields and directed predictions away from catch-all categories.

Conclusion: Large language models show schema-induced disagreement concentration and significant effects based on model size during clinical text extraction tasks, with a reusable methodology proposed for auditing extraction reproducibility in large-scale deployments.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly used for structured extraction from clinical free-text notes, but the sensitivity of their output to upstream configuration choices is less understood than their accuracy on fixed benchmarks. This work measures that sensitivity without human-annotated ground truth, by holding the extraction task fixed and varying one choice at a time. The fixed schema comprises 17 clinical documentation flags on a three-way yes/no/not_documented value set and a 47-tag vocabulary for the primary admission reason. Three prompt variants expressing this schema were each run at two model sizes on MIMIC-IV v3.1 discharge summaries. Cross-prompt agreement was measured by Cohen's kappa on ICD-stratified subsets. A paired same-note comparison isolated the effect of model choice, and a post-hoc collapse of the three-way flags to binary tested the schema's contribution to disagreement. On the three-way flags, the two models reach the same pooled cross-prompt agreement (median kappa 0.69 and 0.68); the larger model raises agreement on some fields and lowers it on others, a redistribution rather than the absence of an effect. Collapsing the schema to binary dissolves most of the cross-prompt disagreement, locating it on the absence-versus-silence distinction rather than on whether the finding is present. On the multi-class admission categorization, changing the model reassigns the dominant tag on close to half of all notes while changing the prompt phrasing reassigns it on roughly one in eight, and the larger model places far less mass on residual catch-all categories (44% to 26%). These patterns indicate a schema-imposed source of disagreement concentrated on the absence-versus-silence axis and a dominance of model over prompt phrasing on multi-class categorization, identified by a reusable methodology for auditing extraction reproducibility on a population-scale deployment.

</details>


### [182] [Beyond Alignment: Value Diversity as a Collective Property in Multicultural Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05985)
*Shaoyang Xu,Jingshen Zhang,Long P. Hoang,Jinyuan Li,Wenxuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces value diversity as a distinct evaluation metric for multicultural multi-agent systems, finding that current systems lack diversity and tend toward homogenization during social interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in evaluating whether multicultural multi-agent systems maintain cultural plurality, rather than just focusing on value alignment of individual agents with specific cultures.

Method: The authors defined a system-level evaluation axis called 'value diversity' and evaluated it using culturally conditioned agent responses to the World Values Survey. They tested 19 cultures and 18 backbone models, exploring system configurations and the impact of social interactions.

Result: The study finds that current multicultural agent systems exhibit significantly lower value diversity compared to human societies. Mixed-backbone systems improve diversity but do not fully close the gap, and social interactions further reduce diversity.

Conclusion: Value diversity is a complementary, system-level evaluation axis distinct from value alignment. Current multicultural agent systems exhibit homogenization tendencies, revealing a need for better designs to preserve cultural plurality.

Abstract: Multicultural multi-agent systems are increasingly deployed in globally diverse settings, where different agents are grounded in different cultural backgrounds. Existing cultural evaluation focuses on value alignment: how closely a single agent matches a target culture. Yet alignment is a per-agent property and cannot reveal whether a system, taken as a whole, preserves the cultural plurality it is meant to represent. We propose value diversity as a system-level evaluation axis for multicultural agent systems, defined through the dissimilarity between culturally conditioned agents' responses on a shared value survey. Using the World Values Survey, we evaluate 19 cultures and 18 backbone models across a wide range of system configurations. We find that diversity is largely uncorrelated with alignment, indicating that the two capture complementary system properties, and that current multicultural agent systems fall substantially below human societies in value diversity. Mixed-backbone systems narrow this gap but do not close it, and the gap persists across culture compositions and agent scales. Social interaction further erodes diversity by driving agents toward consensus, and a participatory budgeting case study shows that this homogenization narrows the breadth of collective decision-making. Together, our results establish value diversity as a distinct evaluation axis for multicultural multi-agent systems and reveal a persistent homogenization tendency in current LLM-based societies. Our code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/iNLP-Lab/MultiAgent-Diversity.

</details>


### [183] [The Generator-Eraser Paradox: Community Guidelines for Responsible LLM-Assisted Dialect Resource Creation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06004)
*Wajdi Zaghouani*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper addresses the opportunities and risks of using large language models (LLMs) in dialect resource development, proposing a framework and guidelines to prevent linguistic erasure and support authenticity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dialect resources are vital for scientific, cultural, and computational purposes, but the use of LLMs in their development can threaten linguistic diversity, particularly for marginalized dialects.

Method: The authors formalize the "generator-eraser paradox" as a theoretical framework, derive 12 community guidelines, and present a case study on Arabic dialects to showcase the application of these guidelines.

Result: The paper provides theoretical insights, practical guidelines, and a detailed examination of challenges in Arabic dialects to ensure LLMs can support dialect work without undermining linguistic diversity.

Conclusion: The work enables dialect communities and resource builders to leverage LLMs responsibly, preserving authenticity, diversity, and sovereignty in language resources.

Abstract: Dialect resources occupy a unique position at the intersection of scientific description, cultural preservation, and computational infrastructure. Large language models offer powerful capabilities for accelerating dialect resource development through retrieval-grounded drafting, corpus navigation, metadata enrichment, and annotation workflow support. However, the same systems pose substantial risks: they can contribute to dialect erasure by privileging prestige varieties, homogenizing orthography, and enabling synthetic feedback loops that reduce linguistic diversity over time. These risks are particularly acute for language varieties characterized by diglossia, limited written standardization, or marginalized speaker communities. This paper makes three contributions. First, we integrate insights from variationist sociolinguistics and corpus linguistics to formalize the generator-eraser paradox as a theoretical framework for understanding the dual nature of LLM-assisted dialect work. Second, we derive 12 community guidelines that operationalize this framework into implementable design requirements for dialect resource creation and documentation. Third, we provide an in-depth case study of Arabic dialects, including a structured comparison of widely used resources, to demonstrate how these guidelines address language-specific challenges including diglossia, orthographic variability, and community governance. The contribution is conceptual and operational rather than experimental, with the goal of enabling dialect communities and resource builders across languages to adopt LLMs without sacrificing authenticity, variation, or sovereignty.

</details>


### [184] [Contextualized Prompting For Stance Detection On Social Media](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06022)
*Tilman Beck,Shakib Yazdani,Simon Kruschinski,Marcus Maurer,Iryna Gurevych*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper explores how incorporating various types of contextual information into zero-shot prompting with LLMs affects stance detection on Twitter, finding mixed results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stance detection on social media is difficult due to the noisy, short, and context-dependent nature of online language. The study aims to improve zero-shot performance of LLMs by incorporating additional contextual information.

Method: The researchers used real-world, derived, and LLM-generated contextual features to enhance zero-shot LLM performance in stance detection. They tested this across four datasets, including a newly introduced German Twitter stance dataset, and analyzed the inclusion of additional tweets and user metadata.

Result: Incorporating contextual features improved performance in some scenarios but impeded it in others. LLM-generated target descriptions helped, while user metadata and additional tweets by the same user showed mixed or negative effects.

Conclusion: While context has potential, LLMs struggle to discern useful information from noise in real-world data. This highlights the challenges of incorporating contextual information in noisy environments like social media.

Abstract: Stance detection on social media is challenging due to short, noisy, and context-dependent language. While large language models (LLMs) show zero-shot generalization, they are typically prompted without contextual information, which limits their ability to interpret ambiguous posts. In this work, we systematically investigate the impact of incorporating real-world (e.g., user biographies), derived (e.g., political party), and LLM-generated (e.g., target descriptions) contextual features into zero-shot prompting for stance detection on Twitter. Our evaluation spans four benchmark datasets, including a new high-quality German Twitter stance dataset. Across multiple LLMs, we find that integrating contextual information improves performance, but only under specific conditions. LLM-generated target descriptions consistently enhance accuracy, while other user metadata has mixed or even detrimental effects. Notably, we show that the inclusion of other tweets by the same user, often beneficial in supervised learning, can impair performance due to input noise. Our qualitative analysis reveals that LLMs struggle to distinguish task-specific useful information from irrelevant context. Our findings highlight both the promise and challenges of prompting with context information in noisy real-world settings. We publish code and data at this \href{https://github.com/tilmanbeck/stance-context-twitter}{page}.

</details>


### [185] [EGTR-Review: Efficient Evidence-Grounded Scientific Peer Review Generation via Multi-Agent Teacher Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06025)
*Xinpeng Qiu,Wang Yihu,Zhifeng Liu,Xiaochen Wang,Jimin Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces EGTR-Review, a framework for automated peer review generation that offers evidence-grounded and traceable comments and overcomes challenges of generic outputs and high inference costs seen in prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to reduce the burden on peer reviewers and improve feedback quality by addressing issues with existing LLM-based review generation systems that produce generic, unsupported comments or incur high computational costs.

Method: The EGTR-Review framework uses a multi-agent teacher model to decompose papers, extract key elements, retrieve external evidence, label evidence states, conduct reasoning, and synthesize reviews. This process is then distilled into a smaller, faster student model using multi-task learning and an evidence-weighted objective to ensure quality.

Result: Experiments showed that the EGTR-Review (Student) model outperformed existing methods in terms of review quality, factual grounding, and efficiency while consuming less computational resources and inference time.

Conclusion: EGTR-Review provides a significant improvement in generating high-quality, evidence-grounded, and traceable peer reviews. Additionally, it achieves these enhancements with lower computational costs, demonstrating practicality for real-world applications.

Abstract: Scientific peer review generation has attracted increasing attention for reducing reviewing burdens and providing timely feedback. However, existing Large Language Model (LLM)-based methods often produce generic comments with insufficient evidence support and weak source traceability, while complex multi-agent systems incur high inference costs. To address these challenges, we propose EGTR-Review, an Evidence-Grounded and Traceable Review Generation framework via Multi-Agent Teacher Distillation. EGTR-Review first constructs a multi-agent teacher that performs structure-aware paper decomposition, key-element extraction, external scholarly evidence retrieval, evidence-state labeling, verification reasoning, and review synthesis. It then distills both intermediate reasoning trajectories and final review comments into a lightweight student model through task-prefix-driven multi-task learning. An evidence-weighted objective further reduces the influence of weak, missing, or non-verifiable supervision. Experiments on public peer-review datasets show that EGTR-Review (Student) outperforms strong prompt-based, fine-tuned, and structured/agentic baselines across automatic metrics, LLM-as-Judge evaluation, and human evaluation, while maintaining strong factual grounding and source traceability with substantially lower token consumption and inference time. Our code, prompts, configurations, and sample data are available on GitHub.

</details>


### [186] [NAVIRA: Decoupled Stochastic Remasking for Masked Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06031)
*Andrey Fomenko,Maksim Kryzhanovskiy,Svetlana Glazyrina,Roman Ischenko*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: NAVIRA improves masked diffusion language models by using a new decoding policy that separates remasking unreliable tokens from replacing them, enhancing fluency and diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the correction problem in masked diffusion language models, where early dependency errors contaminate later contexts due to coupled correction and regeneration processes.

Method: NAVIRA introduces a separate two-step process at inference time: one forward pass scores tokens and selects positions for remasking, while a second pass regenerates from the cleaned context. Stochastic remasking with temperature control reduces repeated corrections and balances fluency against diversity.

Result: On a 170M masked diffusion language model, NAVIRA improves fluency, preserves entropy, and achieves better LLM-judge scores, especially with larger forward-pass budgets.

Conclusion: Remasking strategies are critical to improving the reliability of masked-diffusion text generation, beyond the learned quality signal.

Abstract: Masked diffusion language models generate text by iteratively unmasking many tokens in parallel, but this speed comes with a correction problem: tokens generated in the same step are predicted from marginal distributions, and early local dependency errors can later contaminate the context. PRISM addresses this by learning token-level quality scores and remasking unreliable tokens, but its inference rule is coupled: the same forward pass both detects low-quality tokens and computes logits for their replacements, so the erroneous tokens still condition regeneration. We propose NAVIRA, an inference-time decoding policy that separates these two operations and samples remasking positions stochastically. A first forward pass scores tokens; selected tokens are masked; a second forward pass regenerates from the cleaned context. Temperature-controlled remasking reduces repeated correction of the same positions and balances fluency against diversity. In controlled experiments with a 170M masked diffusion language model, decoupling improves fluency, while scheduled stochastic remasking preserves entropy and achieves stronger LLM-judge scores under larger forward-pass budgets. These results show that remasking policy, not only the learned quality signal, is central to reliable masked-diffusion text generation.

</details>


### [187] [English-to-Prakrit Machine Translation via Multilingual Transfer Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06038)
*Om Choksi,Smit Kareliya,Shrikant Malviya,Pruthwik Mishra*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores English-to-Prakrit machine translation using a multilingual model adaptation strategy in a low-resource setting, yielding BLEU score improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to enable English-to-Prakrit translation, despite Prakrit being an unsupported language by existing language models like IndicTrans2.

Method: The method involves adapting a multilingual model by associating Prakrit with the Hindi language tag (hin_Deva), without making changes to the tokenizer, vocabulary, or architecture, and using a limited parallel corpus.

Result: The study reports BLEU metric improvements on a Maharashtri Prakrit parallel corpus and Ardhamagadhi test set, demonstrating feasibility of the approach.

Conclusion: The research shows that script-compatible language routing can support translation to unsupported classical languages, though performance is constrained by data scarcity and dialect variations.

Abstract: We study English-to-Prakrit machine translation in a low-resource setting where the target language is unsupported by IndicTrans2. We adapt the multilingual model by mapping Prakrit to the Hindi language tag (hin_Deva) without modifying the tokenizer, vocabulary, or architecture. Using a 1,474-pair Maharashtri Prakrit parallel corpus and evaluation on a 20-sample Ardhamagadhi test set, we report corpus BLEU improvements over an untuned baseline. The results indicate that script-compatible language routing can enable feasible transfer to unsupported classical languages, while highlighting limitations due to data scarcity and dialect mismatch. Our code and trained models are released to the public for further exploration https://github.com/D3v1s0m/indictrans2-prakrit-mt.

</details>


### [188] [IA-RAG: Interval-Algebra-Driven Temporal Reasoning for Dynamic Knowledge Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06044)
*Xiaoman Wang,Yaoze Zhang,Wenzhuo Fan,Hongwei Zhang,Ding Wang,Guohang Yan,Song Mao,Botian Shi,Yunshi Lan,Pinlong Cai*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: IA-RAG enhances temporal reasoning in Retrieval-Augmented Generation by modeling knowledge as time intervals and employing Interval Algebra.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of existing RAG frameworks in representing temporal structures and reasoning robustly with time-sensitive data.

Method: Proposes IA-RAG, which structures knowledge as Interval Event Units (IEUs) and organizes it hierarchically using Allen's Interval Algebra, while handling uncertainty with Sub-graph Time Tightening.

Result: Strong performance on benchmarks like TimeQA and ComplexTR, especially for complex temporal reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: IA-RAG effectively models and reasons about temporal knowledge, outperforming existing methods in scenarios requiring nuanced temporal understanding.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has shown strong effectiveness in grounding Large Language Models (LLMs) with external knowledge. However, existing RAG and Graph RAG frameworks largely treat knowledge as static or associate time with coarse-grained timestamps or metadata, failing to capture rich temporal structures such as duration, overlap, and containment. We propose IA-RAG, a hierarchical temporal RAG framework that models knowledge as time intervals and performs retrieval under formal temporal constraints. IA-RAG represents facts as Interval Event Units (IEUs) and organizes them into a hierarchical Thematic Forest, where temporal dependencies are governed by Allen's Interval Algebra. To handle incomplete or uncertain temporal boundaries, IA-RAG further introduces a Sub-graph Time Tightening mechanism that refines fuzzy intervals through logical constraints within connected event subgraphs. In addition, IA-RAG supports implicit temporal semantic retrieval through interval-algebra-guided traversal. Experiments on multiple temporal question answering benchmarks, including TimeQA, TempReason, and ComplexTR, demonstrate that IA-RAG achieves strong temporal retrieval and reasoning performance, particularly on complex compositional temporal reasoning tasks. Our code is released at https://github.com/xiaoAugenstern/LogicalRAG_TemporalQA.

</details>


### [189] [Automatic Labelling of Speech Translation Errors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06047)
*Dominik Macháček,Maike Züfle,Ondrej Klejch*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework, STEL, for labeling and evaluating errors in speech translation, highlighting the role of direct speech processing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the lack of a standardized methodology to gauge the confidence and quality of Speech Translation systems since errors can damage trust and have serious repercussions.

Method: They developed an annotation protocol and a dataset for evaluating ST systems and analyzed text-only (e.g., XCOMET) and multimodal systems' (e.g., Qwen2.5-Omni) performance in error labeling.

Result: The results reveal that existing systems achieve only about half the precision of human annotators and that direct speech processing is indispensable for the task.

Conclusion: Text-only and speech-processing systems are complementary in identifying specific types of errors, and advancing direct speech processing is necessary to improve STEL performance.

Abstract: Errors in speech translations reduce trustworthiness of Speech Translation (ST) systems and can have serious consequences. Yet currently there is no established methodology for evaluating confidence and quality estimation of speech translations. To initiate progress in this direction, we propose Speech Translation Error Labelling (STEL). We create an annotation protocol, a small authentic end-to-end evaluation dataset, and we analyse how existing text-only and speech-processing systems perform the STEL task. Our results show that text-only XCOMET and multimodal LLM Qwen2.5-Omni are able to perform the STEL task in roughly half the precision of humans. We also find that direct speech processing is necessary for the STEL task, and that the current text-only and speech-processing systems are complementary in labelling translation-only vs. speech-processing errors in ST.

</details>


### [190] [Multi-task Learning is Not Enough: Representational Entanglement in Dual-output Second Language Speech Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06065)
*Seung Hwan Cho,Young-Min Kim*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper investigates multi-task learning (MTL) for transcribing pronunciations and meanings in second-language (L2) speech recognition, finding benefits for meaning recognition but surface degradation, particularly in English.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore whether shared representations in multi-task learning (MTL) enhance accuracy for dual tasks of surface transcription and meaning recognition in second-language (L2) speech recognition.

Method: Analyzed MTL performance across Korean and English in automatic speech recognition (ASR). Used encoder and decoder analyses to link performance patterns to task representation entanglement.

Result: MTL improved meaning recognition but degraded surface transcription, especially in English where higher surface-meaning divergence was observed. Korean maintained distinct task representations, unlike English.

Conclusion: The study recommends redesigning MTL frameworks to reduce encoder-level entanglement, addressing surface transcription degradation in L2 speech recognition.

Abstract: Second-language (L2) speech recognition often requires transcriptions of pronunciations and intended meanings. Multi-task learning (MTL) is a natural approach because it assumes that shared representations benefit both outputs. However, this paper shows that this assumption does not hold across Korean and English. MTL improves meaning but degrades surface transcription, especially in English, where the degradation scales with surface-meaning divergence measured by Levenshtein edit distance.Encoder analysis links these patterns to encoder-level entanglement, with Korean preserving distinct task representations while English produces nearly identical ones. Cross-task decoder analysis shows that the meaning dual-output decoder adapts with a unique representation, while the surface dual-output decoder remains constrained by the encoder. These findings motivate the design of MTL frameworks that mitigate encoder-level entanglement to reduce surface degradation in dual-output L2 automatic speech recognition.

</details>


### [191] [SkillComposer: Learning to Evolve Agent Skills for Specification and Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06079)
*Qi Zhang,Zhaopeng Feng,Xiaonan Shi,Xiaomeng Hu,Chu Liu,Pengjun Xie,Xiaobin Wang,Jieping Ye,Bryan Hooi,Haobo Wang,Junbo Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SkillComposer introduces a framework to enhance agent skills by systematically creating, improving, and merging strategies, outperforming existing methods while offering cross-domain generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need to address the trade-off between task-specific skill transferability and the insufficiency of abstract skills due to the lack of explicit mechanisms for specifying and generalizing skills.

Method: SkillComposer decomposes skill construction into three operations—create, improve, merge—trained via systematic rejection sampling, and supports offline, online, and hybrid deployment modes.

Result: SkillComposer achieves superior performance on various benchmarks, improving performance of smaller models and generalizing skills to unseen domains and tasks.

Conclusion: This framework advances skill-augmented inference by addressing skill transferability and quality dimensions through self-evolution and composition, setting a standard for meta-ability development.

Abstract: Agent skills, which consist of reusable strategies that guide agent reasoning and action, have shown strong potential for improving model capability at inference time. However, current skill construction methods treat the problem as one-shot extraction, overlooking a fundamental tension: a skill tailored to the specific task fails to transfer, while the abstracted skill often provides insufficient guidance. We attribute this fragility to the absence of explicit mechanisms for skill specification and generalization. To address this gap, we introduce SkillComposer, a framework that decomposes skill construction into three learnable operations: create, improve, and merge. Trained via systematic rejection sampling recipe, SkillComposer enables language models to self-evolve skills at inference time and supports three deployment modes: offline for building generalized libraries, online for task-specific refinement, and hybrid for combining both. Comprehensive experiments on $τ^2$-Bench, LiveCodeBench v6, and AppWorld show that SkillComposer consistently outperforms baselines. Our SkillComposer-4B improves a 27B executor by up to +4.5 on agent tasks and +3.4 on code tasks, while generalizing across domains and task types unseen during training. Analysis reveals that merge and improve address orthogonal quality dimensions and that skill composition is a transferable meta-ability, providing a practical recipe for skill-augmented inference.

</details>


### [192] [LatentSkill: From In-Context Textual Skills to In-Weight Latent Skills for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06087)
*Aofan Yu,Chenyu Zhou,Tianyi Xu,Zihan Guo,Rong Shan,Zhihui Fu,Jun Wang,Weiwen Liu,Yong Yu,Weinan Zhang,Jianghao Lin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LatentSkill proposes converting textual skills into LoRA adapters using a hypernetwork, removing the need for per-step tokens while significantly reducing overhead and increasing performance in multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aimed to address the inefficiencies and vulnerabilities of current agent systems that repeatedly inject textual skills into prompts, leading to high context overhead and exposure of skill content.

Method: The framework, LatentSkill, uses a pretrained hypernetwork to encode skills as plug-and-play LoRA adapters stored in weight space rather than context space.

Result: LatentSkill improves task performance: a 21.4 and 13.4 point increase in ALFWorld success rates (seen/unseen splits), with a 64.1% reduction in prefill tokens, and a 3.0 point increase in Search-QA exact match with 72.2% lower skill-token overhead.

Conclusion: LatentSkill demonstrates that weight-space skills are modular, efficient, and secure, offering structured control, composability, and scalability for enhancing LLM agents.

Abstract: Agent systems increasingly use textual skills to encode reusable task procedures, but injecting these skills into the prompt at every step incurs substantial context overhead and exposes skill content as plaintext. We present LatentSkill, a framework that converts textual skills into plug-and-play LoRA adapters through a pretrained hypernetwork. LatentSkill stores skill knowledge in weight space rather than context space, removing per-step skill tokens while preserving modular loading, scaling, and composition. On ALFWorld and Search-QA, LatentSkill outperforms the corresponding in-context skill baseline while using substantially fewer prefill tokens: it improves ALFWorld success by 21.4 and 13.4 points on the seen and unseen splits with 64.1% fewer prefill tokens, and improves Search-QA exact match by 3.0 points with 72.2% lower skill-token overhead. Further analysis shows that generated skill LoRAs form a structured semantic geometry, can be precisely controlled via the LoRA scaling coefficient, and can be composed through parameter-space arithmetic when skill components are aligned. These findings suggest that weight-space skills provide an efficient, modular, and less exposed substrate for extending LLM agents.

</details>


### [193] [CHALIS: A Challenge Dataset for Language Identification in Difficult Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06088)
*Michal Tichý,Jindřich Libovický*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces CHALIS, a dataset for testing language identification systems on challenging scenarios involving mutually intelligible languages and orthographic noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve language identification systems by addressing common difficulties such as distinguishing between closely related languages and handling orthographic variations or noise.

Method: They created CHALIS, a dataset with challenging examples: shared sentences between closely related languages and alterations involving transliteration, diacritic removal, homoglyph attacks, and internet slang.

Result: Existing language identification systems struggle notably on both mutual intelligibility cases and orthographically altered inputs, particularly for low-resource languages.

Conclusion: CHALIS can serve as a robust benchmark for evaluating and improving language identification systems in challenging contexts.

Abstract: We present CHALIS (Challenging Language Identification Samples), a new benchmark dataset explicitly designed to address difficult cases in language identification: cousin languages and orthographic noise. Our dataset has two parts: First, we collected sentences shared across mutually intelligible language pairs (Czech/Slovak, Spanish/Catalan, Portuguese/Galician, Danish/Norwegian). The second part tests for orthography noise: we transliterate text across multiple scripts, remove diacritics, simulate homoglyph attacks, and use Internet slang. We evaluate four widely used language identification systems on CHALIS and demonstrate that all struggle substantially in these scenarios, especially on lower-resource languages within cousin pairs and on transliterated input. The resource is publicly available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/michal-tichy/CHALIS.

</details>


### [194] [IR3DE: A Linear Router for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06098)
*Eros Fanì,Oğuzhan Ersoy*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces IR3DE, a ridge regression-based inference router for efficiently and dynamically selecting domain-expert LLMs for prompts, achieving strong performance efficiently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in existing inference routers that either focus on generic tasks or require extensive training for domain-specific routing, creating a need for a more efficient and dynamic router.

Method: Proposed IR3DE, a ridge regression-based inference router, for selecting appropriate LLMs per prompt. It is linear, fast, cost-efficient, and supports dynamic modification of domain experts without retraining.

Result: IR3DE achieved comparable performance to baselines in Causal Language Modeling tasks and outperformed them in reasoning tasks with normalized performance of 98.4%.

Conclusion: IR3DE provides an effective and flexible approach to inference routing, supporting dynamic updates and maintaining strong task performance with minimal cost and complexity.

Abstract: Foundational Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate proficiency on a wide range of general tasks, and achieve remarkable results on various specialized tasks via domain-expert LLMs. With the ever-growing list of available LLMs, inference routers are being proposed to select the most appropriate LLM for each prompt. However, existing routing methods either optimize cost across weak-to-strong generalist LLMs or require substantial training to support domain-expertise routing. In this paper, we propose IR3DE, a Ridge Regression-based Router for Domain Experts that provides cheap and fast routing decisions for each prompt. We evaluate IR3DE in two Causal Language Modeling (CLM) settings where the tasks are next-token prediction for all domains, and one reasoning setting where each domain has its own distinct reasoning task. Despite being a linear router, IR3DE achieves performance comparable to the other baselines in both CLM settings, and surpassing them in the reasoning setting, with a normalized performance of 98.4%. Moreover, IR3DE enables the addition or removal of new domain experts without requiring the router to be retrained from scratch, allowing a dynamic set of LLMs to be served with minimal disruption to the router itself. Our code is available at: github.com/gensyn-ai/IR3DE.

</details>


### [195] [Harnessing Structural Context for Entity Alignment Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06109)
*Xingyu Chen,Yuanning Cui,Zequn Sun,Wei Hu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces ContextEA, an enhanced entity alignment (EA) framework that strengthens the usage of structural context for improved performance in identifying equivalent entities across knowledge graphs (KGs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The recent EA foundation model shows promise in transferring alignment knowledge to unseen KG pairs but suffers from underutilizing structural context during encoding and candidate ranking, limiting its effectiveness.

Method: ContextEA employs a cross-KG interaction encoder for early relation-aware propagation and a structural calibration decoder to refine alignment scores using various structural evidence (entity, neighborhood, relation, and anchor-aware).

Result: ContextEA consistently outperforms strong baselines in 29 EA datasets across OpenEA, SRPRS, and DBP benchmarks. It surpasses finetuned baselines even in its pretrained state, demonstrating excellent transferability to unseen KGs.

Conclusion: Leveraging structural context more explicitly in EA models significantly enhances their effectiveness and transferability, as demonstrated by ContextEA's superior performance.

Abstract: Entity alignment (EA) aims to identify equivalent entities across heterogeneous knowledge graphs (KGs) and is a key component of knowledge fusion and cross-KG reasoning. The recent EA foundation model demonstrates that alignment knowledge, once pretrained, can be directly applied to diverse previously unseen KG pairs. However, it still underuses structural context in two places: cross-KG interaction is weak during encoding, and final candidate ranking still relies too heavily on coarse similarity. We address these limitations with ContextEA, an enhanced encoder-decoder framework for transferable EA. On the encoder side, we introduce a cross-KG interaction encoder that unifies the two KGs with anchor bridges and performs earlier relation-aware cross-graph propagation. On the decoder side, we introduce a structural calibration decoder that calibrates alignment scores with entity-level, neighborhood-level, relation-level, and anchor-aware structural evidence. This design strengthens both structural context construction and structural context exploitation while remaining lightweight. Experiments on 29 EA datasets in OpenEA, SRPRS, and DBP show consistent gains over strong transferable baselines. Notably, the pretrained ContextEA already surpasses the finetuned baselines on all three benchmark groups, demonstrating substantially stronger transfer to unseen KGs. These results suggest that explicitly harnessing structural context is an effective direction for improving EA foundation models.

</details>


### [196] [Ouvia: A User-centered Framework for Measuring Usability of Speech Translation in Real-World Communication Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06177)
*Giuseppe Attanasio,Beatrice Savoldi,Daniel Chechelnitsky,Matteo Negri,Marine Carpuat,Maarten Sap,André F. T. Martins*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents an evaluation framework, Ouvia, to assess the real-world usability of speech translation systems, revealing significant usability limitations and demographic disparities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the inadequacy of current evaluations of speech translation systems, which focus on decontextualized testbeds and overlook user-centered communication needs.

Method: The researchers developed a web app and conducted over 1,750 interactions using four speech translation systems in healthcare and everyday scenarios. Participants included diverse demographics, such as different English dialects and genders.

Result: The study found only half of the interactions were rated as usable, with noticeable usability gaps across demographic groups. QA-based evaluation was found to better predict usability compared to standard quality metrics.

Conclusion: The findings highlight the need for user-centered evaluation frameworks like Ouvia, which focus on real-world usability and address the diverse needs of end users rather than relying on traditional quality scores.

Abstract: Speech translation (ST) is increasingly adopted in user applications, yet its evaluation largely focuses on decontextualized testbeds and holistic quality, rather than end users' communication needs. We introduce Ouvia, an evaluation framework for measuring user-perceived usability of speech translation outputs in real-world settings. Ouvia focuses on one-to-one communication: an English speaker needs to convey a request to a Portuguese speaker, and the message is automatically translated. Through a custom web app and multi-phase study design, we collect more than 1,750 such interactions in healthcare and everyday situations, mediated by four ST systems, involving speakers from three English dialects and two genders. We find that modern ST serves people only to a limited extent -- only around half of interactions are rated as usable -- with significant gaps in reported usability across demographic groups. Moreover, among quality metrics, we find that QA-based evaluation is a substantially stronger predictor of real-world usability than standard approaches. Together, these findings stress the importance of situated, user-centered evaluation frameworks that go beyond holistic quality scores and attend to who the technology serves -- and how well.

</details>


### [197] [The Tell-Tale Norm: $\ell_2$ Magnitude as a Signal for Reasoning Dynamics in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06188)
*Jinyang Zhang,Hongxin Ding,Yue Fang,Weibin Liao,Muyang Ye,Junfeng Zhao,Yasha Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper identifies the l2 norm of hidden states in Large Language Models (LLMs) as a measure of reasoning intensity and introduces methods for enhancing reasoning performance using this intrinsic signal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore and develop a principled, intrinsic signal for understanding and controlling the layer-wise reasoning dynamics in LLMs, which remains an underexplored aspect.

Method: The authors use Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to probe reasoning activation patterns in LLMs, establish a theoretical connection between l2 norms and reasoning intensity, and validate their findings with empirical and causal analysis. They propose three test-time scaling techniques leveraging l2 norms for improving reasoning.

Result: The experiments demonstrate that l2 norm-based techniques significantly enhance reasoning performance across LLM architectures and benchmarks without requiring additional training or data.

Conclusion: This study emphasizes the role of l2 norm as a reliable indicator of reasoning intensity in LLMs and proposes practical, effective methods for interpreting and controlling latent reasoning dynamics, making this approach a valuable contribution to LLM research.

Abstract: Recent work has sought to understand Large Language Models (LLMs) reasoning, yet a principled, model-intrinsic signal that captures its layer-wise reasoning dynamics remains underexplored. We bridge this gap by demonstrating that the l2 norm of hidden states serves as an endogenous signal of the model's reasoning intensity. Using Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) as a diagnostic probe, we observe that LLMs' internal reasoning is marked by a sharp increase in reasoning feature activations concentrated in late layers. Motivated by this pattern, we establish a formal link between reasoning intensity and the model's latent geometry and theoretically prove that the l2 norm of hidden states bounds the activation strength of SAE reasoning features. Empirical correlation analysis and causal interventions further validate the l2 norm as a faithful indicator, where heightened norms consistently correspond to critical reasoning steps. We then introduce three test-time scaling techniques guided by l2 norms: (i) Adaptive Layer-wise Reasoning Recursion, (ii) Endogenous Reasoning State Steering, and (iii) l2-guided Response Selection, which requires no additional training or data and is compatible with advanced inference engines. Experiments across model architectures and benchmarks show that l2-norm-based techniques significantly improve reasoning performance, offering a principled yet simple lens to perceive and control LLM latent reasoning dynamics. Our code is available at https://github.com/zjy1298/The-Tell-Tale-Norm.

</details>


### [198] [Improving Answer Extraction in Context-based Question Answering Systems Using LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06197)
*Hafez Abdelghaffar,Ahmed Alansary,Ali Hamdi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces a fine-tuned Roberta-based QA system utilizing the Stanford SQuAD1.1 dataset to address challenges in precise answer generation, achieving strong experimental results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome the limitations in current QA systems, particularly their struggle with accurate and contextually relevant answers for complex or ambiguous queries.

Method: The authors fine-tuned a pre-trained LLM (Roberta-base model) on the SQuAD1.1 dataset to enhance contextual understanding and precision in answer generation.

Result: The fine-tuned model achieved high performance metrics: ROUGE-L (86.84%), BLEU (28.24%), and BERTScore (95.38%), showcasing significant improvements in QA accuracy.

Conclusion: Fine-tuning large language models, like Roberta-base, on benchmark datasets significantly enhances the reliability and precision of AI-driven QA systems, addressing existing limitations.

Abstract: Question answering (QA) systems have achieved notable progress with the advent of large language models (LLMs). However, they still face challenges in accurately extracting and generating precise answers from given contexts, particularly when dealing with complex or ambiguous queries. Existing approaches often struggle with contextual understanding, answer consistency, and generalization across diverse domains. In this work, we propose a question answering system based on large language models, where the input consists of a textual context and a corresponding question, and the output is a concise and accurate answer. The motivation behind this research lies in addressing the limitations of current QA systems, particularly their tendency to produce irrelevant or imprecise responses despite having access to the correct context. Our methodology involves fine-tuning a pre-trained LLM on a benchmark QA dataset to improve its contextual comprehension and answer extraction capabilities. Specifically, we utilize the Stanford Question Answering Dataset (SQuAD1.1), which provides high-quality context-question-answer triplets for supervised training and evaluation. Experimental results show that the fine-tuned Roberta-base model achieves the highest performance, attaining a ROUGE-L score of 86.84%, a BLEU score of 28.24%, and a BERTScore of 95.38%. These results indicate strong accuracy and answer relevance, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed approach for context-based question answering tasks. Furthermore, the findings confirm that targeted fine-tuning substantially improves the reliability and precision of QA systems.

</details>


### [199] [Dense Contexts Are Hard Contexts: Lexical Density Limits Effective Context in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06203)
*Giovanni Dettori,Matteo Boffa,Danilo Giordano,Idilio Drago,Marco Mellia*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies lexical density as a key factor affecting the long-context performance of large language models (LLMs), alongside input length and relevant information position.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore how lexical density (rate of introducing distinct information within a context) affects the effective context window of LLMs and address its overlooked role in performance degradation.

Method: Three 'find-the-needle' benchmarks with identical lengths (~12k tokens) but varying lexical densities were used to evaluate the performance of LLMs (ranging from 9B to 685B open-weight models). Density was controlled and tested systematically while maintaining other variables constant.

Result: High lexical density leads to a sharp decline in LLM performance, with models that initially performed well in sparse contexts failing to maintain accuracy in dense ones. Performance was restored to some extent by lowering lexical density.

Conclusion: Effective context capacity of LLMs is significantly influenced by lexical density, which could have practical implications for designing systems to handle information-rich inputs.

Abstract: Input length and the position of relevant information are widely cited as the primary causes of degraded LLM long-context performance. Here, we study lexical density -- the rate at which a context introduces distinct information -- as a third, largely overlooked factor that systematically reduces the effective context window of LLMs. We quantify the impact of lexical density on open-weight LLMs (9B-685B) using three "find-the-needle" style benchmarks with identical length (~12k tokens) and controlled needle position, but increasing density of information. We observe a sharp performance collapse in higher-density benchmarks: models that are near-perfect in sparse contexts drop below 60% retrieval score on denser ones. To rule out task-type confounds, we vary and control the density within each benchmark while keeping all other properties unchanged. Reducing density generally restores performance, especially in the high-density regimes where degradation appears. These results show that effective context capacity is a function of lexical density, with direct implications for real-world LLM systems operating on compact, information-rich inputs.

</details>


### [200] [FiLM-Based Speaker Conditioning of a SpeechLLM for Pathological Speech Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06211)
*Fernando López,Santosh Kesiraju,Jordi Luque*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a speaker conditioning method using FiLM for ASR to adapt to pathological speech, proving its competitiveness with baselines while preserving general ASR capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of improving pathological speech recognition in ASR systems while preserving performance on standard speech.

Method: Utilized Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) to condition a frozen ASR encoder with speaker-specific x-vectors for individual adaptation without modifying the base model weights.

Result: Speaker-conditioned ASR showed competitive performance compared to standard adaptation methods and retained capabilities for general speech recognition.

Conclusion: The proposed approach offers an effective and efficient pathway to adapt ASR for pathological speech without compromising overall model performance.

Abstract: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) has advanced remarkably for standard speech; however, pathological speech from neurological conditions remains a significant challenge. We investigate speaker conditioning via Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM), injecting x-vector-derived information into each transformer layer of a frozen ASR encoder to adapt internal representations to individual pathological speakers without modifying base model weights. We benchmark this for the ASR task against standard and parameter-efficient fine-tuning baselines, complemented by post-processing, on Spanish and English pathological speech. Additionally, we evaluate if the adapted model preserves the ability to answer speech-related questions. Results show that speaker-conditioned ASR is competitive with established adaptation strategies while retaining performance on non-conditioned speech.

</details>


### [201] [Benchmarking Open-Source Layout Detection Models for Data Snapshot Extraction from Institutional Documents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06242)
*AJ Carl P. Dy,Aivin V. Solatorio*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper discusses extracting semantically meaningful visual artifacts (figures and tables) from institutional documents, introducing a benchmark, dataset, and evaluation framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Institutional documents hold valuable analytical data in visual formats, which aren't effectively extracted by current generic document layout analysis tools.

Method: The authors benchmarked multiple open-source layout detection models on their proposed dataset, evaluating their ability to detect and spatially extract semantically meaningful visual content.

Result: Current layout detection models struggle to handle operational institutional documents and often misidentify analytical content, fragment composite artifacts, and fail to extract context for interpretation.

Conclusion: A gap exists between existing document layout analysis and the need for effective operational visual data extraction. The provided dataset and resources aim to foster progress in this area.

Abstract: Institutional documents contain substantial amounts of operational and analytical information embedded within figures and tables. Current approaches for extracting visual content from documents are largely built around generic document layout analysis, where figures and tables are treated as uniformly relevant document objects rather than semantically meaningful analytical artifacts. In this work, we introduce a benchmark dataset and evaluation framework for \textit{data snapshot extraction}, the task of identifying and localizing semantically meaningful visual artifacts within institutional documents. The benchmark spans humanitarian reports, World Bank policy research working papers, and project appraisal documents, and includes annotations for figures and tables that contain reusable analytical information. Using this dataset, we benchmarked multiple open-source layout detection models and evaluated both detection performance and spatial extraction quality. Our results show that current models struggle to generalize to operational institutional documents despite strong performance on conventional academic benchmarks. Common failure modes include confusion between analytical and non-analytical content, fragmentation of composite analytical artifacts, and incomplete extraction of contextual information required for interpretation. These findings highlight a persistent gap between generic document layout analysis and operationally useful data snapshot extraction. We release the source PDFs, annotation dataset, metadata, and source code to support future research in operational document intelligence. The dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/ai4data/data-snapshot and the source code is available at https://github.com/worldbank/ai4data/tree/main/experimental/data-snapshot.

</details>


### [202] [From Self to Other: Evaluating Demographic Perspective-Taking in LLM Hate Speech Annotation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06266)
*Paloma Piot,Javier Parapar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores persona-conditioned large language models for simulating diverse demographic perspectives in hate speech detection, finding that no model fully captures inter-group disagreement, in-group sensitivity, and vicarious prediction, but Llama 3.1 performs the best overall.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Demographic groups perceive hate speech content differently, but collecting enough annotations across groups is costly. The paper seeks scalable alternatives using persona-conditioned large language models.

Method: Evaluated large language models on how well they represent human disagreement in social judgment by analyzing inter-group disagreement, in-group sensitivity, and vicarious prediction.

Result: No model consistently succeeds across all three aspects, though vicarious prompting with Llama 3.1 shows the highest alignment with human disagreement patterns across groups.

Conclusion: Llama 3.1 demonstrates potential for providing scalable and reliable annotations aligned with human judgment, but limitations remain in fully capturing human disagreement.

Abstract: Hate speech detection is inherently subjective: people from different demographic groups perceive the same content very differently. Collecting enough annotations from multiple demographic groups is costly and difficult to scale. Persona-conditioned Large Language Models (models prompted to adopt a specific demographic identity) have been proposed as a way to simulate diverse perspectives at scale. But do they actually reflect how different groups disagree? We evaluate three aspects of human social judgement: (i) whether personas from different groups disagree in human-like ways (inter-group disagreement), (ii) whether they become more sensitive when content targets their own identity (in-group sensitivity), and (iii) whether they can accurately predict how another group would react (vicarious prediction). Our results show that no model consistently captures all three dimensions, and performance is highly model-dependent and does not emerge reliably from minimal identity prompts alone. However, vicarious prompting with Llama 3.1 yields the highest cross-group agreement in most demographic axes and provides the closest overall approximation to human disagreement patterns, indicating that this configuration may provide a more reliable setting for automatic annotation aligned with human judgements.

</details>


### [203] [Many Circuits, One Mechanism: Input Variation and Evaluation Granularity in Circuit Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06267)
*Alireza Bayat Makou,Jingcheng Niu,Subhabrata Dutta,Iryna Gurevych*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Structural differences in discovered circuits do not necessarily imply functional differences, termed "phantom specialization," emphasizing the importance of edge-level evaluations and cross-condition transfer testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Circuits discovered in models with structural variations are often interpreted as evidence of different internal mechanisms, but this assumption has not been rigorously tested for validity.

Method: Experiments were conducted involving Literal Sequence Copying under varying input frequency conditions and across multiple Pythia models by extracting and comparing 75 circuits, using edge transferability and causal interventions for validation.

Result: Findings showed that structurally different circuits can perform identical computations. Core circuits recovered 99% of performance, and internal representations were interchangeable across different conditions.

Conclusion: Structural differences alone cannot serve as reliable evidence of distinct mechanisms in model circuits, necessitating edge-level evaluations and cross-condition testing for true functional understanding.

Abstract: Circuit discovery methods identify subgraphs that explain specific model behaviors, and structural differences between discovered circuits are commonly interpreted as evidence of distinct mechanisms. We test this assumption by varying input statistics while holding the task fixed, and show that the resulting structural differences exhibit apparent specialization but do not correspond to functional differences, a pattern we term phantom specialization. Using Literal Sequence Copying across four token-frequency bands plus a control condition in five Pythia models (70M-1.4B), we extract 75 circuits and find that structurally distinct circuits implement the same computation: band-specific edges transfer broadly across bands, a core shared across most bands recovers at least 99% of circuit performance, and causal interchange interventions confirm that internal representations are interchangeable across frequency bands. Repeated extractions within the same frequency band further suggest that discovery algorithms sample from an equivalence class of valid subgraphs rather than recovering a unique mechanism. Standard evaluation practice obscures this pattern: source-level evaluation inflates apparent faithfulness, while edge-level evaluation reveals the many-to-one mapping from structure to function. Our results show that structural differences between circuits are not sufficient evidence for distinct mechanisms, and that exposing this requires edge-level evaluation and cross-condition transfer tests.

</details>


### [204] [FOXGLOVE: Understanding Goal-Oriented and Anchored Writing Feedback from Experts and LLMs on Argumentative Essays](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06271)
*Yijun Liu,Yifan Song,John Gallagher,Sarah Sterman,Tal August*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study examines how feedback from large language models compares to feedback from expert instructors on student essays, focusing on key dimensions like goal-orientation and specificity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the gap in understanding the similarities and differences between LLM-generated feedback and expert writing feedback, particularly given the growing use of LLMs in education.

Method: FOXGLOVE, a dataset of over 2,300 feedback comments, was created, combining instructor-written and LLM-generated feedback. Expert quality ratings were used to evaluate these sources.

Result: Instructors and LLMs distribute feedback similarly across goals and essay sections, but differ in sentence specificity and question usage. LLM feedback often rated higher in quality, attributed largely to its length.

Conclusion: FOXGLOVE helps identify overlaps and differences between human and LLM feedback, highlighting strengths and shortcomings of LLM-generated comments in writing assessment.

Abstract: While large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate writing feedback, there remains no systematic comparison of LLM and expert feedback on the dimensions that writing research identifies as central to revision: goal-orientation, anchoring to specific sentences, and prioritization. We introduce FOXGLOVE, a dataset of 696 feedback comments written by trained writing instructors on 69 twelfth-grade argumentative essays, paired with 1,644 comments generated from four frontier LLMs under a shared protocol, totaling 2,340 comments. We provide expert quality ratings on a subset of both instructor and LLM comments. We find that instructors and LLMs distribute feedback similarly across goals and essay positions, yet instructors and models diverge on the specific sentences on which to provide feedback. Additionally, we find that models tend to write more complex feedback and use fewer questions than instructors. LLM feedback also receives higher ratings on most dimensions of quality, as rated by instructors, but much of this advantage appears to be attributable to lengthier comments. FOXGLOVE enables systematic comparison of where human and LLM feedback align, diverge, and differ.

</details>


### [205] [LLMs Can Leak Training Data But Do They Want To? A Propensity-Aware Evaluation of Memorization in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06286)
*Gianluca Barmina,Peter Schneider-Kamp,Lukas Galke Poech*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework, PropMe, to evaluate how often large language models reproduce training data in realistic settings compared to adversarial scenarios, showing that such models rarely memorize in non-adversarial use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to understand and differentiate between scenarios where large language models are explicitly forced to reproduce training data versus how often they do so in routine, non-adversarial usage.
