AMC demonstrates the feasibility of performing principled RL-style optimization of black-box LLM agents. Code is available at https://github.com/layer6ai-labs/Agentic-Monte-Carlo

</details>


### [349] [Statistically Reliable LLM-Based Ranking Evaluation via Prediction-Powered Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05308)
*Abhishek Divekar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper extends Prediction-Powered Inference (PPI) to compute unbiased, bias-corrected ranking metrics using both human-labeled and LLM-judged datasets, achieving efficiency and accuracy improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of evaluating hierarchical ranking metrics efficiently and accurately, especially when annotations are limited and provided by both human and LLM judges.

Method: PRECISE combines a small human-labeled dataset with a larger LLM-judged dataset, leveraging PPI to ensure unbiased estimates. It reduces computational complexity for metrics like Precision@K by optimizing the output space from O(2^|C|) to O(2^K).

Result: On the ESCI benchmark, the method reduces the error margin of metrics like Precision@4 by 21%, using a mix of human and LLM annotations, and successfully identifies optimal system variants in real-world production with notable impacts on sales.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that leveraging both human and LLM annotations under the PRECISE framework effectively improves the accuracy, efficiency, and real-world applicability of ranking metric evaluations.

Abstract: With PRECISE, we extended Prediction-Powered Inference to produce bias-corrected estimates of ranking evaluation metrics by combining a small human-labeled set with a large LLM-judged set. PPI is provably unbiased regardless of the LLM judge's error profile. We make it applicable to hierarchical metrics like Precision@K, where annotations are per-document but the metric is per-query, by reducing the output-space computation from O(2^|C|) to O(2^K). On the ESCI benchmark, augmenting 30 human annotations with Claude 3 Sonnet judgments reduces the standard error of Precision@4 estimates from 4.45 to 3.50 (a 21% relative reduction). In a production system, our framework correctly identified the best of three system variants from 100 human labels and 2 hours of domain-expert annotation; A/B testing confirmed this ranking with +407 bps in daily sales.

</details>


### [350] [Multimarginal flow matching with optimal transport potentials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05327)
*Raghav Kansal,David Crair,Nghia Nguyen,Scott Pope,Bradley Parry*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces OTP-FM, an algorithm for modeling dynamic flows between distributions with intermediate observations, leveraging connections to dynamic optimal transport.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of modeling temporal evolution in dynamical systems using sequential distributions and constraints from intermediate observations.

Method: OTP-FM incorporates dynamic optimal transport potentials into the flow matching framework, allowing simulation-free, efficient learning of multimarginal flows.

Result: The proposed framework shows state-of-the-art performance in domains like single-cell RNA sequencing, oceanography, and meteorology.

Conclusion: OTP-FM provides a flexible and computationally efficient approach to multimarginal flow modeling, contributing significantly to applications in scientific domains.

Abstract: Flow matching (FM) has emerged as a powerful framework for learning dynamic transport maps between two empirical distributions. However, less explored is the setting with intermediate observed marginals that can help constrain the flows between the endpoints. This "multimarginal" regime is central to modeling temporal evolution in dynamical systems in many scientific domains that can sample sequential distributions. We tackle this problem with a novel approach that leverages the connection between FM and dynamic optimal transport (OT), softly steering the flow towards the intermediate marginals through potential terms in the dynamic OT action. By extending the conditional FM learning target to incorporate these potentials, we derive an efficient, simulation-free algorithm for multimarginal FM that offers considerable flexibility in the spatiotemporal dynamics of the learned flows. We demonstrate state-of-the-art performance and training efficiency of OT-potential FM (OTP-FM) on diverse single-cell RNA sequencing, oceanographic, and meteorological datasets. Our code is available at https://github.com/Bexorg-Inc/OTP-FM.

</details>


### [351] [A prism hierarchy of learning regimes in large linear autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05335)
*Eugene Golikov,Yaroslav Gusev,Dmitry Yarotsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper develops a systematic framework to analyze extreme learning regimes for large weight-tied linear autoencoders, associating them with a triangular prism structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish a unified and systematic understanding of all qualitatively different extreme learning regimes for certain machine learning models, facilitating theoretical tractability.

Method: The authors analyzed large weight-tied linear autoencoders and associated their extreme training regimes with the faces of a triangular prism, identifying five primary regimes. They derived explicit mathematical expressions for the loss evolutions in most regimes.

Result: Explicit expressions for the train and population limiting loss evolutions under gradient flow in specific regimes (large-data, small-data, mean-field, and narrow-latent) were derived and experimentally validated.

Conclusion: The study offers a geometric and theoretical framework for analyzing extreme learning regimes in large weight-tied linear autoencoders, showing good alignment between theoretical and experimental results in the considered cases.

Abstract: Theoretical studies of machine learning models commonly consider different limiting regimes in which the learning dynamics of gradient descent becomes theoretically tractable. It is, however, desirable to have a systematically obtained picture of all qualitatively different extreme learning regimes for a particular type of models. In this paper we propose such a picture for large weight-tied linear autoencoders characterized by input and latent dimensions, initialization magnitude, and training set size. This model is nonlinear in the weights and its gradient flow does not have a general theoretical solution. We show that at the level of the formal loss-expansion hierarchy, its extreme regimes are naturally associated with faces of a triangular prism. In particular, there are five basic extreme regimes associated with the 2-faces of the prism: (1) large-data, (2) small-data, (3) mean-field, (4) narrow-latent, and (5) free. For regimes (1,2,3,4), we derive explicit expressions for both train and population limiting loss evolutions under gradient flow, obtaining very good agreement with experimental results.

</details>


### [352] [Mamba-Assisted Non-Markovian Closure for Reduced-Order Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05371)
*Zhi-Feng Wei,Saad Qadeer,Panos Stinis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel framework, Mamba-Assisted Closure (MAC), to improve reduced-order modeling for dynamical systems by combining sequence modeling techniques inspired by the Mori--Zwanzig formalism. The method outperforms competing approaches in accuracy and stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The goal is to address the challenge of non-Markovian closure terms, which hinder effective reduced-order modeling of high-dimensional dynamical systems, by leveraging sequence modeling and memory-dependent dynamics.

Method: The authors integrate a Mamba-based sequence model that predicts closure effects from resolved trajectories with numerical integrators using dual representations for training and inference. The model is trained in a sequence-to-sequence manner and implemented for autoregressive inference.

Result: The MAC model significantly outperforms existing methods, such as the Markovian reduced-order model, GRU-based sequence model, and Wilks method, in terms of predictive accuracy and long-term simulation stability on benchmarks like the viscous Burgers' equation and Lorenz '96 system.

Conclusion: The MAC framework effectively addresses the limitations of current reduced-order modeling techniques, providing a robust tool for accurate and stable simulation of dynamical systems with high-dimensional complexity.

Abstract: Reduced-order modeling of high-dimensional dynamical systems is often hindered by the non-Markovian closure term that represents the effect of unresolved variables on the resolved dynamics. Inspired by the Mori--Zwanzig formalism, in which the closure takes the form of a memory functional of the resolved trajectory, we recast closure modeling as a sequence modeling problem and propose the Mamba-Assisted Closure (MAC) framework: a Mamba-based sequence model, trained to predict the closure from the resolved trajectory, is coupled with the reduced-order governing equations through a numerical integrator to advance the resolved variables in time. A key feature of the framework is its exploitation of the dual representation of state-space models -- the model is trained in a sequence-to-sequence fashion via the convolutional form, and deployed for step-by-step autoregressive rollout via the recurrent form, yielding both efficient long-trajectory training and constant per-step inference cost. On the viscous Burgers' equation and the chaotic two-scale Lorenz '96 system, the MAC model substantially outperforms the Markovian reduced-order model, the GRU-based sequence model, and the Wilks method in predictive accuracy and long-time rollout stability.

</details>


### [353] [PJ-RoPE: A Fourier-Jet-Affine Position Space for Relative Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05345)
*Yaobo Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces PJ-RoPE, a unified framework for relative positional encoding methods such as RoPE, Jordan-RoPE, and ALiBi, allowing task-specific adaptation of positional spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To consolidate and unify different methods of relative positional encoding for better understanding and task-specific adaptation.

Method: Introduces PJ-RoPE, a Fourier-Jet-Affine representation combining scalar PJ-bias kernels with rotary feature transforms, supported by adaptive diagnostics and controlled probes.

Result: Experiments demonstrate the framework's ability to adaptively select positional spaces for different tasks, showing task-specific benefits and trade-offs.

Conclusion: PJ-RoPE effectively unifies existing encoding methods and provides insights into their task-dependent behaviors and limitations.

Abstract: We unify RoPE's Fourier phase, Jordan-RoPE's finite jets, and ALiBi's affine recency into a single learnable relative-position space, and study which regions of this space are selected by different tasks. PJ-RoPE is a Fourier-Jet-Affine formulation for relative attention, with an optional Poincare-type reading as the affine completion of a homogeneous Fourier-jet positional representation. Algebraically, the same primitives form a finite constant-coefficient difference module: simple roots of the lag-shift operator give Fourier/RoPE characters, repeated nonzero roots give Jordan/Fourier jets, and the repeated unit root gives ALiBi-like affine recency.
  The framework separates scalar PJ-bias kernels from exact PJ-rotary feature transforms, introduces adaptive sector diagnostics, and uses LC/rapidity coordinates to stabilize high-order jets. Controlled probes verify sector containment and selection; small language runs expose an affine/recency boundary; music-token streams provide the clearest case where LC/affine variants remain strong while carrying measurable high-order corrections; and LC diagnostics show a scale-stability gain coupled to phase-resolution loss.

</details>


### [354] [GOTabPFN: From Feature Ordering to Compact Tokenization for Tabular Foundation Models on High-Dimensional Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05441)
*Al Zadid Sultan Bin Habib,Md Younus Ahamed,Prashnna Kumar Gyawali,Gianfranco Doretto,Donald A. Adjeroh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents a method to improve small tabular foundation models for high-dimensional, low-sample size (HDLSS) prediction without retraining large models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of making small tabular foundation models effective for HDLSS prediction scenarios while avoiding the retraining of large backbones.

Method: The authors introduce GO-LR, a graph-guided ordering approach, and propose GOTabPFN, which employs GO-LR and a Neuro-Inspired Subunit Compression unit to create compact representations for prediction.

Result: GOTabPFN enhances the stability and accuracy of predictions while adhering to strict token constraints in various tabular benchmarks.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively improves the practicality of TabPFN-style predictions in challenging HDLSS scenarios by ensuring stable and accurate performance.

Abstract: We investigate how to make small tabular foundation models effective for High-Dimensional, Low-Sample Size (HDLSS) tabular prediction without retraining large backbones. We introduce Graph-guided Ordering with Local Refinement (GO-LR), show its equivalence to weighted Minimum Linear Arrangement, and interpret the practical solver as a TSP-path-style surrogate. We propose GOTabPFN,which builds on GO-LR, and a Neuro-Inspired Subunit Compression (NSC) unit to pool locally adjacent ordered features into meta-features, yielding a compact representation that makes TabPFN-style prediction practical in HDLSS regimes. Across tabular benchmarks, GOTabPFN improves stability and accuracy under tight token budgets.

</details>


### [355] [Evidence-Guided Neural Architecture Selection under Uncertainty for Subject-Specific Blood Glucose Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05373)
*Md Azharul Islam,Dwyer Deighan,Tarunraj Singha,Danial Faghihi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces EVIDENT, a Bayesian-based framework for selecting reliable neural architectures tailored for time-series forecasting, using individualized blood glucose prediction as a case study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Time-series forecasting faces challenges under limited, noisy, and heterogeneous data. Standard methods for architecture design and validation fall short in ensuring accuracy and reliability, especially in critical applications.

Method: EVIDENT combines Bayesian training, evidence-based model ranking, and task-specific validation. It identifies the lowest-capacity neural architecture meeting predefined validation criteria, using temporal convolutional networks (TCNs) for demonstration.

Result: EVIDENT successfully rejected under- and over-parameterized TCN models, identified well-generalizing architectures, and produced reliable predictions for unseen diabetes patients. It also enabled effective ensemble predictions and outperformed a random-search baseline.

Conclusion: EVIDENT provides a robust neural architecture selection strategy for reliable time-series forecasting, particularly in high-stakes and data-constrained scenarios.

Abstract: Reliable neural architecture selection is an open challenge in time-series forecasting under limited, noisy, and heterogeneous data, where standard heuristic architecture design and validation approaches fail to ensure accurate and reliable prediction and generalization. We propose EVIDENT (EVidence-based IDEntification of Neural archiTectures), a framework for architecture selection that integrates Bayesian training, evidence-based ranking, and task-specific validation under uncertainty. The framework explores the candidate architecture pool and identifies the lowest-capacity model that satisfies a prescribed validation criterion. We demonstrate this method using temporal convolutional networks (TCNs) for individualized blood glucose forecasting in type 1 diabetes patients. The results show that EVIDENT systematically rejects both under- and over-parameterized TCN architectures on population-level diabetes data, while identifying models that generalize reliably to unseen patients. When multiple architectures are competitive, the framework further supports plausibility-weighted ensemble predictions that enhance predictive performance. Compared with a random-search baseline, EVIDENT identified smaller architectures with more consistent forecasting performance on unseen patients. These findings establish EVIDENT as a strategy to neural architecture discovery, enabling reliable model selection for high-consequence forecasting in data-limited and heterogeneous settings.

</details>


### [356] [SHALA-LLM: Smartly Handling Ambiguous Labels in Aligning LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05376)
*Jingyao Wu,Ashley Wang,Keane Ong,Paul Pu Liang,Rosalind Picard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces SHALA-LLM, a reinforcement learning method for large language models to better handle label ambiguity by leveraging annotator distributions, resulting in improved label agreement and classification performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges arising from label ambiguity and annotator disagreements in tasks like natural language inference and emotion recognition.

Method: The proposed SHALA-LLM framework uses reinforcement learning to incorporate annotator distributions, dynamically prioritizing ambiguous samples during optimization.

Result: SHALA-LLM significantly reduces disagreement with annotator label distributions, e.g., reducing Jensen-Shannon Distance by 62.1%, and enhances classification metrics like F1 by up to 16.7%.

Conclusion: SHALA-LLM effectively utilizes label ambiguity as informative input rather than noise, leading to improved alignment with annotators and better task performance.

Abstract: Many human-centered tasks, including natural language inference (NLI) and emotion recognition (ER), have multiple plausible interpretations, leading to label ambiguity and challenging disagreements across human annotators. As LLMs are increasingly deployed in real-world settings, faithfully modeling such ambiguity is essential to identify contested inputs, preserve variability in ambiguous cases, and capture the full distribution of human judgments. Yet, existing LLM alignment approaches have predominantly assumed a single correct label, excluding annotator disagreement during optimization. Instead of treating this ambiguity as noise, we show how to treat it as information that improves model behavior through a new algorithm called SMARTLY HANDLING AMBIGUOUS LABELS IN ALIGNING LLMS (SHALA-LLM). This reinforcement learning framework provides a new way for LLMs to learn directly from annotator distributions while dynamically prioritizing highly ambiguous samples during optimization. Experiments on ambiguity-sensitive NLI and ER benchmarks, including ChaosNLI, GoEmotions, and MSP-Podcast, demonstrate that SHALA-LLM improves agreement with annotator label distributions, e.g. on ChaosNLI, it reduces Jensen-Shannon Distance by up to 62.1%. At the same time, SHALA-LLM improves F1 by up to 16.7%, showing that modeling annotator disagreement can also strengthen classification performance.

</details>


### [357] [Mitigating the Curse of Dimensionality in Uniform Convergence of Deep Neural Networks via Smooth Activations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05599)
*Yizhe Ding,Runze Li,Jia Liu,Lingzhou Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes the uniform convergence behavior of deep neural network (DNN) estimators, and proposes smoothly activated DNNs to address the limitations of ReLU networks in mitigating the curse of dimensionality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of standard ReLU networks, which underperform in uniform convergence and suffer from the curse of dimensionality in some applications, especially when uniform reliability is crucial.

Method: The authors derive novel pseudo-dimension bounds, non-asymptotic approximation guarantees, and Hölder-norm bounds for smooth DNNs. They also establish uniform convergence rates for these networks under multiple regression settings.

Result: Smooth DNNs are shown to mitigate the curse of dimensionality in uniform convergence by leveraging the hierarchical composition structure of target functions. This is supported by theoretical analysis, simulations, and a real-world application.

Conclusion: Smooth DNNs provide a robust alternative to ReLU networks for statistical learning tasks requiring uniform guarantees and demonstrate superior performance in uniform convergence analysis.

Abstract: This paper establishes a theoretical framework for the uniform convergence of smoothly activated deep neural network (DNN) estimators. While standard ReLU networks achieve minimax-optimal rates in the $L^2(P)$ norm for various nonparametric regression tasks, we establish a theoretical lower bound demonstrating that least-squares ReLU estimators can suffer from the curse of dimensionality in their uniform convergence behavior. Motivated by the need for reliable uniform guarantees in downstream tasks requiring worst-case reliability, we address this limitation by analyzing smoothly activated DNNs (smooth DNNs), encompassing both feedforward and residual structures. We establish novel pseudo-dimension bounds, non-asymptotic approximation guarantees, and Hölder-norm bounds for the approximators of these models. Leveraging these results, we derive non-asymptotic uniform convergence rates for smooth DNN estimators across multiple statistical contexts, including Huber, least-squares, quantile, and logistic regression. We prove that smooth DNNs can mitigate the {curse of dimensionality} in uniform convergence by adaptively exploiting the low-dimensional hierarchical composition structure of the target function. Supported by both simulation studies and a real-world application, our results position smooth DNNs as a theoretically grounded and practically viable alternative to ReLU networks for statistical learning tasks requiring uniform guarantees.

</details>


### [358] [Pattern Selectivity is Not Task-Causal Structure: A Cross-Architecture Mechanistic Study of Composed-Task Circuits in 1B-Class Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05378)
*Yongzhong Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study explores a unified approach for identifying attention-head circuits in language models and verifies its findings with causal ablations and null checks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether a standardized method can consistently identify task-specific attention-head circuits across different 1B-class language models and task types.

Method: A single recipe involving task-pattern selectivity and causal ablation against null conditions was applied across multiple tasks and model families. Results were categorized using a taxonomy of screen outcomes.

Result: Attention-head circuits for the same tasks varied widely across models, demonstrating diverse implementations. A falsifiable hypothesis is proposed regarding the positional substrate functioning in MoE models.

Conclusion: The methodology can port across pipelines, but identified circuits differ by model. Findings are highlighted by task-specific markers and causal screenings.

Abstract: We test whether a single screen-and-ablate recipe -- identify attention-head circuits by task-pattern selectivity, then verify by causal ablation against a matched-random null -- produces consistent mechanistic claims across model families. The recipe ports across pipelines; the specific circuit it identifies does not. Across four composed tasks (indirect-object identification, greater-than, successor sequences, variable binding) and three 1B-class language models from distinct training pipelines (Pythia 1B / Pile / dense; OLMo 1B / DCLM / dense; OLMoE 1B-7B / DCLM / mixture-of-experts), we run a unified protocol with the matched-random null sampled across ten seeds per cell. The resulting 12 (task, model) cells contain no two that share the same primary causal screen at comparable effect size: the same task, with the same behavioral capability, is implemented through different attention-pattern types across models.
  We introduce a five-category screen-outcome taxonomy -- primary cause, secondary cause, correlate, interferer, null -- with quantitative thresholds, and show that all five outcomes appear in the panel. We propose a falsifiable hypothesis: the MoE model in our panel builds composed-task circuits on top of a foundational previous-token positional substrate (the prev-token-circuit ablation is the strongest causal screen on 3 of 4 tasks for OLMoE 1B-7B), with the IOI exception consistent with IOI being a final-position name-copying task whose structure directly probes a different pattern. The hypothesis comes with explicit predictions for other MoE language models.
  We frame the methodology honestly: the spectral participation-ratio signal from the companion methodology paper is a general indicator of specialized computation; what makes a finding task-specific is the task-pattern screen plus a per-model causal verification.

</details>


### [359] [Zero-Copy Semantic Contagion: An In-Memory Streaming Architecture for Evolving Attention Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05733)
*Kabir Murjani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a financial time-series forecasting architecture leveraging a dynamic graph-based model to account for cross-company propagation effects in market events, showing superior precision over baseline methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the limitation of traditional per-ticker financial forecasting models that fail to account for cross-company effects in financial time series, such as events affecting suppliers or partners.

Method: The authors introduce a heterogeneous Rust-Python streaming architecture that uses continuous-time graphs derived from news text for cross-company attention modeling. A Neural Hawkes Process with LSTM components handles propagation and computational pruning minimizes costs.

Result: The system achieves ~13 ms processing latency per news record, delivering 1.70x precision at high-return thresholds and 3.36x over a same-sector baseline on FNSPID data, with graph topology being essential for effectiveness.

Conclusion: The proposed system effectively integrates dynamic cross-company interactions for financial forecasting and significantly outperforms traditional approaches by leveraging graph-based attention propagation.

Abstract: Per-ticker forecasting models dominate financial time-series work yet remain blind to cross-company propagation: a foundry disruption in Taiwan does not register in a single-asset model until Apple's own price has already moved. To address this limitation, we introduce a heterogeneous Rust-Python streaming architecture that maps cross-company attention as a continuous-time graph driven directly from text. We show that on the ingestion side, a zero-copy Rust edge parses news records in $\sim$100 ns and scans the target equity universe in $\sim$1.2 $μ$s. On the inference end, a multivariate Neural Hawkes Process featuring per-node continuous-time LSTM states and a bilinear latent projection propagates directed excitation, while an adaptive pruning rule bounds the computational cost of dynamic neighborhood updates. Combining these stages, we demonstrate an end-to-end processing latency of $\sim$13 ms per incoming news record on a single commodity CPU. Evaluated on a one-month temporal holdout of the FNSPID corpus (638 articles across 47 tickers), the system delivers a $1.70\times$ precision lift over random at the 90th-percentile next-day return threshold, and $3.36\times$ over a same-sector baseline. Crucially, removing the graph topology collapses precision to zero, confirming that the dynamic attention network is the sole driver of cross-company signal in this architecture.

</details>


### [360] [Generalized TV--$\ell_p$ Structured Priors for Bayesian $T_1$ Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05381)
*Disi Lin,Martin Berggren,Tommy Löfstedt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a structured spatial prior based on the TV function combined with ℓp norms, applied to Bayesian $T_1$ mapping. It demonstrates superior uncertainty quantification and reliable parameter mapping compared to several alternatives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The primary motivation is to improve spatial coherence and uncertainty quantification in $T_1$ mapping by leveraging structured priors that enforce smoothness and consistency.

Method: The method involves constructing a TV–ℓp prior, proving its validity, and incorporating it within a Bayesian regression framework with posterior inference via the No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS). It is evaluated using synthetic and real in-vivo $T_1$ datasets against other Bayesian and maximum-likelihood techniques.

Result: Experiments show that the TV–ℓp prior leads to reduced uncertainty, lower variance, and reduced bias, enhancing the reliability of $T_1$ mapping.

Conclusion: The proposed TV–ℓp prior improves spatial coherence and reduces uncertainty in $T_1$ mapping, offering a more robust framework for parameter estimation in medical imaging applications.

Abstract: We propose an extended family of structured spatial priors that incorporates the total variation (TV) function with $\ell_p$ norms. The prior is proven to be proper and incorporated into a Bayesian regression framework to enable uncertainty quantification in $T_1$ mapping, with posterior inference performed using the No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS). This TV--$\ell_p$ construction is proven to constitute a well-defined family of prior distributions, and it naturally enforces spatial consistency and smooth variations in the estimated parameter maps. The method was evaluated in comparison to maximum-likelihood estimation and several Bayesian alternative priors based on the uniform, Gamma, and bounded TV priors. The evaluation includes experiments on synthetic brain and cardiac $T_1$ mapping datasets, as well as a real in-vivo breast $T_1$ mapping dataset. The results show that the TV--$\ell_p$ prior yields more concentrated posterior densities, indicating reduced uncertainty. It also consistently achieves lower variance and smaller (negative) bias, leading to more reliable estimates. Overall, embedding a TV-based structured penalty along with $\ell_p$ norms in a prior in a Bayesian model improves spatial coherence in $T_1$ maps and enhances uncertainty quantification, offering a robust approach for $T_1$ mapping with uncertainties.

</details>


### [361] [Trust, but Don't Verify: Epistemic Blind Spots in LLM Source Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05403)
*Rohan N. Pradhan,Steve Goley*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Language models fail to evaluate the quality of data during multi-source synthesis, relying on surface style over numeric validity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper investigates whether language models can accurately evaluate the quality of evidence or merely rely on surface presentation during evidence synthesis.

Method: The authors test five language models from three different architectures using behavioral experiments and mechanistic analyses, including causal tracing and probing for signals in the models.

Result: Models were able to detect fabricated statistics in isolation but not during multi-source synthesis. The mechanism relies on stylistic cues rather than numeric validity.

Conclusion: The study highlights a gap between the models' detection capabilities in isolation and their deployment during decision-making, calling for better methods to align epistemic reasoning in AI.

Abstract: Language models increasingly act as epistemic proxies, synthesizing evidence from multiple sources to inform decisions. Whether they evaluate the quality of that evidence, or merely aggregate it based on surface presentation, remains poorly understood. We show that models possess the capability to detect fabricated statistics (correct identification rates of 0.76-1.00 for methodology in isolation) but do not recruit this capability during multi-source synthesis, producing similar numeric estimates whether the statistics are fabricated or valid. Specifically, source influence is governed by a methodology-register gate that responds to the distributional register of analytical text but not to numeric validity: for example, statistically impossible confidence intervals receive the same weight as valid ones. The behavioral dissociation replicates across five models from three families (Claude, Qwen, OLMo) and three professional domains. Mechanistic analyses, including causal tracing, linear probes, and component-level attribution, converge on the same account: the model encodes and causally uses a methodology-register representation that transfers across domains (probe AUC 0.83-0.92), while numeric-validity signals, decodable in isolation, are suppressed to chance during multi-source synthesis. Prompting-based mitigations, even an oracle checklist naming the exact statistical checks, produce blanket skepticism rather than selective discernment, and the post-training pipelines we examine reinforce the stylistic shortcut without building numeric verification. Unlike sycophancy, which tracks user preference, this failure tracks whether a source presents as analytically credible, not whether its claims are internally consistent. We term this epistemic alignment: like preference and safety alignment, the question is not capability but deployment.

</details>


### [362] [Causal Longitudinal Prior-Fitted Networks for Counterfactual Outcome Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05797)
*Amirhossein Zare,Amirhessam Zare,Herlock Rahimi,Reza Salarikia,Mohammad Kashkooli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Causal Longitudinal Prior-Fitted Networks (CausalLongPFN), a pretrained, frozen model for longitudinal causal prediction using synthetic data, which performs competitively without requiring domain-specific training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of predicting outcomes under future treatments in longitudinal settings when faced with time-varying confounding, complex patient dynamics, and limited data.

Method: Pretrain CausalLongPFN on synthetic data generated from prior temporal structural causal models, exposing it to various causal complexities, and use it as a frozen predictor without further domain-specific training.

Result: CausalLongPFN is effective on benchmarks involving cancer, HIV, and warfarin with ground-truth counterfactuals, and performs strongly on factual ICU trajectory predictions (MIMIC-III).

Conclusion: Broad synthetic causal pretraining offers a competitive and practical solution for longitudinal causal prediction where domain-specific training is costly or infeasible.

Abstract: Longitudinal treatment decisions require predicting potential outcomes under future treatment sequences in the presence of time-varying confounding, heterogeneous patient dynamics, and limited domain-specific data. Existing longitudinal causal estimators typically train a new model for each cohort or simulator. We introduce Causal Longitudinal Prior-Fitted Networks (CausalLongPFN), a prior-fitted in-context predictor for longitudinal causal prediction. The model is pretrained entirely on synthetic episodes sampled from a broad prior over temporal structural causal models, exposing it to treatment-confounder feedback, latent heterogeneity, nonlinear state evolution, delayed effects, and cumulative treatment responses. At test time, CausalLongPFN is frozen: it conditions on support trajectories, a query history, and a proposed future treatment sequence, and returns a predictive distribution over future outcomes without gradient updates or propensity-model fitting. Multi-step predictions are obtained by recursively applying the one-step predictor under the specified treatment sequence. We evaluate on branchable cancer, HIV, and warfarin benchmarks with ground-truth counterfactual labels, and on factual-only rolling-origin prediction in MIMIC-III ICU trajectories. CausalLongPFN is competitive with domain-trained longitudinal baselines on counterfactual benchmarks and performs strongly on factual MIMIC-III prediction, suggesting that broad synthetic causal pretraining can provide a useful frozen alternative when repeated domain-specific training is costly or impractical.

</details>


### [363] [CausalPOI: Spatio-Temporal Graph-Based Causal Modeling for Cold-Start POI Check-in Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05413)
*Zhaoqi Zhang,Miao Xie,Yi Li,Linyou Cai,Siqiang Luo,Gao Cong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces CausalPOI, a spatio-temporal graph-based framework for cold-start POI check-in forecasting, focusing on functional dependencies and causal effects in urban environments. It outperforms existing methods on real-world datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations in existing POI forecasting methods which rely on proximity-based graphs and correlation models that fail to capture functional dependencies and causal effects of urban interventions.

Method: Proposes a new framework, CausalPOI, which utilizes a Spatio-Temporal Functional Interaction Graph and constructs treatment/control graphs to simulate factual and counterfactual scenarios for better modeling of dynamic POI behaviors.

Result: Extensive experiments on SafeGraph real-world datasets show that CausalPOI significantly exceeds the performance of state-of-the-art baselines in tasks such as spatio-temporal forecasting, interaction modeling, and causal effect estimation.

Conclusion: CausalPOI provides a robust and interpretable solution to the cold-start POI check-in forecasting problem, paving the way for actionable insights in urban planning and intervention analysis.

Abstract: As urban environments continue to evolve rapidly, accurately modeling the dynamic behaviour of Points of Interest is essential for supporting data-driven urban planning and commercial decision-making. While recent advancements in spatio-temporal graph learning have improved POI forecasting, most methods rely on proximity-based graphs and correlation-driven modeling, which overlook the functional dependencies between POIs and fail to capture the causal effects of urban interventions. In this paper, we introduce a novel research problem -- cold-start POI check-in forecasting, which aims to predict the future check-in pattern of a newly introduced POI, by modeling its temporal evolution and functional interactions with nearby POIs in a structured urban spatial context. To address these challenges, we propose CausalPOI, a spatio-temporal graph-based causal representation learning framework. CausalPOI leverages Spatio-Temporal Functional Interaction Graph to model semantic and spatial relationships between POIs, and constructs structurally aligned treatment and control graphs to simulate factual and counterfactual scenarios. Extensive experiments on real-world SafeGraph datasets demonstrate that CausalPOI significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across the board, validating its effectiveness in spatio-temporal forecasting, semantic interaction modeling, and causal effect estimation, providing a more interpretable and actionable foundation for urban intervention analysis. Source code is available at Github.

</details>


### [364] [Tangram: Unlocking Non-Uniform KV Cache for Efficient Multi-turn LLM Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06302)
*Hyungmin Kim,Minsoo Kim,Hongseok Kim,Jungwook Choi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tangram improves multi-turn LLM serving efficiency by addressing challenges posed by non-uniform Key-Value cache compression, achieving up to 2.6x better throughput.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address inefficiencies in LLM serving systems caused by non-uniform KV cache compression, which leads to memory fragmentation, scheduling complexities, and reduced kernel utilization.

Method: Tangram employs three core techniques: Deterministic Budget Allocation, Head Group Page clustering, and Ahead-of-Time Load Balancing to optimize GPU memory utilization and eliminate performance bottlenecks.

Result: Tangram boosts throughput by up to 2.6x compared to existing solutions, achieving this without sacrificing model accuracy.

Conclusion: Tangram makes non-uniform KV caches practical for LLM serving by resolving systemic inefficiencies, leading to significant improvements in performance and resource utilization. The implementation is publicly accessible.

Abstract: Multi-turn Large Language Model (LLM) serving is critical for consistent user experiences, yet the linear growth of the Key-Value (KV) cache imposes significant pressure on GPU memory and bandwidth. Non-uniform KV compression effectively preserves more information by considering the individual importance of each KV cache. However, such KV cache heterogeneity introduces various systemic challenges - including memory fragmentation, scheduling complexities, and diminished kernel utilization - which collectively lead to significant inefficiencies in existing LLM serving systems.
  To overcome these challenges, we present Tangram, a novel serving system designed to make Non-uniform KV caches practical. Tangram addresses systemic inefficiencies through three core techniques: (1) Deterministic Budget Allocation assigns a static memory footprint to each head based on its intrinsic pattern, entirely eliminating dynamic scheduling overhead and prefill stalls; (2) Head Group Page clusters attention heads with similar retention demands and manages them with independent, vectorized page tables, thereby maximizing physical memory reclamation; and (3) Ahead-of-Time (AOT) Load Balancing leverages static budget profiles to ensure uniform GPU utilization without runtime overhead. Experimental results show that Tangram improves throughput by up to 2.6x compared to existing baselines, while fully preserving model accuracy. Our implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/aiha-lab/TANGRAM.

</details>


### [365] [Dead Directions: Geometric Singular Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05957)
*Tejas Pradeep Shirodkar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper establishes a connection between singular learning theory and information geometry by introducing the concept of "dead directions" and analyzes parameter spaces while addressing overparameterization challenges in neural networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unify and bridge the methodologies of singular learning theory (which resolves Bayesian invariants) and information geometry (which works in original coordinates but struggles with overparameterization).

Method: The study introduces the concept of a ‘dead direction’ — a unit vector tangent to the analytic singular set defined by KL divergence properties — and uses it to recover critical rates and geometric invariants in the original parameter coordinates. This approach is extended to deep neural networks through multi-layer factorizations and a quotient theorem for gradient flow, as well as designing an SGD-compatible optimizer (DDCAdam).

Result: The paper derives new tools for analyzing singular geometry in overparameterized settings, allows predictions specific to neural network architectures, and extracts key metrics like KL decay rates and Watanabe’s invariants from computational checkpoints.

Conclusion: The findings provide a parameter-coordinate framework for analyzing singular geometries and facilitate predictions and practical computations for modern machine learning models without needing posterior sampling.

Abstract: Singular learning theory and information geometry have studied the same parameter spaces in mostly separate vocabularies: the former computes Bayesian invariants in resolved coordinates, the latter works in original coordinates under a non-degeneracy assumption that overparameterised models routinely violate. We bridge them through one primitive, the dead direction: a unit vector along which the Fisher metric degenerates, equivalently a tangent to the analytic singular set with a definite KL order, set by how fast the KL divergence vanishes. The two readings name the same vector; our central move shows its KL order is recoverable as the decay rate of the directional Fisher curvature approaching the singularity, in original parameter coordinates and without a Hironaka resolution. A selection rule on smooth fibres translates this rate into Watanabe's single-direction contribution to the real log canonical threshold, and we extend the recovery to multi-component crossings, multiplicity $m$, the singular fluctuation $ν$ (universal in the KL order for 1D directions), prior-RLCT shifts, and tempered posteriors. We then lift this rate to a deep network: a multi-layer K-FAC factorisation writes each Fisher block as a product of activation- and gradient-side rates with a duality between them, instantiated at modern-network primitives (residual streams, layer normalisation, attention). A quotient theorem carries the rate to the gauge quotient $Θ/G$ under gradient flow on a $G$-invariant metric; SGD qualifies, standard Adam does not, and we construct a $G$-equivariant Adam-family preconditioner (DDCAdam) that does. The bridge yields a parameter-coordinate handle on singular geometry, closed-form per-architecture predictions, and a trajectory-rate readout of Watanabe's triple $(λ, m, ν)$ from one checkpoint's forward and backward passes, without posterior sampling.

</details>


### [366] [Selective-Advantage Entropy-Adaptive Horizon GRPO: Asymmetric Token-Level Discounting for Efficient Reinforcement Learning of Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05434)
*Chirag Chawla,Rohan Charudatt Salvi,Madhav S. Baidya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper expands on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for reasoning tasks by introducing adaptive discounting methods, achieving improved model stability, variance reduction, and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the symmetric treatment of token positions and sampled rollouts in GRPO, which doesn't adequately differentiate between successful and uncertain trajectories in reasoning tasks, leading to suboptimal efficiency and stability.

Method: Two extensions are proposed: (1) AH-GRPO, which applies a cumulative entropy-based discount to uncertain tokens, and (2) SA-AH-GRPO, which focuses this discounting on negative-advantage rollouts while preserving the gradients of successful solutions.

Result: SA-AH-GRPO significantly improves training stability, reducing variance by 3.6 times and achieving notable peak accuracies, such as 0.858 Pass@1 on a 3B reasoning model and an improvement to 0.686 Pass@1 on a 1.5B model, outperforming baselines.

Conclusion: The asymmetric discounting approach not only stabilizes training and prevents entropy collapse but also enhances performance by preserving gradients for correct solutions, offering a robust inductive bias for structured generation tasks in reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimisation (GRPO) has emerged as an effective reinforcement-learning algorithm for aligning language models on reasoning tasks, but it treats every token position and every sampled rollout symmetrically. We introduce two complementary extensions: (i) Adaptive-Horizon GRPO (AH-GRPO), which weights each token's policy gradient using a cumulative entropy-based discount that reduces the effective horizon when the model is uncertain, and (ii) Selective-Advantage AH-GRPO (SA-AH-GRPO), which applies this discounting only to negative-advantage rollouts, leaving positive-advantage, successful trajectories unattenuated. We evaluate standard GRPO with alpha = 0, AH-GRPO with alpha = 0.5, and SA-AH-GRPO with alpha = 0.5 on the GSM8K mathematical reasoning benchmark using both Qwen 2.5-1.5B-Instruct and Qwen 2.5-3B-Instruct fine-tuned with LoRA. On the 3B model, SA-AH-GRPO achieves Pass@1 = 0.858 at its peak at step 30 and maintains 0.846 at 180 steps, with training variance reduced to 0.0246, a 3.6 times reduction relative to GRPO while matching its peak accuracy. On the 1.5B model, SA-AH-GRPO achieves a peak Pass@1 of 0.686, improving over the zero-shot baseline of 0.637. Our analysis shows that asymmetric discounting preserves the full gradient signal on correct solutions, prevents entropy collapse, and substantially stabilises training, suggesting a principled inductive bias for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards on structured generation tasks.

</details>


### [367] [Adaptive state-action abstractions via rate-distortion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06123)
*Fernando E. Rosas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper explores a principle for dynamically refining task abstractions in reinforcement learning, focusing on balancing learning error and abstraction error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning lacks a general method for adjusting the granularity of task abstractions during learning, an issue addressed in this study.

Method: The principle is formalized using a value error decomposition into learning and abstraction errors, with soft state-action abstractions guided by rate-distortion principles to dynamically adjust resolution.

Result: Using this principle, the paper achieves near-optimal performance in various tabular environments, even under significant lossy compression of state and action spaces.

Conclusion: The proposed refinement principle effectively balances learning and abstraction errors, offering a structured approach to dynamically adjusting task abstraction granularity in reinforcement learning.

Abstract: When learning to walk, infants seem to address a coarse version of the problem first - stay upright, reach the caregiver - and refine it only when further practice at that resolution stops paying off. Reinforcement learning offers multiple techniques for building simple versions of complex tasks, but lacks general principles for how to dynamically adjust the granularity of these abstractions during learning. This paper proposes one such principle: refine the abstraction as soon as the learning error within it becomes comparable to the error induced by the abstraction itself. Here, we investigate one way of formalising this principle via a performance certificate that decomposes value error into two terms: a learning error bound captured by a Bellman residual, and an abstraction error bound given by a bisimulation metric. The resulting switching strategy is implemented by soft state-action abstractions built from rate-distortion principles, whose resolution along state and action axes can be continuously adjusted. We validate this construction in a range of tabular settings, showing that near-optimal performance can be achieved under substantial lossy compression of state and action information.

</details>


### [368] [DP-MacAdam: Differentially Private Mechanism with Adaptive Clipping and Adaptive Momentum](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05435)
*Naima Tasnim,Lalitha Sankar,Oliver Kosut*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces DP-MacAdam, an algorithm combining adaptive clipping and adaptive momentum using shared variance and mean estimates for privacy-focused machine learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations in differentially private algorithms like DP-SGD, which use fixed gradient clipping thresholds, and improve training acceleration by combining benefits of adaptive clipping and momentum updates.

Method: The DP-MacAdam algorithm utilizes shared empirical mean and variance estimates for both gradient clipping and adapting momentum in training, ensuring bias-free variance estimates.

Result: DP-MacAdam improves model utility compared to DP-SGD, AdaClip, and DP-Adam baselines, while eliminating the need for manual clipping threshold tuning.

Conclusion: DP-MacAdam offers a unified approach to adaptive clipping and momentum, enhancing training efficiency and accuracy while maintaining privacy guarantees.

Abstract: Differentially private stochastic gradient descent (DP-SGD) has become the standard framework for privacy-preserving machine learning, yet its reliance on a fixed gradient clipping threshold to limit sensitivity remains a significant practical limitation. Adaptive clipping algorithms such as AdaClip shift and scale the gradient prior to clipping and adding noise so that the clipped gradient yields a more informative descent direction. The shift and scaling parameters are selected adaptively based on the empirical mean and variance. However, in existing adaptive clipping algorithms, these empirical estimates have not been also used for momentum to accelerate training itself. On the other hand, DP-Adam is an algorithm that exploits Adam-like momentum updates based on the gradient mean and variance to accelerate training, but does not exploit these estimates for adaptive clipping. In this work, we propose Differentially Private Mechanism with Adaptive Clipping and Adaptive Momentum (DP-MacAdam), a novel algorithm that combines these two approaches so as to use the same mean and variance estimates for both clipping and momentum. We perform an analysis showing that DP-MacAdam estimates the gradient variances in a bias-free manner. In addition, we empirically evaluate the privacy and accuracy of DP-MacAdam, demonstrating that it achieves improved model utility compared to DP-SGD, AdaClip, and DP-Adam baselines, without requiring manual tuning of the clipping threshold.

</details>


### [369] [PAC-Bayesian Adversarially Robust Generalization for Message Passing Graph Neural Networks: A Sensitivity Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06293)
*Ziling Liang,Xinping Yi,Qingsong Wen,Shi Jin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper focuses on improving the understanding of robust generalization in graph neural networks (GNNs) by developing tighter PAC-Bayesian generalization bounds with a sensitivity-aware framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges associated with robust generalization in adversarial settings for GNNs, where existing analyses rely on isotropic Gaussian posteriors and hidden-width-dependent complexity terms, which limit tightness and effectiveness.

Method: The paper quantifies parameter sensitivity via output Jacobians in message passing GNNs and uses anisotropic Gaussian posteriors with Jacobian-aligned sensitivity matrices to refine KL divergence bounds and reduce hidden-width dependence.

Result: The proposed method produces tighter robust generalization guarantees for MPGNNs by reducing complexity dependence on hidden-width terms and focusing instead on the number of classes, improving adversarial robustness.

Conclusion: This work advances PAC-Bayesian-based robust generalization understanding for GNNs, offering insights to design models with improved adversarial robustness by leveraging sensitivity-aware analyses and optimized anisotropic Gaussian posteriors.

Abstract: Whilst the vulnerability of graph neural networks (GNNs) to adversarial attacks poses a critical threat to graph representation learning, the understanding of the robust generalization behavior remains a fundamental challenge in the adversarial setting. Recently, PAC-Bayesian margin-based generalization analysis substantially advances this line of research by providing a flexible and data-dependent analytical framework. However, existing robust analyses often rely on isotropic Gaussian posteriors and control weight perturbations in the full parameter space, which limits the ability to capture heterogeneous parameter sensitivity yet hinges on hidden-width-dependent complexity terms, resulting in not-tight-enough generalization bounds. In this paper, we extend a recently proposed sensitivity-aware PAC-Bayesian framework from deep neural networks to message passing GNNs (MPGNNs) and derive a tighter robust generalization bound in the adversarial setting. Specifically, we first quantify how sensitive the perturbations across different parameter blocks are to the network outputs by deriving the output Jacobians with respect to the weight parameters. Exploiting the fact that these Jacobian matrices have rank at most $K$ in $K$-class graph classification, we then construct Jacobian-aligned sensitivity matrices and use anisotropic Gaussian posteriors with optimized covariances to upper bound the KL divergence in a tight way. Notably, by refining the spectral-norm dependence on the learned weights and reducing the leading dimension factor from hidden-width-dependent terms to the number of classes $K$, our analysis yields much tighter robust generalization guarantees for MPGNNs, thereby guiding their designs to enhance adversarial robustness.

</details>


### [370] [Sharp First-Order Lower Bounds for Higher-Order Smooth Nonconvex Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05438)
*Dongruo Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study establishes matching lower bounds for the first-order oracle complexity in smooth nonconvex optimization under higher-order smoothness assumptions, confirming the optimal rates for specific cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve the gap between accelerated upper bounds and missing lower bounds in higher-order smooth nonconvex optimization, where the optimal complexity rates were previously unclear despite their significant implications.

Method: The authors construct a block-chain mechanism that enforces blockwise oracle revelation and maintains smoothness properties to create hard instances for proving dimension-free lower bounds, demonstrating tight bounds for cases with Lipschitz Hessians and third derivatives.

Result: Matching lower bounds for higher-order smooth regimes were established, particularly proving Ω(ε^{-7/4}) for the Hessian-Lipschitz case and Ω(ε^{-5/3}) for the regime of Lipschitz third derivatives.

Conclusion: The study confirms that the accelerated upper bounds in first-order optimization under higher-order smoothness are indeed tight, filling a critical gap in the theory of deterministic first-order methods.

Abstract: We study the deterministic first-order oracle complexity of finding \(ε\)-stationary points in smooth nonconvex optimization when the objective satisfies higher-order smoothness assumptions. While the classical \(ε^{-2}\) rate is optimal under only Lipschitz gradients, higher-order smoothness leads to accelerated first-order upper bounds, most notably the \(ε^{-7/4}\) rate under Lipschitz Hessians and the \(ε^{-5/3}\) rate under Lipschitz third derivatives. The matching lower bounds, however, have remained open. We resolve this gap by proving a new dimension-free first-order lower bound for higher-order smooth nonconvex functions, valid for every finite smoothness order. In particular, our construction gives a matching \(Ω(ε^{-7/4})\) lower bound in the Hessian-Lipschitz case and a matching \(Ω(ε^{-5/3})\) lower bound in the third-order-smooth regime. The hard instance is based on a \emph{block-chain} mechanism that enforces blockwise oracle revelation while preserving the smoothness structure needed for the scalar hard instance. The lower-bound construction was discovered with the assistance of ChatGPT 5.5 Pro and subsequently verified by the authors.

</details>


### [371] [Efficient Mean Curvature Computation on High-Dimensional Data Manifolds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06329)
*Alexandre L. M. Levada*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces an optimized method to estimate local mean curvature in high-dimensional datasets, reducing computation costs significantly from $O(m^4)$ to $O(k^2 m + k m p^2)$, enabling practical scalability for machine learning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The current $O(m^4)$ cost of estimating local mean curvature using methods like the MCBP makes it intractable for datasets with many features, creating a need for a more efficient approach.

Method: The paper introduces an algebraic identity to eliminate the high-cost matrix $H$, achieving $O(m^2)$ cost, and replaces the full eigendecomposition process with a truncated SVD for better scalability. It also analytically approximates null-space eigenvector contributions.

Result: The proposed method achieves 50-300x speedup compared to the original implementation with negligible loss in accuracy, as confirmed by experiments on real-world datasets.

Conclusion: The optimized approach makes local mean curvature estimation scalable and efficient, enabling it to be a practical geometric feature for various machine learning tasks.

Abstract: Estimating local mean curvature at each point of a high-dimensional dataset is a key ingredient of geometry-aware machine learning algorithms, such as the Mean Curvature Boundary Points (MCBP) method. The naive implementation of this computation, based on a local shape operator approximated from k-nearest neighbor patches, involves an explicit construction of a matrix $H$ whose trace form yields an $O(m^4)$ cost per point, rendering the approach intractable for datasets with more than a few dozen features. This paper introduces two complementary contributions that together reduce this cost by several orders of magnitude. The first contribution is an exact algebraic identity. This identity, derived from the orthogonality of the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix and the cyclicity of the trace operator, eliminates $H$ entirely and reduces the per-point cost to $O(m^2)$ after the eigendecomposition. The second contribution addresses the remaining $O(m^3)$ bottleneck of the full eigendecomposition. Since the local covariance matrix has rank at most $k-1 \ll m$, we replace it with a truncated SVD of the $k \times m$ centered data matrix, an $O(k^2 m)$ operation, and derive an analytical approximation for the contribution of the null-space eigenvectors based on the expected value of their outer product under the Haar measure. The resulting estimator has total cost $O(k^2 m + k m p^2)$, where $p = k-1$. Experiments on real-world datasets confirm speedups of 50 to 300 times relative to the original implementation, with negligible loss when the fast estimator is used to replace the original version. By providing a scalable and data-driven estimate of local curvature, the proposed method establishes curvature as a practical geometric feature for a broad range of machine learning tasks, from classical to modern deep learning pipelines.

</details>


### [372] [Towards Unified and Data-Efficient Prognostics and Health Management with Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05481)
*Raffael Theiler,Lev Telyatnikov,Leandro Von Krannichfeldt,Olga Fink*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework to apply Tabular Foundation Models to industrial time series tasks like prognostics and diagnostics, demonstrating superior performance and data efficiency compared to traditional sequence models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges in industrial PHM data, such as fragmentation, partial observations, and poor labeling, and to make better use of foundation models for heterogeneous time-series problems.

Method: The authors propose converting raw unit-level time-series data into tabular formats and utilizing Tabular Foundation Models with in-context learning for PHM tasks. They compare these models with sequence-based models using a standardized evaluation protocol.

Result: Experimental results indicate that Tabular Foundation Models outperform sequence transformers and gradient-boosted trees on prognostics and diagnostics tasks, especially in low-data scenarios, demonstrating efficiency and robustness.

Conclusion: Tabular Foundation Models are highly effective and practical for industrial PHM tasks, offering a general and flexible solution for fragmented and under-labeled industrial time-series data.

Abstract: Data-driven Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) uses time-varying condition-monitoring data to diagnose system states and estimate remaining useful life in engineered assets. These tasks are central to maintenance planning, but industrial PHM data are often fragmented, partially observed, and poorly labeled, which hinders supervised learning. Foundation models offer a route toward reusable predictive systems, yet most time-series foundation models are designed for forecasting and assume long, coherent, regularly sampled sequences. To address this gap, we propose a framework for applying Tabular Foundation Models to industrial time series using in-context learning, and we evaluate them on a variety of PHM tasks. By converting raw unit-level signals into tabular rows, we show that these models perform well across multiple tasks - including prognostics, and diagnostics - and are highly data efficient. We compare them directly with sequence models, transformer baselines, and gradient-boosted trees under a common evaluation protocol. The results indicate that tabular foundation models achieve the best average ranks across prognostic and diagnostic tasks. Our findings further show that PFN-based models are competitive in low-data regimes, that temporal context can be preserved in the tabular representation, and that performance depends on representative context construction under subsampling. These results demonstrate that tabular foundation models provide a practical and general interface for heterogeneous PHM problems.

</details>


### [373] [Learned Subspace Compression for Communication-Efficient Pipeline Parallelism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05484)
*Paul Janson,Edouard Oyallon,Eugene Belilovsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MAPL introduces a flexible, learning-based approach for compressing inter-stage activations in pipeline-parallel model training, addressing challenges like maintaining orthogonality and performance degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need to address activation communication bottlenecks in pipeline parallelism, especially on low-bandwidth networks, while avoiding performance degradation and complex optimization constraints in existing methods.

Method: MAPL formulates inter-stage compression as a learnable orthogonal projection, employing Stiefel manifold constraints and manifold-constrained steepest descent. It also introduces factorized anchor embeddings and residual vector quantization with a streaming codebook for efficient compression.

Result: The paper demonstrates that MAPL achieves high compression for LLaMA models (150M to 1B parameters) with negligible performance degradation, exhibiting superior performance-compression trade-offs compared to Subspace Networks.

Conclusion: MAPL offers an efficient and scalable solution for inter-stage activation compression in pipeline parallelism, surpassing existing methods in both performance and practicality.

Abstract: Pipeline parallelism enables training of large language models that exceed single-device memory, yet inter-stage activation communication becomes the dominant bottleneck when trained on low-bandwidth networks. Recent work in this area has proposed using fixed orthogonal projections to compress activations. However, this still results in a significant performance degradation and requires a number of non-standard adaptations to constrain the optimization. A natural alternative is to learn a low rank projection for each pipeline stage, however maintaining the necessary orthogonality of these projectors during training remains a challenge. We present Manifold Aware Projection Learning (MAPL), a method that treats inter-stage compression as a learnable orthogonal projection under explicit Stiefel manifold (orthogonal matrices) constraints. Rather than prescribing a fixed global subspace, MAPL lets each pipeline stage discover and continuously adapt its own task-optimal compression subspace via manifold-constrained steepest descent. To recover token-specific signals at stage boundaries, we introduce per-stage factorized anchor embeddings that allow for full-rank activation reconstruction with negligible communication overhead. We further show that we can incorporate residual vector quantization after projection with a streaming codebook synchronization protocol that amortizes dictionary communication. Across LLaMA models from 150M to 1B parameters we show that MAPL can be easily applied to the existing pipeline and can achieve high compression with neglibile performance degradation with a drastically improved tradeoffs in performance vs. compression compared to Subspace Networks.

</details>


### [374] [End-to-End Subgraph Detection with GraphDETR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06364)
*Dexiong Chen,Till Hendrik Schulz,Karsten Borgwardt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraphDETR is a deep learning framework for subgraph detection that extends traditional methods to handle approximate matching using transformer-based architecture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Subgraph detection is a cornerstone problem in various scientific fields, but traditional combinatorial approaches are constrained by the complexity of subgraph isomorphism, limiting their application to small-scale datasets.

Method: GraphDETR employs a graph neural network for encoding the target graph and uses a transformer decoder with learnable query vectors for joint pattern detection in one forward pass. It utilizes bipartite matching for end-to-end training and adapts subgraph detection into a set prediction task.

Result: The model effectively detects diverse pattern types, such as molecular structures, cycles, cliques, and fuzzy patterns, in large graphs. It achieved an average precision of 91.2% in molecular functional group detection using the ChEMBL dataset.

Conclusion: GraphDETR extends the scope of subgraph detection beyond exact matches to include approximate matches, showing strong performance and scalability for large graphs and complex patterns.

Abstract: Subgraph detection seeks to identify whether and where instances of query patterns occur within a larger graph. This problem is fundamental across scientific domains and is closely related to subgraph isomorphism, which is NP-complete, limiting combinatorial approaches to small patterns or moderately sized graphs. We introduce GraphDETR, a deep learning framework that formulates subgraph detection as a set prediction problem, analogous to DETR in object detection. GraphDETR encodes the target graph with a graph neural network, and employs a fixed set of learnable query vectors, decoded via a transformer decoder, to predict all pattern occurrences jointly in a single forward pass. This is enabled by training the model end-to-end with bipartite matching. Unlike traditional combinatorial methods that only solve exact structural matching, GraphDETR naturally extends to approximate matching, enabling detection beyond exact pattern correspondence. Empirically, we show that GraphDETR can detect diverse patterns, such as molecular structures, cycles, cliques, and fuzzy patterns of up to 50 nodes, in target graphs with up to 1000 nodes. We further evaluate on molecular functional group detection over the ChEMBL dataset, where GraphDETR predicts the complete set of functional groups per molecule, achieving a strong performance of $\text{AP}_{100} = 91.2$.

</details>


### [375] [LEVANTE-bench: Multi-Scale Comparison of VLMs to Children Using Cognitive Tasks (or, "Is Your VLM Smarter Than a 5th Grader?")](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05497)
*Alvin Wei Ming Tan,David Cardinal,Tania Lorido-Botran,Laura Bravo-Sanchez,Sunny Yu,Michael C. Frank*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces LEVANTE-bench, a benchmark for comparing vision-language models (VLMs) with human cognitive development across tasks, ages, and populations, showing partial alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding and improving the alignment of vision-language models (VLMs) with human cognitive development across diverse tasks, ages, and cultures.

Method: Developed LEVANTE-bench, a benchmark based on Learning Variability Network data, to compare VLMs to cognitive abilities of children aged 5-12 across six tasks in three countries.

Result: Findings indicate heterogeneous alignment: larger models fared better at task and item-level alignment, but smaller models better matched younger children's error patterns; difficult tasks remain a challenge.

Conclusion: Current VLMs show only partial alignment with children's cognitive development, particularly struggling with specific tasks like matrix reasoning and mental rotation.

Abstract: Given the inherently multimodal nature of human experience, vision-language models (VLMs) hold substantial promise for modeling human cognition as it grows and develops with experience. Realizing their potential requires tools for comparing VLMs with human cognitive development across tasks, ages, and populations. We present LEVANTE-bench, a benchmark based on tasks and data from the Learning Variability Network (LEVANTE), which distributes open-source tasks and data measuring children's cognition across languages and cultures. In LEVANTE-bench, we systematically assess VLMs on six tasks, comparing their alignment with children aged 5-12 ($N$ = 1547) across three countries. We compare models at multiple scales, assessing their overall accuracy, their task- and item-level alignment with children, and how well they match children's trial-level error distributions. Alignment was heterogeneous across scales: at the level of tasks and items, more capable models aligned better with humans. However, match to human error distributions varied widely across tasks, and for several tasks, smaller models matched younger children's errors better. In addition, even the best-performing VLMs struggled on matrix reasoning and mental rotation tasks. Thus, current VLM architectures align only partially with the cognitive abilities of children.

</details>


### [376] [Dominant-Layer ZO: A Single Layer Dominates Zeroth-Order Fine-Tuning of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05516)
*Wanhao Yu,Ziyan Wang,Zheng Wang,Abeer Matar Almalky,Yihang Zuo,Shuteng Niu,Sen Lin,Adnan Siraj Rakin,Deliang Fan,Li Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper finds that zeroth-order fine-tuning in large language models primarily relies on fine-tuning a single dominant decoding layer, which aligns with the first activation-outlier layer. This approach yields efficiency and performance advantages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of zeroth-order optimization in fine-tuning large language models by understanding how useful adaptation is distributed across model layers.

Method: The authors identify the dominant layer using an inference-only analysis of activation outliers in pre-trained models and analyze its properties like perturbation sensitivity and placement in the residual stream. They then evaluate this dominant-layer-focused fine-tuning across multiple models and tasks.

Result: Dominant-layer fine-tuning matches or exceeds the performance of full-model zeroth-order fine-tuning while significantly speeding up training, achieving up to a 4.52× improvement.

Conclusion: Focusing zeroth-order fine-tuning on a single dominant decoding layer, identifiable prior to training, is an effective and efficient strategy for large language models, offering both performance and training speed advantages.

Abstract: Zeroth-order (ZO) optimization enables memory-efficient fine-tuning of large language models (LLMs) using only forward passes, but it remains unclear how useful adaptation is distributed across layers. In this work, we reveal a surprising phenomenon: ZO fine-tuning is sharply dominated by a single decoding layer. Across multiple LLM families and downstream tasks, fine-tuning this dominant layer alone consistently matches or even exceeds full-model ZO fine-tuning. We further show that the dominant layer is task-agnostic but model-specific, and can be identified before training through a simple inference-only analysis of activation outliers. Specifically, the dominant layer consistently aligns with the first activation-outlier layer in the pre-trained model. To explain this phenomenon, we analyze how perturbation effects propagate under ZO optimization. We find that the dominant layer combines two key properties: high perturbation sensitivity and early placement in the residual stream, allowing perturbation-induced effects to propagate and accumulate through remaining subsequent decoding layers. As a result, this layer produces disproportionately strong and stable optimization signals under forward-only updates. Extensive experiments on LLaMA2-7B and Qwen3-8B across nine benchmarks show that dominant-layer ZO fine-tuning improves average performance over full-model MeZO and LoRA-based ZO fine-tuning while achieving up to 4.52$\times$ training speedup.

</details>


### [377] [What Objects Enable, Not What They Are: Functional Latent Spaces for Affordance Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05533)
*Rohan Siva,Neel P. Bhatt,Yunhao Yang,Seoyoung Lee,Nishant Gadde,Christian Ellis,Alvaro Velasquez,Zhangyang Wang,Ufuk Topcu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces A4D, a system for robot planning that focuses on task-relevant functionalities of objects through affordance reasoning, rather than relying solely on visual appearances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current robot planning systems lack generalizability because they rely on appearance-based reasoning, which does not capture task-relevant object functionalities.

Method: The authors propose A4D, which maps visual observations into a latent space structured around affordances, enabling functionality-based planning and incorporating a discovery mechanism for new affordances.

Result: A4D achieved 94% accuracy on existing affordances, outperformed state-of-the-art by 15%, achieved >90% accuracy in new affordance inference with minimal training data, and processes 100x faster inference.

Conclusion: A4D demonstrates significant improvements in accuracy, efficiency, and adaptability in robot planning by focusing on affordance-based reasoning rather than appearance-based approaches.

Abstract: Existing robot planning systems rely on appearance-based reasoning, where visual observations are encoded into latent spaces organized around object appearances (e.g., recognizing a "cart" based on how it looks). However, planning requires reasoning about task-relevant functionalities of objects (e.g., whether an object is "movable"), which appearance-based latent spaces do not capture. As a result, existing approaches struggle to generalize to novel robot-object interactions.
