Drawing on academic literature and guidance from data protection authorities, we find that many GDPR requirements cannot yet be technically met in practice. Our findings further suggest that issues arising in ML supply chains are insufficiently addressed in research. To tackle this gap, we introduce the notion of models in the dark -- derived models created further downstream in an ML chain without sufficient transparency or traceability -- and analyse the urgent challenges posed by this phenomenon. By adopting an interdisciplinary perspective, this work contributes to bridging the gap between legal requirements and the technical implementation of data subject rights in ML, ultimately supporting the development of trustworthy artificial intelligence.

</details>


### [408] [Steering Vectors are an Adversarial Attack Surface](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05958)
*Abzal Aidakhmetov,Donato Crisostomi,Tommaso Mencattini,Adrian Robert Minut,Iacopo Masi,Emanuele Rodolà*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies a stealth data poisoning attack on activation steering pipelines in LLMs, achieving targeted model jailbreaks while maintaining benign steering effects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore vulnerabilities in activation steering methods used to control LLM behavior, particularly against potential stealth attacks by malicious actors.

Method: Demonstrated a stealth data poisoning attack by subtly altering 4-6% of tokens in the steering dataset, tested on two model families across eight model-attribute combinations, and evaluated defenses.

Result: The poisoned vectors achieved 20-55% absolute attack success rates, significantly outperforming clean references. An orthogonalization defense partially mitigated the attack, recovering ~82% of the gap.

Conclusion: Activation steering pipelines are vulnerable to subtle data poisoning attacks, but targeted defenses can reduce the impact while maintaining benign model behavior.

Abstract: Activation steering has become a popular way to control Large Language Model (LLM) behavior without fine-tuning. Since the technique is plug-and-play, users share datasets and precomputed vectors to steer model activations. However, we show that a \emph{stealth data poisoning attack} silently compromises this pipeline. By substituting $4{-}6\%$ of tokens in the steering dataset, an attacker can silently align the resulting vector with an anti-refusal direction. This jailbreaks the target model while preserving the intended steering effect on benign prompts. Under this threat model, a malicious actor can distribute an apparently safe bundle containing texts, vectors, and weights, alongside an equivalence certificate that the end-user can verify. We test the attack on two open-weight model families and eight model-attribute combinations, observing that poisoned vectors reach an absolute attack success rate (ASR) of $20{-}55\%$, $+19\%$ to $+51\%$ over a clean reference. Finally, we find that a refusal-direction orthogonalization defense can recover ${\approx}82\%$ of the ASR gap without harming benign behavior.

</details>


### [409] [LLM Explainability with Counterfactual Chains and Causal Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05972)
*Nirit Nussbaum-Hoffer,Nitay Calderon,Liat Ein-Dor,Roi Reichart*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper explores the use of causal graphs to model how Large Language Models (LLMs) organize high-level concepts during inference, presenting a novel method to construct these graphs for explainability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to enhance transparency in LLMs by using causal graphs as a way to understand how LLMs perceive and organize concepts during inference, addressing a gap in explainability.

Method: The authors propose a four-phase method involving discovering class-discriminative, interpretable concepts, mapping input to concept states, augmenting sparse data via an MCMC-inspired counterfactual method, and performing causal discovery with σ-CG.

Result: They applied the method to three LLMs across various tasks such as diagnosis and sentiment analysis, demonstrating that the learned causal graphs align with the LLMs' reasoning and provide stable, informative, and interpretable structures.

Conclusion: The paper establishes a methodology for creating causal graphs of LLMs, contributing to the field of concept-level explainability and showing that these graphs can capture meaningful dependencies in LLM reasoning.

Abstract: Causal graphs provide a high-level language for making mechanisms transparent. Recent work uses Large Language Models (LLMs) to recover causal graphs of external-world processes. Instead, in this paper, we use causal graphs to model LLM inference itself, providing stakeholders with a transparent view of how the model perceives and organizes high-level concepts to produce a prediction. We propose a four-phase method for constructing such graphs. Given a target LLM and a set of textual examples, our method discovers class-discriminative, human-interpretable concepts and maps each input to LLM-perceived concept states. We then introduce an MCMC-inspired counterfactual augmentation procedure that expands the sparse observational data through chains of counterfactuals. This enables stable causal discovery with $σ$-CG, yielding informative, interpretable graphs. We apply our method to three LLMs across disease diagnosis, sentiment analysis, and LLM-as-a-judge classification tasks. We evaluate the learned graphs for predictive fidelity and structural stability, and the MCMC-inspired augmentation for convergence and downstream utility. Our results show that the discovered causal graphs capture meaningful dependencies consistent with LLMs' reasoning. Together, this paper provides a foundation for concept-level explainability of LLMs.

</details>


### [410] [Compress-Distill: Reasoning Trace Compression for Efficient Knowledge Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05988)
*Maxime Griot,Paul Steven Scotti,Tanishq Mathew Abraham*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies post-hoc compression of chain-of-thought reasoning traces to reduce training and inference costs in knowledge distillation. Compressed traces achieve significant efficiency improvements but come with a trade-off in accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chain-of-thought reasoning traces in knowledge distillation are verbose and require high computational resources for both training and inference, making them costly to utilize.

Method: The study employs two teacher models to generate a large dataset of reasoning traces and uses instruction-tuned models to compress these traces. Experiments are conducted across various configurations to compare compressed, raw, and truncated reasoning traces for efficiency and accuracy.

Result: Compressed traces significantly reduce training tokens (12-30%), speed up training (2.0-7.6x), and shorten inference outputs (3-19x). However, raw traces consistently achieve the highest accuracy. Compression generally outperforms naive truncation, especially for smaller student models.

Conclusion: Reasoning-trace compression provides a trade-off between efficiency and accuracy. While compressed traces can achieve up to 96% of raw-trace accuracy with significantly higher efficiency, raw traces remain the most accurate. The trade-off becomes more favorable with smaller models and LoRA optimizations.

Abstract: Reasoning models produce long chain-of-thought traces that are costly to distill and encourage verbose student outputs. We study post-hoc compression of such traces before knowledge distillation. Two teachers, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B and gpt-oss-120B, generate about 283k correct traces each; two instruction-tuned models then compress them to 8.6-21.0% of their original character length. Across a 48-run main grid plus seven Qwen-teacher truncation ablations, compressed traces reduce training tokens to 12-30% of raw, speed up training by 2.0-7.6x, and shorten inference outputs by 3-19x with smaller reductions under the shorter gpt-oss teacher. However, raw traces retain the highest downstream accuracy at every scale and for both teachers. A length-matched raw-trace truncation ablation shows that compression is not merely benefiting from a smaller token budget: model-compressed traces usually beat or match naive truncation, especially for smaller students, while maintaining shorter inference outputs. Overall, reasoning-trace compression offers an accuracy-efficiency trade-off rather than a free improvement: students retain up to 96% of raw-trace accuracy while gaining up to 18x higher per-token efficiency, and at the 0.8B scale under LoRA compressed traces narrow the raw-vs-compressed gap but do not exceed raw.

</details>


### [411] [HoT-SSM:Higher-order Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning with State Space Models for Health Care](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05994)
*Thummaluru Siddartha Reddy,Vempalli Naga Sai Saketh,Yash Punjabi,Mahesh Chandran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces HoT-SSM, an efficient model for representing medical knowledge graphs in electronic health records, addressing limitations in capturing higher-order interactions and long-range temporal dependencies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing medical knowledge graph-based approaches struggle with capturing complex interactions between clinical concepts and fail to preserve temporal information across patient visits effectively.

Method: HoT-SSM constructs hypergraphs of semantically related clinical concepts for each visit and utilizes a novel dynamic hypergraph-based state space model to capture patient latent states and long-range temporal dynamics.

Result: On MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV datasets, HoT-SSM achieves significant performance improvement over state-of-the-art models, showcasing its ability to model clinical interactions and temporal dependencies effectively.

Conclusion: HoT-SSM enhances clinical prediction tasks by efficiently representing higher-order relations among clinical concepts and modeling long-range temporal dependencies in medical knowledge graphs.

Abstract: Medical knowledge graphs (MKGs) infused with clinical knowledge have been increasingly used to model electronic health records (EHRs) to support interpretable predictions in healthcare domain. However, existing MKG-based approaches are limited in capturing pairwise relations between clinical concepts (e.g., conditions, procedures, and medications), and restricts their ability to model higher-order interactions among co-occurring or semantically related concepts. In addition, most representation learning methods that leverage MKGs either collapse temporal information across visits or lack an explicit mechanism for modeling long-range temporal dependencies, which is critical for clinical tasks such as mortality prediction. To mitigate these limitations, we propose HoT-SSM, a parameter efficient and higher-order temporal graph reasoning with state space models. For each visit, HoT-SSM constructs hypergraphs by grouping semantically related clinical concepts into hyperedges using domain knowledge, thereby preserving visit-level clinical context. Further, to model the temporal dynamics while learning the representations, we introduce a novel dynamic hypergraph-based state space model that explicitly captures patients latent state evolution over time while preserving long-range information. The learned representations are used for downstream clinical prediction and reasoning. Experiments on MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV datasets shows significant performance improvement over the current state-of-the-art models, demonstrating the effectiveness of jointly modeling higher-order clinical interactions and long-range temporal dependencies.

</details>


### [412] [Diffusion Models for Adaptive Sequential Data Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06007)
*Haoyang Cao,Minshuo Chen,Yinbin Han,Renyuan Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new sequential forward-backward diffusion framework to generate realistic time series data adaptively, addressing limitations in current diffusion models for capturing temporal dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion models excel in generating static data but fall short in handling sequential settings, failing to preserve temporal dependencies and adaptive information structure needed for real-world applications like finance and healthcare.

Method: The method uses a sequential forward-backward diffusion process to inject and remove noise while conditioning on previously generated data, ensuring adaptiveness. It incorporates a novel score-matching objective for efficient parallel training and provides statistical guarantees with practical examples using ReLU networks.

Result: The framework was validated on synthetic data such as ARMA models and Gaussian processes, showing its capacity to construct mean-variance optimal portfolios and effectively handle time series generation.

Conclusion: The proposed method successfully addresses the challenges in adaptive time series generation, bridging gaps in existing diffusion models and offering a versatile tool for various real-world sequential data applications.

Abstract: Generating realistic synthetic sequential data is critical in real-world applications across operations research, finance, healthcare, energy systems, and scientific computing, where time-indexed observations are used for prediction, simulation, risk assessment, and data-driven decision-making. While diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in generating static data, their direct extensions to sequential settings often fail to capture temporal dependence and information structure. Designing diffusion models that can simulate sequential data in an adapted manner, and hence without anticipation of future information, therefore remains an open challenge.
  In this work, we propose a sequential forward-backward diffusion framework for adapted time series generation. Our approach progressively injects and removes noise along the sequence, conditioning on the previously generated history to ensure adaptiveness. A novel score-matching objective is introduced for efficient parallel training. We derive rigorous statistical guarantees under a generic framework, then establish score approximation, score estimation, and distribution estimation results with ReLU networks serving as a concrete instance. Empirically, we validate our method on synthetic data, including ARMA models and Gaussian processes, and demonstrate its effectiveness in constructing mean-variance optimal portfolios.

</details>


### [413] [Adaptive Oscillatory-State Alignment for Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06010)
*Zhangyao Song,Ziqiong Li,Xiangfei Qiu,Chao Zha,Yinfei Xu,Tao Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces AOSNET, a Hilbert-guided forecasting framework designed to address challenges in adaptive oscillatory-state alignment for irregular periodic time series.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing periodic forecasting models struggle with real-world time series due to their inability to handle amplitude modulation, phase drift, and local frequency variation.

Method: AOSNET extracts analytic-signal descriptors from observed data and a learnable global oscillatory prior. It uses a descriptor-conditioned gate for adaptive alignment, correcting mismatched regions and enhancing forecasting.

Result: In experiments on eight benchmarks, AOSNET demonstrated state-of-the-art or competitive accuracy with rapid inference. Synthetic tests confirmed its robustness under heightened non-stationarity conditions.

Conclusion: AOSNET provides a flexible approach to modeling dynamic oscillatory states, outperforming traditional fixed-template methods, especially in complex temporal dynamics.

Abstract: Long-term time series forecasting benefits from inductive biases that expose recurring temporal structure. Existing periodic forecasting methods typically model recurrence through predefined periods, global spectral components, or fixed learnable templates. However, real-world temporal dynamics are rarely rigidly periodic: oscillatory behavior often evolves through amplitude modulation, phase drift, and local frequency variation. Under these conditions, fixed-template periodic modeling can become fundamentally mismatched to the underlying temporal states. We propose AOSNET, a Hilbert-guided forecasting framework that reformulates periodic forecasting from fixed template matching to adaptive oscillatory-state alignment. AOSNET extracts analytic-signal descriptors from both the observed sequence and a learnable global oscillatory prior, then adaptively aligns local states through a descriptor-conditioned gate that selectively preserves reliable observations while softly correcting mismatched regions. The learned prior serves not as a rigid repeated template but as a flexible oscillatory reference interpreted through local state dynamics. Experiments on eight benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art or highly competitive accuracy with fast inference speed. Controlled synthetic studies isolating amplitude modulation, phase drift, and local frequency variation confirm that the advantage of oscillatory-state alignment consistently increases as non-stationarity intensifies.

</details>


### [414] [OPRD: On-Policy Representation Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06021)
*Shenzhi Yang,Guangcheng Zhu,Bowen Song,Haobo Wang,Mingxuan Xia,Xing Zheng,Yingfan Ma,Zhongqi Chen,Weiqiang Wang,Gang Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces On-Policy Representation Distillation (OPRD) to improve over On-Policy Distillation (OPD) by incorporating hidden-state alignment, eliminating sampling variance, and delivering better efficiency and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: On-Policy Distillation methods suffer from limitations, such as Monte Carlo KL sampling variance and ignoring teacher's hidden-state information, leading to inefficiencies and performance gaps in training student models.

Method: The proposed method, OPRD, aligns both student and teacher hidden-state representations across selected layers using the same rollouts, bypassing the LM head to avoid sampling variance inherent in traditional OPD.

Result: OPRD bridges the performance gap between student and teacher models on benchmarks like AIME and AIMO, while being 1.44x faster and using 54% less memory compared to top-k OPD baselines.

Conclusion: OPRD outperforms traditional OPD by leveraging hidden-state alignment, enhancing efficiency, and minimizing sampling variance, indicating a significant advancement in on-policy distillation techniques.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) supervises the student only in output space by matching next-token probabilities. This output-only paradigm has two limits: (1) sampling variance from Monte Carlo KL estimates over large vocabularies (e.g., Qwen's ~150k tokens) persists throughout training, and (2) it treats the teacher as a black-box, discarding all intermediate hidden states after the LM head. We propose On-Policy Representation Distillation (OPRD), which lifts distillation into hidden-state space by aligning student and teacher representations across selected layers on the same rollouts, bypassing the LM head entirely. Theoretically, OPRD eliminates sampling variance and provides richer per-layer structural information. Empirically, OPRD closes the student-teacher gap on AIME 2024/2025 and AIMO, while output-space OPD baselines plateau below the teacher. OPRD also trains 1.44x faster and uses 54% less memory than top-k OPD. Code: https://github.com/ShenzhiYang2000/OPRD.

</details>


### [415] [Catastrophic Forgetting as Accessibility Collapse: A Three-Level Framework for Knowledge Persistence in Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06032)
*Ayushman Trivedi,Bhavika Melwani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper reinterprets catastrophic forgetting in neural networks as a failure in information accessibility rather than complete information erasure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to challenge the traditional view of catastrophic forgetting as a loss of learned knowledge and to explore whether task representations might still exist but become inaccessible.

Method: The authors propose a three-level framework (storage, representation, accessibility) and conduct experiments with ResNet-18 on sequential CIFAR-100 classification using various analyses like checkpoint persistence, linear probing, and layer-wise recoverability.

Result: The results show that while task performance collapses (from 54.8% to 0%), retained representational information (76%) and task performance (75.7%) can be restored by retraining just the task classifier, without modifying the backbone network.

Conclusion: Catastrophic forgetting is better seen as a failure in accessing preserved information rather than its complete erasure, with significant task-relevant data embedded in neural representations post-forgetting.

Abstract: Catastrophic forgetting is commonly interpreted as the irreversible erasure of previously acquired knowledge during sequential learning. In this work, we investigate an alternative perspective: that forgetting may arise not from complete destruction of task representations but from a loss of accessibility to preserved information. We introduce a three-level framework separating knowledge storage, representation, and accessibility, and evaluate each component through a series of continual-learning experiments on sequential CIFAR-100 classification using ResNet-18. Our analysis combines checkpoint persistence, linear probing, representation geometry, classifier-reset recovery, and layer-wise recoverability experiments. We observe complete behavioral forgetting of earlier tasks, with task accuracy collapsing from 54.8% to 0%, while linear probe performance retains approximately 76% of the original representational information. Furthermore, retraining only the final classifier restores 75.7% of the original task performance without modifying the backbone network. Layer-wise analysis reveals that early and intermediate layers preserve highly recoverable task information despite severe degradation at later stages. Projection-energy and principal-angle analyses indicate that retained knowledge persists as distributed high-dimensional representations rather than through preservation of a small dominant subspace. These findings suggest that catastrophic forgetting is better characterized as an accessibility failure than complete representational erasure, and that substantial task-relevant information remains embedded within neural representations even after functional forgetting has occurred.

</details>


### [416] [When Good Enough Is Optimal: Multiplication-Only Matrix Inversion Approximation for Quantized Gated DeltaNet](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06034)
*Luoming Zhang,Yuwei Ren,Kui Zhang,Tian Liu,Lingjuan Ge,Denghao Li,Matthew Harper Langston,Yin Huang,Weiliang Will Zeng,Liang Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a fast Matrix Multiplication-based algorithm to address the inefficiency of matrix inversion in chunk-wise parallel linear attention, achieving significant speedup and preserving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Matrix inversion in chunk-wise parallel linear attention is a computationally expensive process and a bottleneck in handling long-context modeling, especially on Neural Processing Units (NPUs) with limited hardware efficiency in traditional methods.

Method: The proposed method uses a MatMul-based algorithm leveraging truncated Neumann expansion, structural masking, parallel residual correction, and low-bits INT adaptions to accelerate and optimize matrix inversion for lower-triangular matrices.

Result: The approach achieves up to 5x speedup at the kernel level and reduces decode-layer overhead by 20% in experiments on Qwen3.5-family models, while maintaining accuracy in both floating-point and low-precision inference.

Conclusion: This MatMul-based solution provides an efficient, scalable, and hardware-friendly approach for linear attention, addressing a critical bottleneck in long-context modeling on modern hardware like NPUs.

Abstract: Matrix inversion in chunk-wise parallel linear attention is a major bottleneck for long-context modeling, particularly on NPUs, where forward-substitution-based methods exhibit limited parallelism and poor hardware utilization. We propose a fast, Matrix Multiplication (MatMul)-based algorithm tailored for strictly lower-triangular matrices arising in chunk-wise linear attention. Motivated by the rapid growth of Neumann-series terms and the diagonal concentration of the inverse matrix, we employ a truncated Neumann expansion with structural masking and parallel residual correction to eliminate sequential dependencies. We further extend our method to low-bits INT by mitigating the dynamic range expansion arising from repeated matrix power operations, and adapt the approximation order and residual step to the chunk size to minimize computational cost while preserving the model's accuracy. Experiments on Qwen3.5-family models demonstrate up to 5$\times$ kernel-level speedup and a 20% reduction in decode-layer overhead, while preserving accuracy under both floating-point and low-precision inference. Our method offers an efficient and hardware-friendly solution for scalable linear attention.

</details>


### [417] [Online KL-Regularized Reinforcement Learning with Function Approximation under Misspecification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06053)
*Haoyang Hong,Zichen Wang,Quanquan Gu,Huazheng Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses KL-regularized contextual bandits and reinforcement learning under misspecified models. It proposes KL misspecification formulations and establishes high-probability KL-regret guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing guarantees in KL-regularized contextual bandits and episodic RL rely on realizability, which does not extend well to misspecified models. There's a need for a framework to handle model misspecifications.

Method: The authors introduce KL misspecification formulations for contextual bandits and RL. They analyze regression-based algorithms with Gibbs policy updates and derive explicit KL-regret guarantees accounting for misspecification.

Result: The study provides KL-regret guarantees with explicit terms for misspecification, demonstrating that they reduce to standard realizable guarantees under realizable conditions.

Conclusion: KL misspecification frameworks offer robust guarantees even for misspecified models, extending classical results and enabling more general applications.

Abstract: We study KL-regularized contextual bandits and episodic reinforcement learning (RL) under general function approximation with model misspecification. Existing guarantees rely on realizability and therefore do not extend to misspecified models, where classical regret bounds may fail. This work introduces KL misspecification formulations for contextual bandits and episodic RL and analyzes regression-based algorithms with Gibbs policy updates. High-probability KL-regret guarantees with explicit misspecification terms are established, recovering the standard realizable KL-regularized setting as a special case.

</details>


### [418] [MDP-GRPO: Stabilized Group Relative Policy Optimization for Multi-Constraint Instruction Following](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06058)
*Mohammad Mahdi Salmani-Zarchi,Zahra Rahimi,Heshaam Faili,Mohammad Javad Dousti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses instabilities in Group-Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for reinforcement learning under low-dispersion discrete rewards by proposing a new method, MDP-GRPO, which resolves these issues and achieves better constraint satisfaction and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard GRPO struggles with stability in low-dispersion reward settings, which are common in multi-constraint instruction-following tasks. Addressing these challenges is vital for reliable performance.

Method: The authors propose MDP-GRPO, a method comprising four techniques: (1) multi-temperature sampling, (2) dual-anchor advantages, (3) prospect-theoretic shaping, and (4) asymmetric KL regularization. These collectively stabilize training and address identified pathologies.

Result: MDP-GRPO outperforms GRPO in experiments, showing a 5.0% improvement in strict constraint satisfaction and enabling stable learning with small group sizes while maintaining general performance.

Conclusion: MDP-GRPO effectively resolves identified issues in GRPO, boosting constraint satisfaction, enhancing stability, and maintaining general capabilities in instruction-following tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards is ideal for multi-constraint instruction following, yet standard group-relative policy optimization (GRPO) becomes unstable under discrete, low-dispersion rewards, where within-group reward distributions are frequently homogeneous. We identify and formalize three pathologies of z-score group normalization in this regime: low-variance amplification, mean-centering blindness, and zero-variance collapse. To address them, we propose MDP-GRPO, which stabilizes learning through (1) multi-temperature sampling to increase reward dispersion, (2) dual-anchor advantages to restore gradients in homogeneous groups and stop mean-centering blindness, (3) prospect-theoretic shaping to bound updates and penalize violations based on Kahneman and Tversky's theory, and (4) asymmetric KL regularization. Evaluated on FollowBench, IFEval, and a curated multi-constraint dataset, MDP-GRPO outperforms standard GRPO, improving strict constraint satisfaction by up to 5.0% on Llama-3.2-3B. Our method also enables stable convergence with small group sizes while preserving general capabilities on MMLU and ARC.

</details>


### [419] [On Advantage Estimates for Max@K Policy Gradients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06080)
*Shota Takashiro,Soichiro Nishimori,Paavo Parmas,Yongmin Kim,Kohsei Matsutani,Gouki Minegishi,Yusuke Iwasawa,Takeshi Kojima,Yutaka Matsuo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper focuses on reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards, addressing sparse outcome rewards and inference-time objectives for large language model (LLM) post-training. It proposes the MaxPO method, which uses a novel Leave-Two-Out baseline to unify and enhance reward estimation methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address difficulties arising from sparse outcome rewards in reinforcement learning and to unify the approach to policy-gradient estimators for inference-time objectives such as pass@K and max@K.

Method: The paper introduces the MaxPO method, which employs a Leave-Two-Out (L2O) baseline to create centered advantages while maintaining policy-gradient unbiasedness. This method is efficient and integrates into group-based reinforcement learning for large language models.

Result: The authors provide empirical evidence showing that the L2O baseline reduces gradient variance and leads to better performance compared to non-centered advantage estimators.

Conclusion: The MaxPO method improves the optimization of inference-time objectives by unifying baseline designs, reducing gradient variance, and integrating efficiently with reinforcement learning for LLM post-training.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards is widely used for post-training reasoning models, but sparse outcome rewards make exploration difficult. A complementary approach is to optimize inference-time objectives such as pass@K and max@K directly, yet existing policy-gradient estimators for these objectives use different signals, baselines, and normalizations, making their relationships unclear. We study this issue through baseline design and advantage centering. Starting from the advantage estimator of a leading method in the field, we show that it is policy-gradient unbiased but yields a non-centered advantage. We then introduce a Leave-Two-Out baseline that preserves policy-gradient unbiasedness while making realized batch advantages exactly centered. The resulting method, MaxPO, has an efficient quadratic-time implementation and integrates naturally into group-based RL for LLM post-training. We further derive the canonical finite-batch advantage for max@K, providing a unified view of existing advantage estimators. Empirically, we verify that the L2O baseline reduces gradient variance and outperforms non-centered alternatives.

</details>


### [420] [OrderGrad: Optimizing Beyond the Mean with Order-Statistic Policy Gradient Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06096)
*Paavo Parmas,Yongmin Kim,Kohsei Matsutani,Shota Takashiro,Soichiro Nishimori,Takeshi Kojima,Yusuke Iwasawa,Yutaka Matsuo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OrderGrad is a method for optimizing order-statistic objectives using policy-gradient techniques, handling risks and robust exploratory learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current policy-gradient methods optimize expected returns but fail to address real-world application requirements such as tail risk, robust outcomes, or best-of-K discovery.

Method: OrderGrad employs likelihood-ratio and reparameterization gradient estimators for order-statistic objectives by transforming rewards in a way compatible with policy-gradient updates.

Result: The method provides unbiased gradient estimators for order-statistic objectives like VaR, CVaR, and medians, showcasing improved effectiveness in tasks misaligned with mean optimization.

Conclusion: OrderGrad offers a versatile, easily integrated approach for learning objectives focused on risk aversion, robustness, and exploratory behavior.

Abstract: Policy-gradient methods usually optimize expected return, but many real world applications care about distributional properties of returns: tail risk, outlier robustness, or best-of-K discovery. We introduce OrderGrad, a family of likelihood-ratio and reparameterization gradient estimators for order-statistic objectives. OrderGrad optimizes finite-sample L-statistics, i.e., weighted averages of sorted rewards or costs, recovering objectives such as VaR, CVaR, trimmed means, medians, and top-m/best-of-K criteria by changing only the rank weights. For any fixed sample size and rank-weight vector, OrderGrad provides an unbiased gradient estimator for the corresponding order-statistic objective. The method is implemented as a simple reward transformation that can then be used in an otherwise standard policy-gradient or reparameterized update. We study the resulting estimator's variance behavior and evaluate it on tasks where mean optimization is mismatched to the deployment objective, including LLM math post-training and other tasks. OrderGrad provides a unified, plug-and-play route to risk-averse, robust, and exploratory learning.
  Code: https://github.com/paavo5/ordergrad

</details>


### [421] [A Sliced-Wasserstein Framework on Correlation Matrices for EEG Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06104)
*Chen Hu,Rui Wang,Jiale Zhou,Jingjun Yi,Shaocheng Jin,Yidong Song,Yefeng Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Pullback Euclidean Metric Sliced Wasserstein (PEMSW) for EEG decoding, improving generalization under distribution shifts in EEG datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the sensitivity of covariance descriptors in EEG decoding pipelines to channel-wise scaling, and to improve robustness and generalization of EEG decoding under distribution shifts.

Method: Proposes a novel general framework called Pullback Euclidean Metric Sliced Wasserstein (PEMSW), which includes two Correlation Sliced-Wasserstein (CorSW) discrepancies using specific correlation geometries, and applies this within a domain generalization framework for EEG decoding.

Result: Experiments on three EEG datasets show improved generalization performance under distribution shifts, with low training overhead and no additional inference costs.

Conclusion: The proposed PEMSW framework, with CorSW discrepancies, is an effective solution for scale-invariant EEG decoding, offering robust generalization capabilities in challenging conditions.

Abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) offers noninvasive, millisecond resolution recordings of neuronal activity and is widely used in neuroscience and healthcare. Many EEG decoding pipelines rely on covariance descriptors for their robustness to noise, but such representations are sensitive to channel-wise scaling. Recent studies have therefore advocated full-rank correlation matrices as a scale-invariant alternative for EEG decoding. In this paper, we propose a general framework for Sliced Wasserstein (SW) discrepancies on manifolds endowed with Pullback Euclidean Metrics (PEMs), termed Pullback Euclidean Metric Sliced Wasserstein (PEMSW). Within this framework, we instantiate two Correlation Sliced-Wasserstein (CorSW) discrepancies on the manifold of full-rank correlation matrices under two recently introduced correlation geometries, \textit{i.e.}, the Off-Log Metric (OLM) and Log-Scaled Metric (LSM). Building on CorSW, we further develop a domain generalization (DG) framework for EEG decoding. Experiments on three EEG datasets demonstrate improved generalization under distribution shifts, with low training overhead and no additional inference cost. The source code is available at https://github.com/ChenHu-ML/CorSW.

</details>


### [422] [Tight list replicability bounds via a novel sphere covering theorem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06148)
*Ari Blondal,Hamed Hatami,Pooya Hatami,Chavdar Lalov,Sivan Tretiak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper explores the relationship between list size and accuracy for replicability in learning theory, utilizing a novel topological sphere covering theorem derived from the Borsuk-Ulam theorem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the foundational problem in learning theory of determining how list size correlates with accuracy and complexity for replicability in various hypothesis classes.

Method: The authors develop a topological sphere covering theorem and apply it to analyze replicability bounds for VC classes and large-margin half-spaces.

Result: They derive sharp bounds for list replicability in VC classes and demonstrate that for large-margin half-spaces, the optimal list size can range from the ambient dimension to $\lceil d/2 \rceil + 1$ depending on the margin.

Conclusion: The research advances the theoretical understanding of list replicability by leveraging topological techniques, leading to precise bounds for specific hypothesis classes.

Abstract: In recent years, list replicability has emerged as a framework for formalizing reproducibility in learning theory. A central question is how the required list size relates to the accuracy parameter and natural complexity measures of the hypothesis class.
  To achieve sharp bounds on list replicability, we prove a novel topological sphere covering theorem, derived from the Borsuk-Ulam theorem. Specifically, if the $d$-sphere is covered by open sets, each of which lies in an open hemisphere, then $d+1$ of these sets must have a common intersection. Using this result, we obtain a sharp bound on the relationship between list size and accuracy for VC classes. We also show that for large-margin half-spaces, provided the margin is not too large, the optimal list size equals the ambient dimension. However, when the margin is taken to be very large, we devise a replicable algorithm achieving the minimal list size of $\lceil d/2 \rceil + 1$.

</details>


### [423] [Trust-Aware Predictive Emissions Monitoring for Gas Turbine Fleets with Limited Labelled Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06156)
*Rebecca Potts,Aiden Durrant,Rick Hackney,Georgios Leontidis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a trust-aware probabilistic framework for predicting NOx emissions in gas turbines using machine learning, even when only partial labeling is available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Emissions monitoring in gas turbines is crucial, but acquiring labeled data for all turbines in a fleet is often impractical. An alternative framework is needed to enable accurate and reliable predictions under limited labeled supervision.

Method: The framework integrates a multi-head recurrent prediction model with learned confidence estimation, uncertainty quantification, auxiliary feature prediction, feature-space analysis, and operational diagnostics. Trust scores per sample are used to assess prediction reliability.

Result: With confidence-based filtering, the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of predictions was reduced significantly, demonstrating the model's ability to identify the most reliable predictions. The framework also successfully detects unlabelled and out-of-distribution samples.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively ensures reliable NOx emissions prediction in scenarios with limited supervision, supporting transparent and trustworthy deployment across gas turbine fleets.

Abstract: Machine learning-based predictive emissions monitoring systems offer a practical alternative to direct emissions measurement, but their deployment across gas turbine fleets is challenging when emissions labels are available for only a small subset of assets. In this work, a trust-aware probabilistic framework is proposed for fleet-level gas turbine NOx prediction under limited labelled supervision. The framework combines a multi-head recurrent prediction model with learned confidence estimation, ensemble-based uncertainty quantification, auxiliary feature prediction, feature-space distance analysis, and operating-range diagnostics. These signals are calibrated on labelled data to produce interpretable per-sample trust scores, providing indicators of prediction reliability on unlabelled turbines, supporting the identification of predictions that should be treated with greater caution during fleet-level deployment. Confidence-based filtering reduces MAE from 0.202 at full coverage to 0.070 for the highest-confidence 10\% of predictions, demonstrating that confidence estimates are meaningfully related to prediction error. Unlabelled and out-of-distribution samples exhibit increased uncertainty and reduced confidence, indicating that the framework responds appropriately to distributional shift. The results show that the proposed trust framework provides actionable reliability information for emissions prediction on unlabelled turbines, supporting more transparent and trustworthy deployment of PEMS across industrial fleets.

</details>


### [424] [On the training of physics-informed neural operators for solving parametric partial differential equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06164)
*Nanxi Chen,Chuanjie Cui,Airong Chen,Sifan Wang,Rujin Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how to efficiently and robustly train Physics-informed Neural Operators (PINOs) by systematically examining architecture, optimization techniques, and data regimes for various PDE systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To optimize the training of PINOs, which combine neural operator generalization and physics-informed data efficiency, but whose training process is less understood compared to data-driven neural operators and PINNs.

Method: The authors analyzed architectural designs (DeepONet, FNO, CViT), optimization practices, and data handling strategies across five parametric PDE systems to evaluate PINO effectiveness.

Result: CViT emerged as a top performer among the architectures studied. Optimization challenges like gradient conflicts in PINNs persist in PINOs but are manageable using similar mitigation strategies. Physics-informed training can match or even outperform data-driven approaches in certain cases.

Conclusion: The study builds a comprehensive understanding of PINO training challenges and offers a practical guide to accelerating and refining physics-informed operator learning. Code and data were made publicly accessible.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural operators (PINOs) aim to learn solution operators for partial differential equations by using the governing physics as supervision, rather than relying solely on paired input-output simulation data. By incorporating physical constraints into the training objective, PINOs combine the cross-instance generalization of neural operators with the data efficiency of physics-informed learning. Despite this promise, how to train PINOs efficiently and robustly remains less well-understood than the training of either data-driven neural operators or physics-informed neural networks (PINNs). To bridge this gap, we examine key components of the PINO training pipeline, including architecture design, optimizer choice, loss balancing, and collocation-point sampling strategy. We study three representative operator backbones, Deep Operator Network (DeepONet), Fourier Neural Operator (FNO), and Continuous Vision Transformer (CViT), across five diverse parametric PDE systems. Our results show that CViT provides consistently strong and stable performance across the considered benchmarks. Beyond architecture, we find that several optimization pathologies previously identified in PINN training naturally arise in PINOs, including gradient conflicts and causal violation. We also find that mitigation algorithms developed for PINNs remain effective in the PINO setting. We further compare physics-informed and data-driven training under different data regimes, revealing that a carefully designed physics-informed training pipeline can match, and in some cases, outperform purely data-driven neural operators. Taken together, these findings provide a systematic empirical understanding of the optimization challenges in PINO training and inform a practical pipeline for efficient and robust physics-informed operator learning. Code and data are available at https://github.com/NanxiiChen/PI-CViT.

</details>


### [425] [Learning to model pediatric asthma exacerbation from multiple risk factors: a case study in coastal Virginia](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06174)
*Jonathan Colen,Eric Werner,Maryam Golbazi,Heather Richter,Diana McSpadden,Amy Quinn,Jocel Santos,Mary Jane Darling,Mary Margaret Gleason*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The study compares three modeling techniques (GLM, NN, and sparse dictionary learning) for predicting childhood asthma exacerbations (AE) in a coastal Virginia region influenced by air pollution, weather, and socioeconomic factors. The models find consensus on AE risk factors, enabling potential public health interventions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Childhood asthma exacerbation is influenced by multiple factors, including air pollution, weather, and socioeconomic conditions. There is a need for models that disentangle these factors to improve predictions and inform interventions.

Method: The authors used three modeling techniques: Generalized Linear Models (GLM) as a baseline, Neural Networks (NN) for maximum predictive power, and an interpretable sparse dictionary learning framework. They analyzed a dataset from 2018-2023, including air pollution, weather, neighborhood opportunity, and acute AE visit data.

Result: The comparative study demonstrated that all three models (GLM, NN, and sparse dictionary learning) provided reliable predictions of AE and exhibited consensus on the relative risks and interacting factors influencing asthma exacerbations.

Conclusion: The study bridges statistical modeling and interpretable machine learning, offering insights into interacting factors driving childhood asthma exacerbations. This approach may facilitate better public health planning and interventions in regions like coastal Virginia.

Abstract: Childhood asthma is a common illness exacerbated by air pollution as well as meteorological and neighborhood-level socioeconomic factors. Modeling asthma exacerbation (AE) in large spatiotemporal datasets requires disentangling impacts from multiple contributors. In this case study, we compared three techniques that balance predictive power with interpretability to predict AE in Hampton Roads, a coastal Virginia region comprising 7 cities and over 1.5 million people. After collating ambient air pollution measurements, weather data, and measures of neighborhood opportunity, we modeled zip code-level acute AE visits to a regional children's hospital and affiliated providers from 2018-2023. Generalized linear models (GLM) provided a baseline while neural networks (NN) served as a maximally predictive target. To bridge between statistical models and deep learning, we developed a framework based on sparse dictionary learning to identify and interpret parsimonious nonlinear interacting equations. After comparing each model's predictive performance, we estimated relative risks for AE due to input exposure variables and found consensus across frameworks. Our work links statistical and interpretable machine learning models to highlight possible synergistic interactions influencing AE, and may enable future studies to guide public health interventions in coastal Virginia.

</details>


### [426] [Learning to Route LLMs from Implicit Cost-Performance Preferences via Meta-Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06178)
*Jiahao Zeng,Ming Tang,Ningning Ding*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces MetaRouter, a meta-learning framework for cost-efficient LLM routing that personalizes model selection based on user preferences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs come with a trade-off between performance and cost, and current routing methods fail to accommodate diverse user cost-performance preferences.

Method: The authors propose a perceptive LLM routing paradigm that uses contextual bandit formulation to model user preferences. MetaRouter, their meta-learning framework, efficiently personalizes LLM routing by capturing implicit user preferences with minimal interaction.

Result: MetaRouter outperformed baselines in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution tasks. It demonstrated high efficiency in learning preferences, robustness to changing LLMs, and scalability to multiple models.

Conclusion: MetaRouter presents a promising approach to balancing cost and performance in LLMs by enabling personalized, efficient, and robust routing tailored to user preferences.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) present a trade-off between performance and cost, where more powerful models incur greater expense. LLM routing aims to mitigate expenses while maintaining performance by sending queries to the most suitable model. However, existing methods cannot perform well for different user cost-performance preferences. To address this gap, we introduce a novel perceptive LLM routing paradigm for personalized and user-centric cost-performance optimization, which efficiently learns users' implicit preferences through little interaction. To handle the challenge of heterogeneous user needs, we formulate preference profiles as a set of distinct tasks in contextual bandit and propose MetaRouter, a meta-learning framework designed for preference-aware LLM routing. Experimental results show that MetaRouter outperforms strong baselines on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution tasks. Furthermore, it exhibits high efficiency in learning user preferences, robustness to changes in the routable LLMs, and scalability to multi-model routing.

</details>


### [427] [A Machine Learning-Based Framework for Discovering Huntington's Disease Stages: Integrating Graph Representation Learning and clustering to Uncover Progression Dynamics in Longitudinal Enroll-HD Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06196)
*Lubna M. Abu Zohair,Marta Vallejo,MD Azher Uddin,John R. Woodward,Hind Zantout*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents an unsupervised machine learning framework to dynamically and accurately stage Huntington's disease by clustering patient data into meaningful disease stages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The research aims to improve the accuracy and consistency of Huntington's disease staging, overcoming limitations of existing clinical frameworks that rely on subjective thresholds and are prone to inter-rater variability.

Method: The researchers developed a dynamic graph-based unsupervised learning model, applied K-means++ clustering, and conducted stability analysis to discover robust and distinct clusters for staging patients with Huntington's disease.

Result: The framework effectively grouped 302 Huntington's disease patients into four meaningful and statistically distinct stages using dynamic graph representations and stability analysis.

Conclusion: The proposed model provides a data-driven, objective, and robust method for identifying Huntington's disease stages while addressing intra-stage and inter-rater variability of previous methods.

Abstract: Huntington's disease (HD) is a progressive brain disorder that gradually affects movement, cognitive function, and behavior. Identifying the stage of the disease accurately and consistently is important for understanding its course, grouping patients, personalized care, and discovering treatment. Existing clinical staging frameworks rely primarily on predefined clinical measurement thresholds and clinical expert decisions, yet these discrete cut-offs may obscure meaningful intra-stage variability and remain vulnerable to inter-rater differences, especially in motor and functional assessments. To address these limitations, we developed an unsupervised machine learning framework based on dynamic graph representation learning to capture temporal relationships within and across patients from longitudinal clinical measurements. Using the learned representations, we applied K-means++ clustering to identify well-separated groups. We then iteratively increased the number of clusters (k), using stability analysis to assess robustness and reveal additional meaningful clusters beyond the initial optimal solution. We applied the framework to 302 individuals from the Enroll-HD cohort (1,477 visits, 44 clinical variables per visit; 80% manifest participants), enabling data-driven discovery of HD stages reflecting natural clinical progression. Despite the limited cohort size, the proposed framework achieved robust clustering performance using a four-dimensional latent space, identifying four meaningful and statistically distinct disease stages through clustering stability analysis. Each stage corresponded to well-defined clinical measurement boundaries, with minimal overlap compared to previously established clinical staging methods.

</details>


### [428] [Non-Negative Matrix Factorization for Event Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06205)
*Raphaël Romero*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces EventNMF, a method for analyzing continuous-time event data that avoids the limitations of binning or smoothing by modeling event times directly via non-negative B-spline basis Poisson processes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for analyzing continuous-time event data rely on preprocessing steps like binning, which can erase fine-grained temporal features and entity-level heterogeneities.

Method: EventNMF models events as Poisson processes with intensities that factorize through non-negative B-spline bases. It offers a computationally efficient and mathematically principled framework without the need for binning or smoothing.

Result: EventNMF recovers interpretable temporal templates and outperforms existing models in terms of bias-variance tradeoffs, demonstrated on synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed EventNMF method provides an effective and interpretable tool for uncovering temporal patterns in continuous-time event data, with applications across various domains.

Abstract: Continuous-time event data, in which entities emit instantaneous events over time, arises naturally across many domains such as neuroscience, seismology, and social networks. Non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) is a natural tool to uncover interpretable structure in such data, but it has so far only been applied after binning or smoothing the entity-level counting measures. This preprocessing step comes with the risk of erasing entity-level heterogeneities and fine-grained temporal features. In this paper, we introduce EventNMF, a continuous-time non-negative factorization model that operates directly on event times: each entity's events are modeled as a Poisson process whose intensity factorizes through a non-negative B-spline basis, and a simple estimation procedure recovers interpretable temporal templates shared across entities. The resulting method is mathematically principled, easy to implement, and computationally efficient. We further show that standard binned-count approaches arise as the special case of degree-zero splines, explore bias-variance tradeoffs and compare against existing methods on a synthetic latent factor model, and demonstrate the effectiveness of EventNMF on several real-world applications.

</details>


### [429] [Design a Reliable LLM-Integrated Interface for Mortality Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06235)
*Thi Kim Ngan Nguyen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new LLM-integrated interface enhances mortality forecasting usability while preserving statistical accuracy and transparency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mortality forecasting is complex for non-expert users despite its importance in actuarial and policymaking decisions.

Method: A structured forecasting pipeline integrates a large language model (LLM) to process natural-language inputs into deterministic configurations, with evaluations conducted using CoMoMo package, rolling-origin methods, and user-facing prototype interfaces.

Result: The system achieves enhanced usability and accuracy while fulfilling reproducibility, transparency, and actuarial requirements.

Conclusion: LLMs can improve accessibility in mortality forecasting workflows without sacrificing analytical integrity.

Abstract: Mortality forecasting plays an important role in actuarial and policy decision-making, but its implementation remains technically complex and inaccessible to non-expert users. This project proposes a reliable large language model (LLM)-integrated interface that improves usability while maintaining statistical power. The LLM is designed as a constrained orchestration layer that translates natural-language inputs into structured configurations for a deterministic forecasting pipeline. A three-phase methodology is employed to ensure accuracy, usability, and transparency. First, a baseline pipeline is implemented using the CoMoMo package, reproducing established mortality forecasting results. Second, the pipeline is extended to generate multi-step forecasts using rolling-origin evaluation and mean squared error (MSE). Third, a prototype interface uses a local LLM to handle users' forecasting requests in plain language. The system demonstrates that LLMs can enhance accessibility without compromising reproducibility, transparency, or actuarial validity in high-stakes analytical workflows.

</details>


### [430] [Tracing the Oracle: Improving Diffusion Timestep Scheduling for 3D CT Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06236)
*Yujia Wu,Zhaoqiang Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Tracing the Oracle (TrO), a framework to optimize timestep schedules in pretrained diffusion models for 3D CT reconstruction, boosting efficiency and accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the computational inefficiencies and truncation errors caused by uniform timestep schedules in pretrained diffusion models for solving challenging 3D CT inverse problems.

Method: The authors propose TrO, which uses dynamic programming to optimize timestep schedules by minimizing the cumulative error between a densely sampled oracle trajectory and few-step approximations, enabling better allocation of sampling steps to reduce truncation errors.

Result: Experiments on the AAPM dataset using 3D CT reconstruction tasks show that TrO, when paired with the state-of-the-art DDS method, delivers significantly better reconstruction fidelity and computational efficiency under strict sampling step budgets.

Conclusion: Tracing the Oracle effectively overcomes the limitations of uniform timestep schedules in diffusion models, offering a plug-and-play solution that improves 3D CT reconstruction results and efficiency.

Abstract: Pretrained diffusion models demonstrate impressive potential in solving highly ill-posed 3D computed tomography (CT) inverse problems, while the inference process suffers from significant computational overhead. Furthermore, existing uniform timestep schedules fail to capture the non-uniform evolution of the reverse conditional diffusion stochastic differential equation, thereby introducing substantial truncation errors. To overcome this limitation, we propose Tracing the Oracle (TrO), a plug-and-play framework for improved timestep scheduling. Specifically, we treat densely sampled numerical integration trajectories on a few samples as the reference oracle. The optimized schedule is extracted by leveraging dynamic programming to globally minimize the cumulative error between the few-step approximation and the oracle. This mechanism precisely allocates the limited sampling steps to critical evolution stages that are highly susceptible to truncation errors. Our extensive experiments on the AAPM dataset across multiple 3D CT reconstruction tasks demonstrate that, when combined with the state-of-the-art 3D CT reconstruction method DDS, our optimized timesteps significantly improve reconstruction fidelity and computational efficiency compared to existing heuristic schedules, especially under a strict budget of no more than 10 sampling steps.

</details>


### [431] [Generative Criticality in Large Language Model Temperature Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06238)
*Huajian Ruan,Jinyang Li,Xingyu Guo,Lingxiao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a statistical framework to analyze text from large language models, revealing phase-transition-like behavior as the softmax temperature changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand the collective statistical structure of large language model (LLM) outputs and their connection to critical phenomena.

Method: The study treats token embeddings as continuous variables, analyzes their behavior across varying softmax temperatures, and uses tools like susceptibility, order parameter, and intrinsic dimensionality (TwoNN method).

Result: The study identifies a peak in susceptibility and a sharp change in the order parameter near a critical temperature $T_c$, suggesting phase-transition-like behavior. These results are consistent across models and prompt types.

Conclusion: LLM outputs exhibit phenomena akin to continuous phase transitions. This framework offers insights into decoding strategies and highlights the need for further non-equilibrium investigations.

Abstract: We propose a statistical-field framework for text generated by large language models (LLMs), treating token embeddings as continuous spin variables on a one-dimensional chain. Defining a susceptibility from the connected two-point correlator and an order parameter from the ensemble-averaged embedding field, we vary the \texttt{softmax} temperature $T$ and observe a sharp susceptibility peak near a characteristic $T_c$ with power-law-like scaling, a concurrent rapid change in the order parameter, and a collapse onto a single semantic direction below $T_c$. The intrinsic dimension estimated by the two nearest neighbor (TwoNN) method independently corroborates these findings, reaching a minimum near $T_c$. Results are robust across model scales (Qwen3: 0.6B--32B) and prompt categories. While the phenomenology closely resembles a continuous phase transition, the non-equilibrium nature of autoregressive generation warrants further investigation. Our framework provides quantitative tools for probing the collective statistical structure of LLM outputs and suggests connections between decoding strategies and critical phenomena.

</details>


### [432] [Your GFlowNet Secretly Learns an Optimal Transport Plan](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06272)
*Ian Maksimov,Nikita Morozov,Denis Belomestny,Sergey Samsonov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper establishes a theoretical link between Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) and optimal transport (OT), showing that GFlowNets can serve as a framework for solving OT problems efficiently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the connection between non-acyclic Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) and optimal transport problems, particularly focusing on improving the capabilities of GFlowNets to handle large and structured OT applications.

Method: The authors reduce the minimum-flow GFlowNet objective to a Kantorovich OT problem by fixing the initial flow distribution and leveraging graph-induced shortest path costs. The resulting GFlowNet policy encodes an optimal transport plan, and the approach incorporates neural parameterization for application to large graphs.

Result: The paper demonstrates that the learned GFlowNet policy equates to an optimal transport plan, and experiments confirm agreement with existing OT solvers as well as the ability of GFlowNets to produce high-quality transport plans.

Conclusion: GFlowNets can effectively solve OT problems on large and complex graphs by learning optimal transport plans, providing a novel application and theoretical basis for GFlowNets in this domain.

Abstract: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) are a framework for sampling structured objects via stochastic trajectories in a directed graph. In this work, we establish a theoretical connection between non-acyclic GFlowNets and optimal transport (OT). We show that fixing the initial flow distribution in a minimum-flow GFlowNet reduces its objective to a Kantorovich OT problem with graph-induced shortest path costs. At the optimum, the learned GFlowNet policy therefore encodes an optimal transport plan from the source distribution to the target distribution: we show that sampling trajectories from the minimum-flow GFlowNet recovers the corresponding optimal coupling. Our formulation enables applying the GFlowNet learning framework to OT problems on large graphs via edge flows and neural parameterization. Experiments confirm agreement with exact OT solvers and demonstrate that GFlowNets can learn high-quality transport plans.

</details>


### [433] [Reactive Flux Matching: Mechanism Discovery and Adaptive Sampling of Rare Events](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06295)
*Rishal Aggarwal,David Ryan Koes,Nicholas M. Boffi,Eric Vanden-Eijnden*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Flux Matching, a framework to learn reaction pathways and reaction coordinates from trajectory data without prior knowledge of dynamics or distribution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extracting mechanistic insights from reactive trajectory data remains challenging, despite advances in path sampling methods.

Method: Flux Matching learns current velocity and scalar potential directly from trajectory data, minimizing quadratic functionals, and relies on adaptive interfaces for better sampling.

Result: Validated via trajectory generation and rate constant calculations in molecular systems.

Conclusion: Flux Matching effectively provides tools for understanding and sampling reaction mechanisms in non-Markovian and complex systems.

Abstract: Path sampling methods generate ensembles of reactive trajectories connecting metastable states, but extracting mechanistic insight from these data remains nontrivial. We introduce Flux Matching, a framework that learns two complementary objects directly from reactive trajectory data: a current velocity $u(z)$, whose streamlines trace the dominant reaction pathways, and a scalar potential $h(z)$, obtained from a weighted Helmholtz-Hodge decomposition of the reactive current, that serves as a data-driven reaction coordinate. Both minimize quadratic functionals over the reactive path ensemble, analogous to the flow matching loss in generative modeling, and require no knowledge of the underlying dynamics or stationary distribution. Unlike committor-based methods, $u$ and $h$ remain well-defined under projection onto non-Markovian collective variables, and their level sets in turn provide adaptive interfaces for improved sampling with enhanced sampling methods. Flux Matching is validated through the generation of current velocity trajectories and rate constant calculations on molecular systems.

</details>


### [434] [Plug-and-Play Guidance for Discrete Diffusion Models via Gradient-Informed Logit Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06303)
*Hongkun Dou,Zike Chen,Fengji Li,Hongjue Li,Yue Deng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces GILC, a plug-and-play framework for controllable generation using discrete diffusion models, eliminating the need for retraining while maintaining computational efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome challenges in controllable generation with discrete diffusion models, particularly the computational burden and necessity of retraining.

Method: A new framework called GILC is proposed, leveraging a pretrained denoising network and a Jacobian-free mechanism to correct logits and provide guidance signals, compatible with both differentiable and non-differentiable reward functions.

Result: GILC achieves state-of-the-art performance in DNA, protein sequence, and molecular generation tasks without requiring additional training.

Conclusion: The proposed GILC framework is effective and computationally efficient, enabling controllable discrete diffusion model generation that often outperforms existing fine-tuning methods.

Abstract: Controllable generation with discrete diffusion models is often hindered by high computational overhead or the need for retraining. In this paper, we present \underline{\textbf{G}}radient-\underline{\textbf{I}}nformed \underline{\textbf{L}}ogit \underline{\textbf{C}}orrection (\textbf{GILC}), a plug-and-play framework that efficiently estimates guidance signals by repurposing the pretrained denoising network as a variational proxy. To circumvent the gradient instability inherent in high-dimensional discrete spaces, we introduce a Jacobian-free mechanism that directly corrects the clean prediction logits, facilitating stable and effective guidance. Our method accommodates both differentiable and non-differentiable reward functions. Extensive experiments across DNA, protein sequence, and molecular generation tasks demonstrate that GILC achieves state-of-the-art performance without additional training, frequently outperforming fine-tuning approaches.

</details>


### [435] [Learning What to Forget: Improving LLM Unlearning via Learned Token-Level Importance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06320)
*Gizem Yüce,Giorgos Nikolaou,Nicolas Flammarion*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Alternating Token-Weighted Unlearning (ATWU), a framework for unlearning in language models by optimizing token-level specificity without external supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve machine unlearning for language models by addressing token-level relevance in forgetting and avoiding reliance on extensive external resources, heuristics, or auxiliary models.

Method: A joint optimization framework that determines forget-specific tokens through interaction with retain objectives, formalized as a joint optimization problem, and implements a lightweight ATWU method using a linear scorer over hidden states.

Result: ATWU achieves state-of-the-art performance in forget-retain trade-offs, surpassing existing methods while aligning well with semantically meaningful forget-specific spans.

Conclusion: Retain conflict is a viable criterion for token forgetting, and ATWU provides an effective and computationally efficient approach to handle unsupervised token-level unlearning in language models.

Abstract: Machine unlearning aims to remove targeted knowledge from a trained model while preserving its general capabilities. For autoregressive language models, not all tokens in a forget sample are equally relevant to forgetting. Existing approaches either ignore this heterogeneity or rely on auxiliary models, heuristics, or external annotations to estimate each token's relevance for forgetting. We instead characterize it through the interaction with the retain objective: a token is forget-specific to the extent that minimizing the forget loss on that token does not conflict with retain optimality. We formalize this perspective as a joint optimization problem over the model parameters and the token weights and show that, under a natural separation condition, the resulting objective recovers the oracle forget-specific token support. Motivated by this formulation, we introduce Alternating Token-Weighted Unlearning (ATWU), a lightweight framework that jointly learns token forget-specificity and model parameters during unlearning using a simple linear scorer over the hidden states, without external token level supervision. Across TOFU and RWKU, ATWU achieves state of the art forget-retain trade-offs, outperforming sample-level methods, probability-based token weighting heuristics, and auxiliary-model-based approaches. Moreover, the learned scores align substantially better with ground truth forget-specific spans, indicating that ATWU identifies semantically meaningful token level forgetting signals. Overall, our results suggest that retain conflict provides an effective criterion for identifying what language models should forget, enabling unsupervised learning of token level forget-specificity directly from model representations with minimal computational overhead.

</details>


### [436] [PAMF: Prior-Aware Multimodal Fusion for Incomplete Time Series Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06328)
*Ziwen Kan,Wugeng Zheng,Tianlong Chen,Song Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents PAMF, a framework to address multimodal time-series tasks with missing data in healthcare, performing both imputation and prediction by considering distinct missing data patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of existing methods that fail to explicitly handle and learn from instance-specific missing data, particularly under distinct missingness patterns in multimodal time-series tasks in healthcare.

Method: The method, PAMF, leverages prior-aware flow matching and weight sharing to handle distinct missingness patterns. It initializes flow-matching states with type-specific priors and integrates imputation with prediction through architecturally aligned encoders.

Result: PAMF outperforms existing baselines in downstream prediction tasks on multiple healthcare time-series datasets with diverse missing data settings.

Conclusion: The framework effectively addresses distinct missingness patterns in multimodal healthcare time series by coupling imputation with prediction, resulting in superior performance across benchmarks.

Abstract: In healthcare, multimodal time series tasks often operate on incomplete observations in practice, for example when ECG segments are lost because electrodes detach or an entire respiratory channel is unavailable during overnight monitoring. Such missingness typically appears in two structurally distinct patterns: within-modality missing, where values are absent within an otherwise observed modality, and modality-level missing, where an entire modality is unavailable. Existing methods typically represent unobserved data implicitly through masks or missing embeddings, without learning instance-specific missing information, and most are designed for only one missingness pattern. A natural approach is to explicitly estimate the missing data; however, existing imputation methods treat missingness uniformly despite their different structural priors, and the imputation process is often isolated from downstream tasks, preventing downstream tasks from guiding imputation toward more informative representations. To address these limitations, we present PAMF, a multimodal time-series framework that explicitly handles different missingness patterns while coupling imputation with downstream prediction through prior-aware flow matching and weight sharing. Specifically, the method initializes the flow-matching source state with type-specific priors to distinguish two missing types. It further connects imputation and classification through architecturally matched encoders with weight sharing, transferring task-relevant representations into the imputation process. Experiments on multiple multimodal healthcare time-series benchmarks show that the proposed method achieves the strongest overall downstream performance across diverse datasets and missing settings compared with existing baselines.

</details>


### [437] [Subspace-Aware Sparse Autoencoders for Effective Mechanistic Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06333)
*Seyed Arshan Dalili,Mehrdad Mahdavi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies and addresses the limitation of traditional sparse autoencoders (SAEs), which assume features in models are one-dimensional, leading to distorted feature representations. The proposed Subspace-Aware Sparse Autoencoders (SASA) enhance interpretability and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the assumption in standard sparse autoencoders (SAEs) that model features are one-dimensional, which leads to fragmentation of features and hampers the interpretability of large language models (LLMs).

Method: The paper introduces Subspace-Aware Sparse Autoencoders (SASA), which replace single-direction decoders with learned decoder subspaces, enforce block sparsity via a Top-s group gating mechanism, and adapt effective rank with nuclear-norm regularization. This approach ensures that multi-dimensional features are represented cohesively rather than fragmented.

Result: The results demonstrate that SASA reduces feature splitting and absorption, improves the interpretability (monosemanticity) of latent representations, and performs as well as or better than standard SAEs with approximately half the training token cost.

Conclusion: Subspace-Aware Sparse Autoencoders (SASA) overcome the limitations of traditional SAEs by enabling multi-dimensional feature representation, significantly improving both computational efficiency and mechanistic interpretability in large language models.

Abstract: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) are widely used for mechanistic interpretability in large language models, yet their formulation assigns each latent feature a single decoder direction, implicitly assuming features to be one-dimensional.
