Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper evaluates the Physics-Informed Neural Networks' (PINNs) structural conflict using Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) to quantify effective degrees of freedom (d_eff) and introduces efficient subspace projection strategies for boundary adaptations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PINNs face task interference due to the shared parameter space, making it challenging to balance differential equations and boundary conditions. This necessitates a quantitative and structural understanding of the PINNs' limitations to improve their efficiency.

Method: The authors use the Fisher Information Matrix to define $d_{eff}$ as the measure of parameter directions unconstrained by differential operators. They provide mathematical analysis showing its convergence properties, propose it as a diagnostic, and introduce subspace projection techniques for efficient boundary condition adaptation.

Result: The analysis demonstrates $d_{eff}$ as a structural invariant for finite-dimensional kernels, and as a measure of bandwidth for infinite-dimensional kernels. Subspace projection methods are shown to allow quick adaptations to boundary changes, performing competitively against gradient-based tuning while being faster.

Conclusion: Quantifying $d_{eff}$ offers insights into the structural interplay between physics and boundary constraints in PINNs. Subspace projection strategies effectively address adaptation challenges, paving the way for more robust and efficient approaches to deploying PINNs.

Abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Networks inherently suffer from task interference because they rely on a shared parameter space to satisfy both governing differential equations and boundary conditions. We analyze this structural conflict using the Fisher Information Matrix to quantify the effective degrees of freedom ($d_{eff}$) in a physics-constrained model. Unlike the classical $d_{eff}$ which measures how many parameter directions are informed by data against a statistical prior, our $d_{eff}$ measures the dimension of the parameter directions unconstrained by the differential operator. For operators with finite-dimensional kernel, we show that $d_{eff}$ converges to the kernel dimension exactly, independent of network width, depth, or activation function, recasting it from a fit diagnostic into a structural invariant of the underlying continuous operator. For operators with infinite-dimensional kernel, $d_{eff}$ instead measures the network's finite-dimensional representational bandwidth for that kernel rather than recovering an integer invariant. Importantly, $d_{eff}$ also serves as an a priori structural diagnostic. Driving $d_{eff}$ of a well-posed problem to zero certifies that the physics and boundary constraints have absorbed the network's free directions. Building on this characterization, we introduce subspace projection strategies for boundary adaptation. Rather than retraining from scratch, we project parameter updates into the null space of the pre-trained physics operator so that new boundary conditions are satisfied without disturbing the learned physics. Gradient-based fine-tuning can match or exceed this but needs more wall-clock time and tuning, whereas subspace projection delivers near-equivalent quality in seconds to minutes. We validate on linear and nonlinear operators, demonstrating accurate adaptation to initial and boundary shifts and unencountered constraint types.

</details>


### [553] [Diffusion Models Observe Only Gradients: A Geometric Perspective on Score Matching Errors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06179)
*Naïl B. Khelifa,Richard E. Turner,Ramji Venkataramanan*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper shows that $L^2$ score error is not an intrinsic measure of marginal distributional quality in score-based diffusion models due to its decomposition into gradient and solenoidal components. Only the gradient component affects marginal dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the mismatch between $L^2$ score error minimization and actual distributional quality in score-based diffusion models, identifying meaningful theoretical insights and practical estimators.

Method: The authors decompose the $L^2$ score error into gradient and solenoidal components using Helmholtz-Hodge decomposition, derive theoretical results on bounds and estimators, and propose a novel Sobolev-based estimator correlated with sample quality.

Result: They proved the $L^2$ error cannot uniformly bound divergences, derived a tighter Kullback-Leibler divergence upper bound, and presented an estimator for the gradient component that better correlates with distribution quality.

Conclusion: The paper identifies the limitations of $L^2$ score error as a quality metric, introduces new theoretical foundations for understanding score-based models, and offers a practical tool for better evaluating sample quality.

Abstract: Score-based diffusion models are typically trained by minimizing the $L^2$ score matching error, and standard theoretical analyses rely on this quantity to bound the sampling discrepancy between the learned and target distributions. We show the $L^2$ score error is not the right intrinsic measure of marginal distributional quality: a learned diffusion model can incur arbitrarily large $L^2$ score error while perfectly matching the target distribution. By decomposing score errors into a gradient and a solenoidal component (a Helmholtz-Hodge decomposition), we identify the geometric reason behind this: only the gradient component enters the marginal Fokker-Planck dynamics, while the solenoidal component is structurally invisible. We make this precise in three results. First, building on the corrected geometry, we prove an impossibility result: no monotone function of the $L^2$ score error can uniformly lower bound any divergence between the learned and target distributions. Second, we derive an upper bound on the Kullback-Leibler divergence that depends only on the observable gradient component of the error, tightening the standard Girsanov bound and identifying its looseness as the cost of operating on path-space rather than marginal-space dynamics. Third, we give a tractable estimator of the gradient component via a dual Sobolev identity, which is shown to empirically correlate substantially better with sample quality than the full $L^2$ error.

</details>


### [554] [Anchor PCA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06233)
*Benedikt Seiter,Anya Fries,Julius von Kügelgen,Jonas Peters*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: Anchor PCA is an unsupervised dimension-reduction technique tailored for data across multiple domains, focusing on shared variation directions to provide robust embeddings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Principal component analysis (PCA) struggles with pooled data across multiple domains due to spurious high-variation directions from individual domains.

Method: Anchor PCA modifies the PCA target matrix to trade off explained variance for robust agreement between shared and domain-specific embeddings, achieving efficient computation.

Result: Anchor PCA successfully recovers a maximally invariant subspace and outperforms traditional PCA on unseen domain data in terms of explained variance.

Conclusion: Anchor PCA is a robust, efficient, and promising method for unsupervised dimension reduction in multi-domain data scenarios.

Abstract: Principal component analysis (PCA) is one of the most widely used unsupervised dimension reduction techniques. We study PCA for data from multiple related domains. Since principal components generally differ across domains, one way to obtain a shared low-rank embedding is to perform PCA on the pooled data. However, this approach can focus on spurious directions that exhibit high variation in only a few domains. To find a robust embedding that still explains most variance in unseen but similar domains, we propose instead to focus on shared directions of variation. To this end, we introduce Anchor PCA which trades off overall explained variance with agreement between the shared and domain-specific low-rank embeddings. Anchor PCA amounts to PCA on a modified target matrix and thus can be solved efficiently. Moreover, we show that Anchor PCA recovers a maximal invariant subspace and admits a minimax reconstruction interpretation under bounded domain-specific covariance inflations. On simulated and real-world gas sensor data with temporal drift, we demonstrate, respectively, that Anchor PCA recovers the maximally invariant subspace and yields embeddings that explain more variance on unseen domains than the pooling baseline and a worst-case alternative. Taken together, these findings establish Anchor PCA as a promising approach to robust unsupervised dimension reduction from multi-domain data.

</details>


### [555] [Discrete Causal Representations from Heterogeneous Domains: A Bayesian Approach with Social Survey Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06288)
*Ankur Garg,Michael Stettler,Aaron Schein,Julius von Kügelgen*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces a Bayesian method for learning interpretable causal representations from heterogeneous data by leveraging hierarchical models and sequential Monte Carlo sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in practical and uncertainty-aware methods for learning causal representations, especially when dealing with real-world heterogeneous data undergoing distribution shifts.

Method: Proposes a Bayesian framework featuring hierarchical models with priors tailored for causal assumptions and interpretability, complemented by sequential Monte Carlo sampling for posterior approximation.

Result: Applied case studies on social survey data show that the model infers meaningful high-level causal concepts and relationships among them, relevant to cultural or political factors across different environments.

Conclusion: The approach effectively learns causal representations for complex, real-world data, supporting its utility in cases like cultural and political analysis.

Abstract: Causal representation learning aims to infer the high-level latent causal concepts that give rise to observed low-level measurements. This is particularly relevant for heterogeneous data from different environments or domains since distribution shifts often arise through sparse, localized changes in some of the underlying causal mechanisms, while other parts of the generative process remain unchanged. Whereas identifiability of causal representations has been studied extensively, practical uncertainty-aware methods and real-world use cases remain less explored. In this work, we propose a Bayesian approach to learning causal representations from multi-environment data, focusing on the case of discrete causal concepts and unknown multi-node soft interventions. To this end, we translate causal assumptions and interpretability desiderata into suitable priors and parametric choices within a hierarchical model. We then devise an inference scheme based on sequential Monte Carlo sampling to approximate the resulting multimodal posterior. We showcase our approach through case studies on social survey data, where latent causal concepts correspond to cultural values or political opinions, measurements to survey responses, and environments to different countries or states. Our model infers meaningful high-level concepts and plausible causal relations among them, demonstrating its utility for learning causal representations of complex real-world data.

</details>


### [556] [Symmetric Divergence and Normalized Similarity: A Unified Topological Framework for Representation Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06342)
*Yan Wang,Tianyang Hu*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a unified topological toolkit to overcome limitations in current neural representation analysis methods, specifically addressing asymmetry and sample-size dependence in topological divergences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing topological divergences are hindered by heuristic asymmetry and produce scores that depend on sample sizes, making cross-scenario benchmarking unreliable.

Method: The authors propose two key advancements: (1) Symmetric Representation Topology Divergence (SRTD) and its efficient variant SRTD-lite, resolving asymmetry and providing precise diagnostics, and (2) Normalized Topological Similarity (NTS), offering a scale-invariant, bounded metric for standardized comparisons.

Result: Experiments reveal that the proposed methods outperform existing geometric measures, effectively capturing shifts in CNNs and mapping LLM genealogy under challenging conditions like distance saturation.

Conclusion: The toolkit provides a rigorous, topology-aware framework with precision and robustness, complementing existing methods like CKA for analyzing neural representations.

Abstract: Topological Data Analysis (TDA) offers a principled, intrinsic lens for comparing neural representations. However, existing paired topological divergences (e.g., RTD) are limited by heuristic asymmetry and, more critically, unbounded scores that depend on sample size, hindering reliable cross-scenario benchmarking. To address these challenges, we develop a unified topological toolkit serving two complementary needs: fine-grained structural diagnosis and robust, standardized evaluation. First, we complete the RTD framework by introducing Symmetric Representation Topology Divergence (SRTD) and its efficient variant SRTD-lite. Beyond resolving the theoretical asymmetry of prior variants, SRTD consolidates diagnostic information into a single, comprehensive cross-barcode signature. This allows for precise localization of structural discrepancies and serves as an effective optimization objective without the overhead of dual directional computations. Second, to enable reliable benchmarking across heterogeneous settings, we propose Normalized Topological Similarity (NTS). By measuring the rank correlation of hierarchical merge orders, NTS yields a scale-invariant metric bounded between -1 and 1, effectively overcoming the scale and sample-dependence of unnormalized divergences. Experiments across synthetic and real-world deep learning settings demonstrate that our toolkit captures functional shifts in CNNs missed by geometric measures and robustly maps LLM genealogy even under distance saturation, offering a rigorous, topology-aware perspective that complements measures like CKA.

</details>


### [557] [Function-Space Priors for Bayesian Neural ODEs with Application to Vessel Trajectory Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06351)
*Jaeyeong Lee,Wonmo Koo,Heeyoung Kim*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper addresses the challenges of vessel trajectory prediction by introducing a Bayesian Neural ODE approach with a GP-kernel-based prior for uncertainty quantification and handling irregular AIS trajectory data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to improve vessel trajectory prediction from AIS data, a critical task for maritime situational awareness, by addressing challenges like irregular sampling, missing data, and the need for accurate uncertainty estimation.

Method: The authors propose a Bayesian Neural ODE framework with a GP-kernel-based prior on the vector field. They integrate this with probabilistic multiple shooting to manage long and irregular trajectories while preserving global consistency.

Result: The proposed approach effectively incorporates structural priors into Neural ODEs, allowing for more robust trajectory modeling with uncertainty quantification under challenging AIS data conditions.

Conclusion: The study presents a practical and principled method for enhancing maritime trajectory prediction, combining the strengths of Bayesian Neural ODEs, GP priors, and probabilistic methods to address key challenges in the domain.

Abstract: Vessel trajectory prediction from Automatic Identification System (AIS) data is essential for maritime situational awareness, yet it remains challenging due to irregular sampling, missing reports, and complex dynamics. Beyond accurate point forecasts, maritime applications also demand well-calibrated uncertainty estimates for reliable decision-making. Bayesian Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) offer a principled framework for continuous-time trajectory modeling with uncertainty quantification by placing a prior over the neural vector field parameters. However, the commonly used isotropic Gaussian weight prior fails to encode informative structural properties of vessel dynamics, such as smoothness and locality. Existing function-space Bayesian neural network methods address this limitation for static mappings, but do not transfer directly to Neural ODEs, where the primary quantity of interest is the trajectory rather than the vector field itself. In principle, one could place a Gaussian process (GP) prior directly over ODE solutions, but this requires propagating distributions through a nonlinear ODE solver, which is analytically intractable. To address this challenge, we adopt a practical approach that imposes a GP-kernel-based prior directly on the vector field evaluated at a finite set of measurement points. Specifically, we augment the standard weight-space variational objective with a kernel-based regularizer that penalizes deviations of the vector field from the structure implied by a GP prior. To handle long and irregular AIS trajectories, we further combine this function-space regularization with probabilistic multiple shooting, which decouples inference across temporal segments while maintaining global consistency.

</details>


### [558] [Conformal Risk Sharing: Certified Cost Allocation with Participation Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06391)
*Ieva Kazlauskaite*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces a method called Conformal Risk Sharing to establish fair sharing of rare financial risks within a group. It minimizes extreme burdens while ensuring no individual is significantly disadvantaged.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the problem of equitably distributing rare adverse financial impacts among a group without causing harm to any participant, ensuring they remain part of the arrangement.

Method: Develops the Certified Allocation Problem framework, proposing Conformal Risk Sharing as a solution. This approach combines interpretable sharing policies and split conformal calibration, ensuring finite-data guarantees without distributional assumptions.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world data demonstrate that Conformal Risk Sharing significantly reduces extreme obligations for high-risk agents while managing potential harm to others.

Conclusion: Conformal Risk Sharing offers a practical, interpretable, and fair mechanism for redistributing rare adverse financial events among group participants, ensuring credible limits on individual obligations.

Abstract: Sharing the financial impact of rare adverse events across a group can soften extreme individual burdens, but any participant made worse off by the arrangement has reason to leave. A credible mechanism must therefore provide each agent with a trustworthy cap on their future obligation and should be deployed only if the aggregate harm across participants is bounded. We formalise this as the Certified Allocation Problem: from finite data and without distributional assumptions, find a redistribution rule, produce obligation caps for every participant, and verify that no participant is made materially worse off. We propose Conformal Risk Sharing, which solves this problem by pairing an interpretable sharing policy with split conformal calibration. The sharing intensity is tuned on training data, while held-out calibration data produces distribution-free per-agent guarantees (valid under exchangeability). Experiments on synthetic and real-world data, including precipitation and energy-cooperative data, confirm that the framework can substantially reduce extreme obligations for high-risk agents while controlling harm to others.

</details>


<div id='stat.ME'></div>

# stat.ME [[Back]](#toc)

### [559] [Wasserstein Exponential Smoothing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05560)
*Takuo Matsubara,Peiwen Jiang,Minh-Ngoc Tran,Wilson Ye Chen*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: The paper generalizes exponential smoothing (ES) to handle distributional time series using the Wasserstein space and demonstrates practical applications in finance and electricity demand forecasting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exponential smoothing has proven effective in traditional time series forecasting, but its extension to distributional time series was unexplored, which motivated the study.

Method: The authors developed a generalization of exponential smoothing using the Wasserstein space framework and introduced a methodology to estimate the smoothing parameter via minimizing Wasserstein distance.

Result: The proposed approach effectively forecasts distributional time series, demonstrated through theoretical validation and applications in financial returns and electricity demand data.

Conclusion: Wasserstein-based exponential smoothing retains the simplicity of classical ES while extending its application to distributional time series, offering a robust and practical forecasting tool.

Abstract: Exponential smoothing (ES) often outperforms other techniques in time series forecasting across a wide range of data-generating processes. While ES has traditionally been applied to time series in $\mathbb{R}$, this paper extends the methodology to distributional time series, where each observation is a probability distribution on $\mathbb{R}$. The primary contribution of this work is twofold. First, we propose a principled and intuitive generalization of ES within the Wasserstein space, which retains the exceptional parsimony of classical ES. Second, we theoretically and empirically demonstrate that the smoothing parameter can be consistently estimated by minimizing a Wasserstein distance. Applications to distributional time series of high-frequency financial returns and household electricity demands confirm the practical effectiveness of our Wasserstein ES model.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.CB'></div>

# q-bio.CB [[Back]](#toc)

### [560] [Quantifying the biophysical properties of stomatocytes in health and disease](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05227)
*Zhaojie Chai,Jianlu Zheng,He Li,Ming Dao,George Em Karniadakis*

Main category: q-bio.CB

TL;DR: The paper investigates the biomechanics of hereditary stomatocytosis (HS) in red blood cells, using simulations and microfluidic imaging to explain the splenectomy paradox and propose pre-operative risk stratification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to resolve the paradoxical outcomes of splenectomy in overhydrated and dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis by analyzing RBC biomechanics using innovative methods.

Method: The researchers utilized dissipative particle dynamics simulations and microfluidic imaging, constructing models of RBCs with varying volumes and analyzing their behavior across five orthogonal mechanical assays.

Result: The study found that interendothelial-slit traversal and blood viscosity are significantly influenced by RBC geometry and rigidity, with a microfluidic device demonstrating stark separations among RBC types.

Conclusion: The findings provide insight into the mechanics of HS, offer an explanation for the splenectomy paradox, and suggest microfluidic tools for pre-operative risk assessments in HS patients.

Abstract: Hereditary stomatocytosis (HS) comprises red blood cell (RBC) disorders characterized by cup-shaped erythrocytes that respond oppositely to splenectomy: curative in overhydrated HS (OHS) but potentially thrombogenic in dehydrated HS (DHS/xerocytosis). This paradox persists because RBC biomechanics is governed by partly independent parameters--shear modulus, bending rigidity, surface-to-volume ratio (S/V), and cytoplasmic viscosity--that existing assays capture only piecemeal. Here we combine dissipative particle dynamics (DPD) simulations with microfluidic imaging to construct a control discocyte and three stomatocyte models (ST-RBC1-3) at fixed membrane area and decreasing volume (109.7, 101.5, 89.8 fL), spanning the OHS-to-DHS range. Tracing this parameter set through five mechanically orthogonal assays, we find that interendothelial-slit (IES) traversal is geometry-dominated: overhydrated ST-RBC1 requires an order of magnitude higher critical pressure than healthy RBCs, whereas dehydrated ST-RBC3 passes freely. ST-RBC3 nonetheless suppresses membrane tank-treading and raises low-shear whole-blood viscosity by ~29% at physiological haematocrit, comparable to Gaucher-disease hyperviscosity. A funnel-obstacle chip amplifies these differences into a label-free centerline-offset signal predicted to separate all four RBC types (~4.5 standard deviations between extreme phenotypes). These results unite single-cell mechanics, splenic filtration, and hemorheology in one framework, resolve the splenectomy paradox, and point toward microfluidic pre-operative risk stratification in HS.

</details>


<div id='nlin.CD'></div>

# nlin.CD [[Back]](#toc)

### [561] [Uncovering Extreme Event Mechanisms for Prediction and Control with Sensitivity-Balanced Projections](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05618)
*Nicholas Zolman,Sajeda Mokbel,Samuel E. Otto,Steven L. Brunton*

Main category: nlin.CD

TL;DR: The paper presents a new technique to understand, forecast, and control extreme events in chaotic systems using interpretable sensitivity mechanisms and neural-network-assisted surrogate models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Characterizing and predicting extreme events in chaotic systems is challenging due to the subtle instability mechanisms driving these events.

Method: The authors propose the CoBRAS method to identify sensitive linear projections for extreme events, bypass cumbersome adjoint calculations using backpropagation, and adapt the method for spatially localized phenomena.

Result: They showcase accurate forecasting and mechanism identification for extreme events in diverse systems, including turbulent flows, oscillator networks, and rogue waves, along with successful event suppression control laws.

Conclusion: The study highlights the effectiveness of CoBRAS and neural network surrogate models for understanding and intervening in extreme events in both theoretical and experimental systems.

Abstract: Extreme events -- such as earthquakes and coronal mass ejections -- are common in many chaotic dynamical systems, yet are difficult to characterize and predict due to the subtle instability mechanisms that drive them. In this work, we develop an interpretable technique that reveals the underlying mechanisms behind extreme events and uses them to build data-driven forecasts and intuitive event suppression controllers. In particular, we utilize the covariance balancing reduction using adjoint snapshots (CoBRAS) method to identify linear oblique projections that best capture the sensitivity of a quantity of interest and reconstruct the original state. Importantly, we bypass the need for cumbersome adjoint calculations, instead using backpropagation via modern automatically differentiable numerical frameworks. To accommodate spatially localized events, we also introduce a new variant of CoBRAS to obtain local sensitivity-balanced projections. We demonstrate the utility of this approach to characterize extreme events across a diverse set of challenging systems, including turbulent bursts of energy dissipation in the 2D Kolmogorov Flow, spontaneous synchronization in networks of coupled FitzHugh-Nagumo oscillators, and the localized formation of ocean rogue waves from a modified nonlinear Schrödinger equation. For each example, we show that our simple forecast models accurately predict extreme events and that the underlying mechanisms may be used to design control laws to prevent these events. Finally, we demonstrate that by learning a neural network surrogate model of the dynamics directly from data, we may extend this approach to experimental systems and systems that are not natively written in an automatically differentiable programming language.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [562] [Oklch+: A Three-Parameter Extension of Oklab for Improved Color Difference Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05255)
*Naoyuki Uchida*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The paper introduces Oklch+, an improvement over the Oklab color space that better approximates perceptual color differences, nearing CIEDE2000's accuracy using fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Oklab, widely used for color interpolation and design, lacks the color difference prediction accuracy of CIEDE2000.

Method: A three-parameter extension, Oklch+, applies transformations (power on L-axis, Naka-Rushton on C-axis) to the Oklab coordinates, enabling Euclidean distance to reflect perceptual differences.

Result: On the COMBVD dataset, Oklch+ achieves STRESS = 29.09, nearly matching CIEDE2000, with improved uniformity and accuracy compared to Oklab. Cross-validation further supports this.

Conclusion: Oklch+ enhances Oklab by providing near-CIEDE2000 performance in perceptual distance, with potential for improved applications in design and interpolation workflows.

Abstract: Oklab and its cylindrical representation Oklch are widely adopted in interpolation and design workflows as perceptually motivated color spaces, but their color difference prediction accuracy falls short of CIEDE2000. We propose Oklch+, a three-parameter extension of Oklab comprising a power transformation on the L-axis and a Naka-Rushton compression on the C-axis, with Euclidean distance computed in the resulting transformed Oklab coordinates. The Naka-Rushton function is bounded in [0,1], reflecting the saturating nature of chroma sensitivity at high colorimetric values. Evaluated on COMBVD -- 3,813 suprathreshold color difference pairs spanning six independent experimental datasets -- Oklch+ achieves STRESS = 29.09, closely matching CIEDE2000 (29.13; difference = 0.04), using only three parameters optimized against color difference data compared to approximately 17 for CIEDE2000. Cross-validation on a held-out BFD-P D65 subset (2,028 pairs) confirms generalization (STRESS = 26.14), with Oklch+ substantially outperforming Oklab (51.45) and achieving STRESS comparable to CIEDE2000 (24.12) on the held-out set. Improvement over Oklab (47.35) is confirmed across all six COMBVD sub-datasets. Because Oklch+ defines a coordinate system in which Euclidean distance approximates perceptual distance, linear interpolation in the transformed space offers substantially improved perceptual uniformity relative to Oklab. Current evaluation is limited to the sRGB-centered COMBVD dataset; validation in high-chroma regions with empirical observer-rated discrimination data remains future work.

</details>


<div id='cs.MM'></div>

# cs.MM [[Back]](#toc)

### [563] [GS-NFS: Bandwidth-adaptive Streaming of Dynamic Gaussian Splats and Point Clouds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05650)
*Rajrup Ghosh,Haodong Wang,Haoran Hong,Eduardo Pavez,Amartya Chaudhuri,Weiwu Pang,Harsha V. Madhyastha,Antonio Ortega,Ramesh Govindan*

Main category: cs.MM

TL;DR: This paper introduces GS-NFS, a GPU-accelerated method for compressing dynamic 3D Gaussian Splatting frames, achieving 1-2 orders of magnitude faster encoding and decoding with competitive compression performance and rendering quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the inefficiency and slowness of existing dynamic 3DGS compression methods, which hinder practical applications like 3D video streaming.

Method: GS-NFS employs GPU-based parallelization to optimize existing algorithms for encoding positions and attributes of Gaussians in frames.

Result: GS-NFS successfully provides 1-2 orders of magnitude speed improvement in compression/decompression while maintaining competitive rendering quality.

Conclusion: The method accelerates 3D video streaming technology, paving the way for real-time applications with both high fidelity and efficient compression.

Abstract: Dynamic 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) holds great promise as a 3D video streaming technology since it can represent complex 3D scenes with high fidelity. In this approach, every frame in a 3D video represents the environment as a collection of Gaussians with position and other attributes such as scale, rotation, opacity, and color. Frames capture fine details, permit views from any arbitrary perspective, but are an order of magnitude, or more, larger than 2D video frames. A line of recent work has explored how to compress dynamic 3DGS frames, but these approaches are often slow, in part because their compression techniques are not amenable to efficient acceleration. GS-NFS accelerates dynamic 3DGS compression and decompression on a GPU, to the point where it can encode and decode at full frame rate. It achieves this by developing novel GPU-based parallelizations of existing algorithms for encoding both positions and attributes of Gaussians. As a result, it is 1-2 orders of magnitude faster than the state-of-the-art in encoding and decoding a frame, while offering competitive compression performance and rendering quality.

</details>


### [564] [UNIVID: Unified Vision-Language Model for Video Moderation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05748)
*Kejuan Yang,Yizhuo Zhang,Mingyuan Du,Yue Zhang,Dixin Zheng,Kaili Zhao,Yang Xiao,Hanzhong Liang,Kenan Xiao*

Main category: cs.MM

TL;DR: This paper introduces UNIVID, a unified vision-language model for video moderation that generates interpretable, policy-aware captions and improves video moderation efficiency and accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges in traditional video moderation systems, which are fragmented, lack transparency, and struggle to provide fine-grained and interpretable reasoning for decision-making.

Method: The paper proposes a new vision-language model called UNIVID, trained on a specialized dataset combining human-refined labels and synthetic data to align with safety guidelines. UNIVID generates policy-aware captions for video moderation.

Result: UNIVID reduces violation leakage by 42.7% and overkill rates by 37.0%. It replaces over 1,000 policy-specific models, saving computational resources and reducing engineering maintenance complexity.

Conclusion: UNIVID effectively bridges the gap in scalable video moderation by enhancing interpretability, efficiency, and safety alignment, further supporting industrial-scale and cross-functional applications.

Abstract: Global-scale video moderation faces a dual challenge: the need for fine-grained multi-modal reasoning and the demand for interpretable outputs to support downstream enforcement. Traditional moderation systems often rely on fragmented black-box classifiers that are difficult to maintain and lack transparency. In this paper, we present UNIVID, a UNIfied VIsion-language model for video moDeration. Unlike standard classification models, UNIVID generates policy-aware captions that serve as an interpretable intermediate representation, enabling human-verifiable decisions and multi-task reusability. While existing open-source and commercial VLMs often suffer from safety-guardrail refusals and lack fine-grained policy alignment, we develop a specialized training data recipe that combines expert human-refined labels with synthetic data to align the model with our safety guidelines. By integrating UNIVID as the core captioner, we design a novel end-to-end video moderation system that reduces violation leakage by 42.7% and overkill rate by 37.0% relatively. Meanwhile, by replacing over 1,000 policy-specific models with a single UNIVID backbone, we recycled extensive computation resources while reducing engineering maintenance overhead. To our knowledge, this is one of the first reports of a high-efficiency captioning VLM successfully supporting industrial-scale moderation and cross-functional business.

</details>


### [565] [LLMCodec: Adapting Video Codecs for Efficient Weight Compression of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05861)
*Rui Wang,Yan Zhao,Li Song,Zhengxue Cheng*

Main category: cs.MM

TL;DR: This paper introduces LLMCodec, a method leveraging video codecs for compressing large language models, showcasing significant improvements in perplexity and task accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing model compression and quantization methods struggle with generalization across tensor types and depend on fine-tuning or calibration data. The authors propose leveraging video codecs, which are inherently suited for data compression and have well-developed implementations.

Method: The authors present LLMCodec, which integrates affine quantization with the VVC/H.266 video codec. They also compare various video codecs and encoding profiles to measure their impact on performance.

Result: LLMCodec demonstrates improved performance on different models. Specifically, it achieves a 1.5x reduction in perplexity and a 21% improvement in downstream task accuracy for LLaMA-3-8B at 2-bit precision.

Conclusion: Video codecs can serve as an effective and practical approach for compressing large language models, offering robustness and general applicability, as highlighted by the results of LLMCodec.

Abstract: The rapid development of large language models(LLMs) has led to remarkable advances in natural language processing. However, the increasing scale of these models introduces substantial challenges in terms of storage, transmission, and deployment. Though great efforts have been devoted to model compression and quantization, existing methods often rely on fine-tuning or calibration data, which exhibit limited generalization across different tensor types. In this paper, we argue that video codecs offer a promising solution for LLM compression, due to their inherent compatibility with matrix structured data, configurable compression strategies, and the availability of highly optimized, off-the-shelf implementations. Therefore, we present LLMCodec, a video codec-based LLM compression method that integrates affine quantization with the recent VVC/H.266 video codec. Beyond VVC, we further compare a range of video codecs and encoding profiles to evaluate their impact on compression performance. Experiments on different models demonstrate the robustness and generality of LLMCodec. Notably, on LLaMA-3-8B at 2-bit precision, LLMCodec reduces perplexity by over 1.5x and improves downstream task accuracy by 21% compared with the existing method.

</details>


<div id='cs.DL'></div>

# cs.DL [[Back]](#toc)

### [566] [MIRAI: Prediction and Generation of High-Impact Academic Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05443)
*Alex Li,Joseph Jacobson*

Main category: cs.DL

TL;DR: MIRAI is a deep learning model predicting paper impact, achieving high correlation metrics using title, abstract, and date inputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of identifying and synthesizing high-impact scientific work among the vast volume of publications.

Method: Developed and trained MIRAI on the arXiv academic graph, predicting 5-year PageRank and citation counts.

Result: MIRAI achieved Spearman's $ρ$ of 0.4686 for PageRank and 0.6192 for citation prediction in 2021 papers, and enhanced research idea generation.

Conclusion: MIRAI demonstrates strong predictive capabilities and practical applications in forecasting academic impact and guiding research ideation.

Abstract: The rapid pace of scientific publishing has made the identification and synthesis of high-impact work an increasingly urgent challenge. We introduce MIRAI (Multi-year Inference of Research trends and Academic Impact), a deep learning framework that predicts paper impact using only it's title, abstract, and publication date. We train MIRAI on the arXiv academic graph to predict 5-year PageRank and citation counts, achieving Spearman's $ρ$ of 0.4686 on PageRank prediction and 0.6192 on citation prediction for papers published in 2021. We propose a research ideation pipeline built on top of MIRAI that produces research ideas oriented towards high impact. These ideas were judged as more impactful than a baseline without MIRAI by an unbiased LLM judge at a 4:3 ratio. We make the 5-year citation prediction model publicly available at https://predict-paper-impact.vercel.app.

</details>


<div id='cs.CR'></div>

# cs.CR [[Back]](#toc)

### [567] [Bitcoin After Block Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05503)
*Junhyuk Lee*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper examines Bitcoin's long-term security by identifying conditions for dishonest mining as block rewards approach zero and evaluates mechanisms to mitigate such risks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address concerns about Bitcoin network security as miners transition from block rewards to relying solely on transaction fees.

Method: Analyzed miner behavior using a sequential decision model, identifying a deviation threshold ($G_t$) where honest mining ceases to be optimal. Examined three protocol mechanisms to mitigate deviations.

Result: Found that large-scale deviation is unlikely around the 2024 Bitcoin halving but may occur once block rewards vanish. Demonstrated protocol changes that can raise the deviation threshold and enhance security in a fee-only regime.

Conclusion: Combining protocol adjustments can help maintain Bitcoin's security even after block rewards are eliminated, offering a guideline for network sustainability.

Abstract: Bitcoin's block reward is scheduled to decline to zero, raising concerns about whether the network can remain secure once miners rely solely on transaction fees. This paper seeks to identify the conditions under which large-scale and persistent deviation from honest mining can arise.
  We analyze and compare the payoffs of honest and deviating miners in a sequential decision model, and identify a deviation threshold $G_t$ at which honest mining ceases to be privately optimal. Around the 2024 Bitcoin halving, we show that current mining behavior does not exhibit large-scale or structural deviation. However, when the block reward is removed, the $G_t$ criterion implies that deviation can arise even with a very small fraction of transaction fees.
  Finally, we evaluate three protocol-level mechanisms: Base Fee, Fee Floor, and an adaptive maximum block size rule, and show that their combination raises the deviation threshold and mitigates incentive breakdown in a fee-only regime. These results provide a practical benchmark for assessing Bitcoin's security as block rewards disappear.

</details>


### [568] [Willing but Unable: Separating Refusal from Capability in Code LLMs via Abliteration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05396)
*Cristina Carleo,Pietro Liguori,Naghmeh Ivaki,Domenico Cotroneo*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper explores a novel method, called abliteration, to overcome the refusal of instruction-tuned LLMs to generate code containing specified vulnerabilities, using SQL injections as a case study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the challenge of creating labeled vulnerable code for training learning-based vulnerability detection systems, as mined corpora and LLM-based augmentations often suffer from label noise.

Method: The authors use a low-rank weight edit method (abliteration) to modify LLMs, focusing on Python code and SQL injection vulnerabilities, and evaluate model refusal rates and injection capability across different model sizes.

Result: Abliteration significantly reduces refusal rates to zero or near-zero and preserves syntactic validity above 93%, while injection capability remains dependent on model parameters, with higher-sized models showing better capacity.

Conclusion: Abliteration can decouple an LLM's refusal to generate vulnerable code from its code-generation competence, providing a path to safely create labeled vulnerable datasets for research.

Abstract: Producing a labeled vulnerable code at scale is a recurring obstacle for learning-based vulnerability detection: mined corpora carry substantial label noise, and existing LLM-based augmentation propagates these inaccuracies because it transforms vulnerable seeds rather than synthesising vulnerabilities from a specification. A complementary route is to start from safe code and ask an instruction-tuned LLM to inject a specified CWE (which would shift the labeling burden from open-ended detection to bounded binary confirmation) but safety-aligned code LLMs systematically refuse such prompts. This paper is a preliminary feasibility study of abliteration, a low-rank weight edit that orthogonally projects out the refusal direction in the residual stream, as a tool to remove this barrier. We use Python and CWE-89 (SQL injection) as a case study, evaluating the Qwen2.5-Coder-Instruct family at 3B, 7B, and 14B parameters on safe samples drawn from PromSec and SafeCoder, replicated three times per condition. We find that (i) refusal on injection prompts is strongly size- and prompt-context-dependent: the 14B refuses 100% of prompts, the 7B refuses 73% of PromSec but only 5% of SafeCoder, whereas the 3B is essentially never blocked; (ii) abliteration reduces refusal to zero or near-zero across all sizes while leaving syntactic validity above 93%, supporting the view that, in this setting, refusal can be detached from measured code-generation capability; and (iii) the post-abliteration injection rate remains capacity-bound (88-97% on the 14B, 89-90% on the 7B, and 25-48% on the 3B) separating willingness, which abliteration unlocks, from capability, which scales with parameters. Vulnerability verdicts are produced by a three-tool detector ensemble (CodeQL, Semgrep, Bandit) followed by manual adjudication by two authors on detector-positive outputs.

</details>


### [569] [A formal framework for the economic security of DeFi compositions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05418)
*Massimo Bartoletti,Riccado Marchesin,Roberto Zunino*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces 'MEV non-interference' as a formal security framework to address vulnerabilities in DeFi composability due to adversarial interactions between newly deployed and existing smart contracts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to tackle the security risks in DeFi systems caused by the composability of smart contracts, which allows adversaries to cause economic losses through interactions between newly deployed and pre-existing contracts.

Method: The authors define 'local MEV' to measure economic attacks on specific victim contracts, study adversarial models with bounded and unbounded wealth, and propose sufficient conditions and locality principles for secure composability of DeFi systems.

Result: Using the proposed framework, the paper evaluates various DeFi compositions and distinguishes between secure and vulnerable setups, providing insight into economic security for exchanges, AMMs, lending pools, and more.

Conclusion: The framework establishes a formal foundation for reasoning about the security of DeFi systems, addressing key composability challenges and aiding in the identification of secure DeFi contract compositions.

Abstract: Decentralized Finance (DeFi) services are usually constructed by composing a variety of smart contracts. While composability is a key driver of the success of DeFi, it also creates security risks: adversaries may exploit interactions between newly deployed contracts and the pre-existing ones to inflict economic losses. We introduce MEV non-interference, a formal security notion for DeFi composability requiring that the maximal extractable value from a set of newly deployed contracts is not increased by interactions with the existing blockchain state. To support this notion, we define local MEV, a novel measure of economic attacks that focusses on the loss of a given set of victim contracts. We study two adversarial models, with bounded and unbounded wealth, and establish sufficient conditions and locality principles that enable modular reasoning about secure composability. We apply the framework to representative DeFi compositions, including exchanges, AMMs, options, lending pools, routers, and arbitrage contracts, showing how it distinguishes secure compositions from vulnerable ones. Our results provide a formal foundation for reasoning about the economic security of DeFi compositions.

</details>


### [570] [TinyML-Driven Cybersecurity for Autonomous Spacecraft: Latency-Accuracy Analysis for SPARTA RF and Cyber Threat Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05779)
*Van Le,Trevor Tran,Tan Le*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The study evaluates TinyML-compatible models for detecting cyber-RF threats in spacecraft, finding Logistic Regression to be a fast and effective option with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable autonomous spacecraft to efficiently detect and counter cybersecurity threats to RF communication, ensuring both rapid response and operational reliability.

Method: Analyzed several TinyML-compatible machine learning models (Random Forest, Logistic Regression, SVM, and MLP) on cyber-RF threat recognition tasks using theoretical and empirical methods, including physics-informed analysis and adversarial RF spectrogram testing.

Result: Logistic Regression outperforms other models in latency while maintaining high accuracy, providing microsecond-level inference with only a 1% accuracy drop compared to Random Forest.

Conclusion: Logistic Regression is a viable TinyML baseline for onboard spacecraft autonomy, with the study also highlighting directions for improvement through advanced feature encoders and learning architectures.

Abstract: Autonomous spacecraft require rapid, lightweight, and reliable onboard detection of cyber-RF threats. Using the SPARTA attack model, we analyze the latency-accuracy trade-offs of TinyML-compatible classical models -- Random Forest, Logistic Regression, SVM, and MLP -- for detecting uplink jamming, Fake-NR spoofing, payload manipulation, ground-segment compromise, and unauthorized command injection. We present a physics-informed theoretical analysis of each model's computational complexity, VC dimension, Lipschitz continuity, and latency scaling, supported by empirical measurements on adversarial RF spectrograms generated via BandErasure, FakeNR, and NoiseBurst corruption modes. Results show that Logistic Regression achieves microsecond-level inference with only a 1\% accuracy drop relative to Random Forest, making it an effective TinyML baseline for onboard autonomy. The study also identifies opportunities for advancing spacecraft cybersecurity through richer feature encoders and multi-timescale learning architectures, building on recent progress in edge intelligence and trustworthy AI.

</details>


### [571] [Domain-Conditioned Safety in Frontier Computer-Using Agents: A 793-Episode Browser Benchmark, a Coding-Domain Cross-Reference, and a Reproducibility Audit of Recent Red-Teaming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05233)
*Nicholas Saban*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper evaluates the effectiveness of hand-crafted prompt-injection attacks on current frontier computer-using agents (CUAs), revealing that attack success rates (ASR) drop significantly compared to prior studies focusing on outdated or vulnerable models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether previously reported high ASR results from prompt-injection attacks remain valid against current, state-of-the-art CUAs and to assess the generalizability of safety mechanisms across different domains.

Method: The researchers introduced CUA-HandCrafted, a benchmark covering 24 multi-step tasks, 56 attack templates, 8 families of attacks, and 4 system-prompt configurations. They tested against Claude Sonnet 4.6 and GPT-5.4 to measure susceptibility.

Result: The models were resistant to multi-step attacks, achieving a 0/140 success rate with an upper confidence bound of 2.60%. However, the same models were highly vulnerable in a different domain-focused benchmark, SkillBench, with up to 100% ASR for skill-injection attacks.

Conclusion: The study suggests that high ASRs in earlier literature are largely due to RL-optimized attack text rather than inherent vulnerabilities in attack categories. Current safety measures are model and domain-specific, which limits result reproducibility across different domains.

Abstract: Recent computer-using-agent (CUA) red-teaming papers report prompt-injection attack success rates (ASR) of 42-98%, but these headline numbers cluster on retired models and on the most-vulnerable model in each paper's panel. We ask whether those techniques, reproduced as hand-crafted templates, still work against current frontier CUAs. We release CUA-HandCrafted, a public benchmark of 793 episodes spanning 24 multi-step web tasks, 56 attack templates, 8 attack families, and 4 system-prompt configurations. Against Claude Sonnet 4.6 and GPT-5.4 we measure 0/140 multi-step attack success (Clopper-Pearson 95% upper bound 2.60%); a prompt ablation shows this resistance lives in the model weights. Yet it does not generalize: on a sister coding-agent benchmark (SkillBench), the same weights fall to hand-crafted skill-injection at up to 100%. We argue that the literature's high ASR is largely attributable to RL-optimized injection text rather than the attack categories, and that frontier safety hardening is domain-conditioned, specific to the heavily-targeted browser surface. Reporting techniques without releasing the optimized strings, or extrapolating browser-domain safety to other CUA modalities, makes published ASR numbers unreproducible.

</details>


### [572] [An Embarrassingly Simple Detector for Model Extraction Attacks in Large Language Model API Traffic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05725)
*Shuze Liu,Qianwen Guo,Yushun Dong*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper proposes a simple but effective method for detecting model extraction attempts on hosted Large Language Model (LLM) APIs, achieving strong empirical results compared to existing baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: With the increasing deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) through hosted APIs, there’s a rising threat of model extraction, which jeopardizes model ownership and service security. Traditional methods fall short in accurately detecting extraction attempts due to their focus on single-query anomalies or simplistic attacker settings, leaving a demand for more robust detection mechanisms.

Method: The paper introduces a detector based on traffic-window distribution testing using Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD). Incoming API queries are embedded in a semantic space, and their aggregate distribution is tested against historical benign traffic without any direct attacker profiling. The decision threshold is calibrated only on benign-vs-benign data.

Result: In evaluations across fourteen attacker-normal query pairs and in four extraction scenarios, the proposed MMD detector achieved excellent detection performance, including 0.3% false positive rate for benign traffic, 100% true positive rate for purely attacker traffic, 90.5% average true positive rate for mixed attacker-traffic settings, and 95.1% balanced accuracy.

Conclusion: The results validate the robustness of benign-calibrated distribution testing as an effective method for detecting model extraction attempts in LLM API traffic. This approach sets a new empirical baseline for detection systems and is suitable for both user-level and mixed traffic settings, offering a practical solution for safeguarding LLM APIs.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed through hosted APIs, making model extraction a practical threat to model ownership and service security. However, individual extraction queries often resemble benign requests, and existing evaluations often focus on single-query anomaly scoring or pure benign-versus-attacker user settings. We formulate model extraction monitoring as benign-calibrated traffic-window distribution testing and show that an embarrassingly simple detector is effective: embed incoming queries into a semantic space and test whether their aggregate distribution deviates from historical benign traffic. We instantiate the detector with maximum mean discrepancy (MMD), using only benign-vs-benign comparisons to set the decision threshold. We evaluate on fourteen attacker-normal query pairs from four extraction scenarios and compare with adapted PRADA, SEAT, CAP, DATE, and marginal Mahalanobis baselines. Across three random seeds, MMD achieves 0.3% benign FPR, 100.0% pure-attacker TPR, 90.5% average TPR over attacker fractions, and 95.1% balanced accuracy. These results show that benign-calibrated distribution testing is a strong empirical baseline for model extraction detection in both user-level and mixed multi-user LLM API traffic. Code is released at: https://github.com/LabRAI/mmd-llm-mea-detection.

</details>


### [573] [Membrane: A Self-Evolving Contrastive Safety Memory for LLM Agent Defense](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05743)
*Minseok Choi,Seungbin Yang,Dongjin Kim,Subin Kim,Jungmin Son,Yunseung Lee,Jaegul Choo,Youngjun Kwak*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Membrane introduces Contrastive Safety Memory (CSM) as a self-evolving guardrail for improving the adaptability and precision of large language models against jailbreak attacks by pairing harmful queries with benign counterparts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety classifiers and adaptive guardrails are unable to effectively manage evolving jailbreak attacks without over-refusing benign requests, introducing a gap in robust safety enforcement.

Method: Membrane uses Contrastive Safety Memory (CSM) cells that dynamically index harmful queries and their benign counterparts without retraining, enabling adaptive and content-specific safety decisions.

Result: Membrane achieves top safety performance on datasets such as HarmBench and AgentHarm, maintaining low benign refusal rates (7-14%) while achieving high F1 scores (87-88%) under cross-attack conditions and resilience against memory poisoning.

Conclusion: Membrane's self-evolving approach enhances model-level and agent-level safety, offering precise threat handling without sacrificing benign interaction quality, making it a robust option for evolving security challenges.

Abstract: Despite advances in safety alignment, large language models remain vulnerable to continuously evolving jailbreaks. Existing fine-tuned safety classifiers cannot adapt to these evolving attacks, while adaptive memory-based guardrails tend to over-refuse benign queries that resemble stored attacks. We propose Membrane, a self-evolving guardrail built on Contrastive Safety Memory (CSM): each cell pairs the conditions for blocking a harmful query with those for permitting a superficially similar benign request. Without retraining, Membrane evolves CSM by distilling each harmful interaction and its benign counterpart into a contrastive cell indexed by the underlying attack strategy, so that one cell generalizes across topical variants of the same mechanism. At inference, retrieved cells serve as grounding context for precise safety decisions. Across model-level safety on HarmBench and agent-level safety on AgentHarm, Membrane achieves the highest F1 on all six jailbreak attacks. Notably, benign refusal on AgentHarm stays at 7-14%, well below the 28-85% range of prior guards. Memory cells also retain 87-88% F1 under cross-attack transfer and remain stable under memory poisoning.

</details>


### [574] [Search-Time Contamination in Deep Research Agents: Measuring Performance Inflation in Public Benchmark Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05241)
*Yongjie Wang,Xinyue Zhang,Kunhong Yao,Zhiwei Zeng,Kaisong Song,Jun Lin,Zhiqi Shen*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper highlights the issue of Search-Time Contamination (STC) in evaluating LLM reasoning with deep research agents that use web search, leading to inflated performance metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the issue of unfair and deceptive performance evaluations of deep research agents caused by the retrieval of benchmark metadata or answers during evaluation.

Method: The researchers define three levels of STC contamination, develop detection algorithms to identify these contaminations, and evaluate the contamination’s impact on six public benchmarks.

Result: They demonstrate that STC is widespread and can inflate performance by up to 4%, suggesting evaluations often overestimate true reasoning capabilities.

Conclusion: The paper suggests adopting contamination-aware practices such as isolated sandboxes, transparent search trajectory logs, and controlled benchmark access to ensure integrity in evaluations.

Abstract: Public benchmarks enable fair and reproducible evaluation of LLM reasoning, but they become fragile for deep research agents that actively search the web during inference. Such agents may retrieve public benchmark metadata, question context, or even ground-truth answers via web search. This gives rise to Search-Time Contamination (STC), where external retrieval bypasses intended reasoning and inflates measured performance. We systematically study STC in deep research agent evaluation. We define three contamination types with increasing severity, namely Benchmark Metadata Leakage, Question-Context Leakage, and Explicit Answer Leakage, and develop detection algorithms to identify them and quantify their impact on agent performance. Evaluating modern deep research agents on six public benchmarks, we find that STC is widespread and can inflate performance by up to 4%. Our findings show that existing evaluations may overestimate true reasoning ability. We therefore advocate contamination-aware practices, including isolated sandboxes, transparent search trajectories, and controlled benchmark access.

</details>


### [575] [From Attack Simulation to SIEM Rule: Deterministic Detection-as-Code Synthesis with Probe-Level Traceability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05252)
*Alexandre Cristovão Maiorano*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This study explores automating the creation of Sigma detection rules from Breach-and-Attack Simulation (BAS) tool findings using a deterministic synthesis function and locked corpora, enabling reproducible and typed rule generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Today, creating detection rules for SIEM systems based on BAS findings requires manual effort, making the process time-consuming and error-prone. Automating this task can significantly streamline security operations while maintaining reliability and reproducibility.

Method: The authors developed a deterministic synthesis function that maps findings from locked attack simulation corpora to starter Sigma rules using a small library of templates, with back-references to their originating probes and related MITRE ATT&CK techniques.

Result: The system generated valid Sigma rules for all findings in two locked corpora, which successfully converted to Splunk and Elasticsearch-compatible formats. Testing showed the rules were reliable with a 30% and 14% success rate on held-out datasets for LLM and Web corpora respectively, with minimal false positives.

Conclusion: The proposed system offers a reproducible approach to partially automate Sigma rule generation from BAS findings, prioritizing traceability and accuracy over the breadth of generative methods. This innovation simplifies the leap from simulation to detection deployment.

Abstract: Security teams routinely simulate attacks against their own systems to check whether their monitoring would catch a real intruder. These Breach-and-Attack-Simulation (BAS) tools surface findings, but the security information and event management (SIEM) systems that watch production need detection rules -- and today a human bridges that gap by hand, reading each finding and writing the corresponding Sigma rule (a vendor-neutral detection format). We show this translation can be partially automated when probes are drawn from a locked corpus, so each finding carries a stable identifier back to the originating probe. We describe a deterministic synthesis function that maps each finding to a starter Sigma rule through a small template library (N=23, indexed by categories from the OWASP LLM and Web Top 10), with a back-reference to the originating finding and its MITRE ATT&CK technique. On two locked corpora (17-probe LLM, 23-probe Web), every bypassed-probe finding yields a starter rule, and all 17/17 emitted rules parse and convert to Splunk and Elasticsearch backends. Replayed through a live OpenSearch SIEM, the LLM rules fire on 30% of a held-out AdvBench subset and 14% of HarmBench at 7.7% false positives on a benign baseline; the Web side is validated structurally, not against a held-out attack set. The contribution is a verifiable, byte-stable path from BAS finding to operator-deployable starter rule, re-derivable from the published corpus and template library alone -- trading the breadth of LLM-generative methods for exact reproducibility and a typed traceback from any fired alert to the originating probe.

</details>


### [576] [SlotGCG: Exploiting the Positional Vulnerability in LLMs for Jailbreak Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05609)
*Seungwon Jeong,Jiwoo Jeong,Hyeonjin Kim,Yunseok Lee,Woojin Lee*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: SlotGCG introduces a novel method to identify vulnerable insertion points (slots) within prompts for more effective jailbreak attacks against large language models, outperforming prior optimization-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limited exploration of positional vulnerabilities in jailbreak attacks, as prior approaches focused on fixed token insertion points within prompts.

Method: Development of Vulnerable Slot Score (VSS) to quantify slot vulnerability and SlotGCG, which evaluates all slots with VSS to select the most vulnerable points and optimize attack success, functioning as an attack-agnostic plug-in.

Result: SlotGCG achieves superior performance with a 14% higher Attack Success Rate (ASR) than GCG-based methods, faster convergence, and 42% higher ASR robustness against defenses.

Conclusion: The SlotGCG enhances the efficiency and effectiveness of jailbreak attacks on LLMs by strategically determining vulnerable prompt positions, offering a significant improvement over existing techniques.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) are widely deployed, identifying their vulnerability through jailbreak attacks becomes increasingly critical. Optimization-based attacks like Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) have focused on inserting adversarial tokens to the end of prompts. However, GCG restricts adversarial tokens to a fixed insertion point (typically the prompt suffix), leaving the effect of inserting tokens at other positions unexplored. In this paper, we empirically investigate \emph{slots}, i.e., candidate positions within a prompt where tokens can be inserted. We find that vulnerability to jailbreaking is highly related to the selection of the \emph{slots}. Based on these findings, we introduce the \textit{Vulnerable Slot Score} (VSS) to quantify the positional vulnerability to jailbreaking. We then propose SlotGCG, which evaluates all slots with VSS, selects the most vulnerable slots for insertion, and runs a targeted optimization attack at those slots. Our approach provides a position-search mechanism that is attack-agnostic and can be plugged into any optimization-based attack, adding only 200ms of preprocessing time. Experiments across multiple models demonstrate that SlotGCG significantly outperforms existing methods. Specifically, it achieves 14\% higher Attack Success Rates (ASR) over GCG-based attacks, converges faster, and shows superior robustness against defense methods with 42\% higher ASR than baseline approaches. Our implementation is available at \href{https://github.com/youai058/SlotGCG}{https://github.com/youai058/SlotGCG}

</details>


### [577] [Dimensionality Reduction for Cyberattack Classification: A Comparative Evaluation of PCA and Linear Predictive Coding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05584)
*Nelly Elsayed,Zag ElSayed,Navid Asadizanjani*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper investigates feature compression in cyberattack detection and compares PCA and LPC, finding PCA performs well even with aggressive compression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-dimensional features in cyberattack detection increase computational overhead, limiting usability in resource-constrained scenarios. Reducing dimensionality is critical for efficiency.

Method: The study compared PCA and LPC for feature compression and examined their impact on classification models using experimental analysis.

Result: PCA maintained classification performance during aggressive compression, while LPC resulted in slightly larger degradation but remained competitive.

Conclusion: Substantial feature dimensionality reduction is possible with minimal effect on accuracy, enabling efficient cybersecurity analytics.

Abstract: High-dimensional feature representations are widely used in machine learning-based cyberattack detection systems. However, they increase computational complexity and may hinder deployment in resource-constrained environments. In this paper, we investigate feature compression techniques for cyberattack classification by comparing two dimensionality reduction approaches: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Linear Predictive Coding (LPC). Compressed feature representations with varying dimensionalities are generated and evaluated across several classification models. Experimental analysis demonstrates that PCA preserves classification performance even under aggressive compression. On the other hand, LPC provides competitive predictive representations with slightly larger performance degradation. The results show that substantial reductions in feature dimensionality can be achieved with minimal impact on classification accuracy, highlighting the potential of lightweight feature compression for efficient cybersecurity analytics.

</details>


### [578] [Hybrid CNN-LSTM Framework for Intelligent Cyber Attack Detection and Prevention in U.S. Critical Digital Infrastructure: A Comparative Machine Learning Evaluation on CSE-CIC-IDS2018](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05714)
*Md. Iqbal Hossan,Md. Serajul Kabir Chowdhury Rubel,Md. Arifur Rahman,B. M. Taslimul Haque*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This study develops an AI-powered smart cyber-defense system using machine learning models to improve intrusion detection against advanced cyber threats.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the growing exposure to sophisticated cyber threats in critical U.S. sectors due to rapid digital infrastructure expansion, and limitations of traditional signature-based cybersecurity systems.

Method: The approach involves leveraging AI/ML algorithms and the CSE-CIC-IDS2018 dataset. It uses diverse models like Random Forest, XGBoost, CNN, and LSTM for identifying malicious activity, with processes including preprocessing, feature engineering, and automated threat prevention.

Result: The proposed system demonstrates the integration of advanced ML techniques for real-time monitoring and intelligent threat classification.

Conclusion: AI and ML-based systems provide a promising solution for overcoming the shortcomings of traditional cybersecurity methods and can significantly enhance detection and defense against evolving cyber threats.

Abstract: Digital infrastructure is growing at a rapid pace in the United States, and as a result, exposure to advanced cyber threats to critical sectors including healthcare, finance, transportation, energy and government systems is growing. The traditional cybersecurity approaches, including signature-based intrusion detection systems, have become less effective against today's cyber attacks, as they are unable to detect unknown and changing attacks in real time. To overcome these constraints, this research suggests a smart cyber-defense system, which utilizes Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) algorithms in the detection and prevention of cyber attacks in the U.S. digital infrastructure. This study uses the CSE-CIC-IDS2018 dataset, which is a realistic network traffic dataset, along with various cyber attack scenarios, including Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS), brute force attacks, botnets, infiltration attacks, and web-based attacks. A number of machine learning and deep learning models such as Random Forest, XGBoost, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks are implemented and evaluated to be used in identifying malicious network behavior and boosting the accuracy of intrusion detection. The framework proposed combines data preprocessing, feature engineering, real-time traffic monitoring, intelligent threat classification with automated prevention mechanisms to build cybersecurity resilience. E

</details>


### [579] [An Improved CNN-LSTM Based Intrusion Detection System for IoT Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05776)
*Mohammad Tariq Ikhlas,Pohanyar Khowaja Khil,Malik Muhammad Mueed Aslam,Muhammad Khuram Shahzad*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces an improved CNN-LSTM model for intrusion detection in IoT networks, achieving high accuracy of approximately 97% by combining spatial and temporal feature learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rapid growth of IoT devices has led to heightened security concerns, necessitating effective intrusion detection systems for network protection.

Method: The paper employs a CNN-LSTM based approach with multi-class classification, dataset integration, and temporal feature learning to enhance detection performance.

Result: The proposed model achieves an accuracy of roughly 97% and effectively detects multiple categories of network attacks.

Conclusion: Integrating convolutional and recurrent neural networks enables robust spatial and temporal analysis of IoT network traffic, significantly enhancing intrusion detection capabilities.

Abstract: With the rapid proliferation of IoT devices, security concerns have dramatically escalated and intrusion detection systems have become critical for protecting networked environments. This paper presents an improved CNN-LSTM based intrusion detection model that combines multi-class classification, dataset integration, and temporal feature learning to enhance detection performance in IoT networks. Using network traffic data, the proposed approach is evaluated on intrusion detection tasks and achieves an accuracy of approximately 97%. Experimental results demonstrate that the model effectively detects multiple attack categories while maintaining stable training and validation performance. The integration of convolutional and recurrent neural network components enables the framework to capture both spatial and temporal characteristics of network traffic, improving overall intrusion detection capability in IoT environments.

</details>


### [580] [Cognitive Threat Intelligence and Explainable Federated Security Analytics for distributed Infrastructure Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05701)
*Md. Arifur Rahman,B. M. Taslimul Haque,Md. Iqbal Hossan,Md. Serajul Kabir Chowdhury Rubel*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper presents a privacy-preserving cybersecurity framework for distributed systems, combining Federated Learning, Explainable AI, and advanced threat detection techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current centralized approaches to intrusion detection face challenges such as scalability, data privacy, and communication overhead, especially in distributed infrastructure systems like IoT and edge computing setups.

Method: The study introduces a decentralized system where Federated Learning trains local security models on distributed nodes. Only encrypted model parameters are shared, and Explainable AI methods are used alongside cognitive analytics and algorithms like Random Forest and Autoencoders for threat detection.

Result: The proposed framework enhances privacy, reduces reliance on centralized systems, and introduces intelligent methods for distributed cyber threat detection.

Conclusion: The framework addresses critical cybersecurity challenges, offering a scalable, transparent, and privacy-focused solution for protecting distributed infrastructure environments.

Abstract: The increasing adoption of distributed infrastructure systems, cloud computing, Internet of Things (IoT) technologies, and edge-based architectures has significantly expanded the cybersecurity attack surface and introduced increasingly sophisticated cyber threats. Conventional centralized intrusion detection approaches often face challenges related to scalability, data privacy, communication overhead, and limited transparency in artificial intelligence-driven decision-making processes. To address these limitations, this study proposes a Cognitive Threat Intelligence and Explainable Federated Security Analytics framework for distributed infrastructure systems. The proposed framework integrates Federated Learning (FL), Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI), and cognitive cybersecurity analytics to enable collaborative and privacy-preserving cyber threat detection across distributed network environments. Instead of transmitting sensitive raw network traffic data to centralized servers, local security models are independently trained at distributed nodes, where only encrypted model parameters and updates are shared through a federated aggregation mechanism. This decentralized learning architecture improves privacy protection while reducing communication dependency and centralized security risks. To enhance intelligent threat analysis, the framework incorporates machine learning and deep learning algorithms including Random Forest, XGBoost, Autoencoder

</details>


### [581] [Explainable AI-Driven Cyber Risk Analytics and Model Reliability Assessment for Intelligent Governance of U.S. Critical Infrastructure: An XGBoost and SHAP-Based Intrusion Detection Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05710)
*B. M. Taslimul Haque,Md. Arifur Rahman,Md. Serajul Kabir Chowdhury Rubel,Md. Iqbal Hossan*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This study develops a machine learning-based cyber risk analytics framework to improve the resilience and reliability of systems within the U.S. critical infrastructure. It incorporates explainable AI techniques for better transparency and decision-making.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
