Motivation: Growing cyber threats like DDoS attacks and ransomware jeopardize the security, resilience, and trustworthiness of critical U.S. infrastructure that is increasingly reliant on AI-driven governance and automated systems.

Method: The study employs the CICIDS2017 dataset to design and test intrusion detection systems and cyber risk prediction models using classifiers like XGBoost, Random Forest, and Decision Tree. Explainable AI techniques are also integrated to boost interpretability.

Result: The framework proved effective in identifying malicious network activities and assessing cyber risk levels through robust performance measures such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, ROC-AUC, and false positive rate.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enhances the reliability and resilience of AI-driven cybersecurity systems for critical infrastructure, offering intelligent governance and decision-making support while ensuring transparency with Explainable AI.

Abstract: The increasing penetrations of the critical infrastructure sector in the United States with intelligent digital technologies have greatly increased exposure to advanced cyber adversaries and operational vulnerabilities. AI-powered governance and automated decision-making systems are becoming a key part of the operation of critical infrastructure systems, including energy, healthcare, transportation, financial services, and communication infrastructure, in order to improve efficiency and strategic management. The growing cyber threat environment, such as Distributed Denial of Service (DDos) attacks, botnets, ransomware, and Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) pose significant challenges to infrastructure resilience, cyber security reliability, and governance trustworthiness. In a changing attack landscape and dynamic network environment, traditional cybersecurity mechanisms can often fall short of meeting the evolving needs and protecting critical systems. This study will develop a resilient cyber risk analytics and model reliability assessment framework to support intelligent governance and decision support for cyber risk exposure in the U.S. critical infrastructure environment. This study is based on the CICIDS2017 dataset for the development and testing of intrusion detection system models and cyber risk prediction models based on machine learning. Various classifiers like XGBoost, Random Forest, and Decision Tree are used to detect malicious activities on the network and determine the level of cyber risk. Furthermore, the Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) techniques are integrated to enhance transparency, interpretability, and trust in cybersecurity decision-making processes. The proposed framework presents the reliability and resilience of the model by having various performance measures such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, ROC-AUC, and false positive rate.

</details>


### [582] [GenTI: Benchmarking LLMs for Autonomous IDPS Rule Generation for Unseen Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05844)
*Hassan Jalil Hadi,Rehana Yasmin,Ali Shoker*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper presents GenTI, a framework and dataset leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) to automatically generate intrusion detection and prevention rules for unseen attacks. The dataset includes over 200k annotated rules and the method significantly boosts detection and reduces false positives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing rule-based IDPS systems lack adaptability for emerging and zero-day threats due to their reliance on manually crafted, signature-driven rules.

Method: The authors proposed a combination of an extensive dataset (GTI) and an LLM-based pipeline for automatic rule generation. Techniques like structured prompt engineering, Chain-of-Thought (CoT), and Chain-of-Verification (CoVe) loop were utilized for validation and optimization.

Result: GenTI improves unseen attack detection from 45% to 87.4% and reduces the false-positive rate from 8.5% to 2.3%, achieving an overall rule-quality score of 89.4% with 94.8% CTI coverage.

Conclusion: GenTI establishes the first comprehensive benchmark for blending Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) with LLMs, enabling more adaptive and effective intrusion detection and prevention systems.

Abstract: Rule-based Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDPS) offer precise attack detection as well as mitigation, however their manually crafted, signature-driven rules limit adaptability to emerging and zero-day threats. Additionally, existing public datasets (e.g., CICIDS2017, UNSW-NB15) focus on traffic classification and provide little structured information to support automatic rule synthesis or prevention logic. To address this gap, we propose Generative Thread Intelligence (GenTI) \footnote{GenTI refers to the proposed framework, and GTI refers to the dataset.} an LLM-driven benchmark for automatic generation of IDPS rules targeting unseen attacks. The dataset (GTI) aggregates over 150k detection and prevention rules from Snort, Suricata, Emerging Threats, as well as 50k YARA, each annotated with protocol behavior, payload signatures, contextual relationships, mappings to Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI), along with actionable response types (alert, drop, reject). Moreover, on top of this corpus we design an LLM-based pipeline that transforms analyst prompts and representative payloads into deployable rules via structured prompt engineering, Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning, as well as a Chain-of-Verification (CoVe) loop for syntactic, semantic, and security validation. The generated rules are executed in real time on (Snort/Suricata) and evaluated by syntax accuracy, semantic similarity, CTI coverage, security effectiveness as well as unseen attacks detection. Furthermore, our GenTI instantiation achieves a composite rule-quality score of 89.4\%, with 94.8\% CTI coverage, improving unseen attacks detection from 45\% to 87.4\% and reducing the false-positive rate from 8.5\% to 2.3\%. Overall, GenTI establishes the first large-scale benchmark that tightly couples rule-level CTI with LLM-based automation, enabling adaptive, self-evolving IDPS.

</details>


### [583] [AttackPathGNN: Cross-function vulnerability detection in smart contracts using state interference graphs and conjunction pooling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05986)
*Gabriela Dobrita,Simona-Vasilica Oprea,Adela Bara*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces AttackPathGNN, a graph neural network for detecting vulnerabilities in Solidity smart contracts by reasoning over explicit attack paths instead of relying on single-function syntactic patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing vulnerability detectors overlook that many severe exploits in smart contracts arise from interactions between functions and conditions, not just single-function issues.

Method: The proposed method uses AttackPathGNN, a GNN leveraging a State Interference Graph to model function relationships and conjunction pooling to aggregate exploit preconditions.

Result: AttackPathGNN achieves an F1 score of 92.3% on SmartBugs Wild and performs well on the SmartBugs Curated benchmark, accurately identifying 6/10 DASP10 vulnerabilities with high precision.

Conclusion: AttackPathGNN efficiently detects vulnerabilities in Solidity smart contracts, providing actionable insights for developers through structured remediation reports.

Abstract: Existing learning-based detectors for Solidity smart-contracts reduce vulnerability detection to syntactic pattern matching within single functions, yet many of the most consequential exploits (The DAO, Cream Finance) exist not in any individual function but in the relationship between functions and in the combination of conditions that made the attack feasible. Thus, we propose AttackPathGNN, a graph neural network (GNN) that reframes detection as reasoning over explicit attack paths. Two architectural choices distinguish it from prior GNN-based detectors: (1)a State Interference Graph that links every pair of functions sharing mutable storage through typed, weighted edges and through directed reentrancy-path edges defined by an explicit five-condition predicate; (2)conjunction pooling, a differentiable AND-aggregator over eight named exploit preconditions whose log-sigmoid form causes the per-function exploit score to collapse whenever any single mitigation (a reentrancy guard, an access-control modifier or SafeMath) is in place. Across five independent training runs, AttackPathGNN attains 92.3+/-0.2% F1 on the SmartBugs Wild held-out test partition (4.3+/-0.3% false-negative rate, 90.8+/-2.5% detection rate on the independently human-labelled SmartBugs Curated benchmark), recovering 6/10 DASP10 categories at 100% on every seed and Reentrancy at 98.7+/-1.8%. Each prediction is emitted with a structured remediation report, turning each verdict into an actionable, function-level audit finding.

</details>


### [584] [Will the Agent Recuse Itself? Measuring LLM-Agent Compliance with In-Band Access-Deny Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06460)
*Thamilvendhan Munirathinam*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces the "Recuse Signal," a cooperative governance standard that provides a way for servers to signal autonomous LLM agents to voluntarily withdraw from accessing resources. This is tested experimentally, showing promising results with agents honoring the signal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: With the growing use of autonomous LLM agents managing real credentials and infrastructure, there is a lack of mechanisms for servers to signal agents to stop accessing a resource without a hard failure or allowing access entirely.

Method: The authors propose the Recuse Signal as a lightweight, in-band deny mechanism that operates through existing protocol channels. They define its specification, develop low-impact adapters for common systems (SSH and PostgreSQL), deploy these in production, and run controlled experiments to evaluate agent compliance.

Result: Their experiment shows that the Recuse Signal reliably induces recusal in LLM agents, achieving 100% signal compliance in the pilot study. However, they also find that compliance is context-sensitive—agents proceed when explicitly authorized.

Conclusion: The Recuse Signal demonstrates potential for cooperative governance of LLM agent operations, showing promise as a voluntary control mechanism. However, its effectiveness in broader contexts requires more study. All resources, including the standard, implementation adapters, and experimental setup, are published for further use.

Abstract: As autonomous LLM agents increasingly hold real credentials and operate infrastructure without a human in the loop, operators have no standard way to tell an agent that a resource is off-limits. Access controls either let the agent in (it has valid credentials) or hard-fail it (indistinguishable from any other client). We propose a third mode: a lightweight, published in-band deny signal -- the Recuse Signal -- that a server emits over a protocol's existing channels (an SSH banner, a PostgreSQL NOTICE) asking a connecting automated agent to voluntarily withdraw. This is a cooperative governance control, the robots.txt analogue for live access; it is explicitly not a security boundary. Its value is entirely empirical and, to our knowledge, unmeasured: do compliant LLM agents actually honor such a signal? We define the signal as an open mini-standard, implement two zero- or low-footprint adapters (an SSH banner/PAM hook and a PostgreSQL wire-protocol proxy), deploy them on a live production host, and run a controlled experiment in which fresh agents are given a benign operations task and observed for recusal. In a pilot (SSH; OpenAI GPT-4o and GPT-4o-mini; and Claude Code as a deployed agent), the signal cleanly induces recusal -- 100% recusal when present versus 100% task completion in a no-signal control -- and, revealingly, behaves as a cooperative rather than absolute signal: an explicit operator-authorization framing flips the most capable model to proceed, while other agents continue to defer to the on-host policy. We release the standard, adapters, and experiment harness for reproduction.

</details>


<div id='eess.AS'></div>

# eess.AS [[Back]](#toc)

### [585] [Revisiting Lexicon Evaluation in Unsupervised Word Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06183)
*Simon Malan,Danel Slabbert,Herman Kamper*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: The paper critiques the normalized edit distance metric used in zero-resource speech lexicon evaluations and proposes two improved metrics to address its shortcomings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a fairer and more accurate assessment of lexicon quality by addressing the biases and limitations in current evaluation metrics for zero-resource speech processing.

Method: The authors propose two new metrics: a size-weighted metric for within-cluster consistency and an inverse metric to evaluate the spread of true words across clusters. They validated the metrics on synthetic and real-world lexicon data.

Result: The proposed metrics showed a stronger correlation with true lexicon quality and were less prone to evaluation biases, ensuring a more trustworthy measurement.

Conclusion: The new metrics provide a more balanced and reliable evaluation framework, improving the accuracy and fairness of lexicon quality assessments in zero-resource speech processing.

Abstract: Building a lexicon from discovered word-like units is a central goal in zero-resource speech processing. But do our evaluations provide a trustworthy indication of lexicon quality? A common metric, normalized edit distance, averages the phoneme edit distances between discovered units in each cluster. We show that this metric has an inherent bias toward the quality of large clusters, inhibiting fair evaluation. Moreover, it ignores how well true classes are distributed across clusters. Based on established theory in clustering literature, we propose two metrics that address these shortcomings: a modified metric that weighs cluster size when assessing within-cluster consistency, and an inverse metric that assesses how true words are spread across clusters. Through experiments on synthetic and real-world lexicons, we demonstrate that combined, these metrics are: (1) more closely correlated with how similar a lexicon is to the ground-truth distribution, and (2) more robust to biases that skew lexicon evaluations.

</details>


### [586] [USAD 2.0: Scaling Representation Distillation for Universal Audio Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06444)
*Heng-Jui Chang,Alexander H. Liu,Saurabhchand Bhati,Mrudula Athi,Anton Ratnarajah,Amit Chhetri,James Glass*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: The paper introduces USAD 2.0, an advanced universal audio encoder combining SSL and supervised techniques for multi-domain application, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve audio encoder performance by integrating diverse knowledge sources, addressing teacher mismatch, and extending domain coverage for better performance in LLM-based applications.

Method: USAD 2.0 utilizes domain-aware distillation, second-stage supervised distillation, and depth scaling to integrate knowledge from SSL and supervised models while expanding to the music domain.

Result: USAD 2.0 demonstrates strong or state-of-the-art performance across diverse evaluations, including probing and LLM-based tests.

Conclusion: USAD 2.0 is a significant advancement in universal audio encoders, effectively addressing multi-domain coverage and achieving high performance.

Abstract: Audio encoders are critical to modern audio applications as large language models (LLMs) increasingly rely on a single encoder for diverse inputs. While self-supervised learning (SSL) has yielded strong domain-specific encoders like speech or music experts, multi-domain approaches like USAD and SPEAR remain limited in coverage and evaluation. Recent studies also suggest supervised encoders align better with audio LLMs. We present USAD 2.0, a universal encoder integrating knowledge from both SSL and supervised foundation models. USAD 2.0 introduces domain-aware distillation to address teacher mismatch, extends coverage to the music domain, and adds second-stage supervised distillation for downstream use. We further scale the model to one billion parameters via depth scaling. Experiments show USAD 2.0 achieves strong or state-of-the-art performance across probing and LLM-based evaluations.

</details>


<div id='econ.GN'></div>

# econ.GN [[Back]](#toc)

### [587] [Can AI Refute Economic Theory? Evidence from Beyond the Knowledge Cutoff](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05383)
*Alexis Akira Toda*

Main category: econ.GN

TL;DR: Experiments tested AI's ability to refute economic theory, showing that ChatGPT Pro performed relatively well but AI can't independently locate errors without human assistance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore whether artificial intelligence can effectively identify and refute errors in published economic theory papers.

Method: Four economic theory papers with known errors were analyzed using AI models like ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Refine. The performances of these models were compared in their ability to identify errors.

Result: ChatGPT Pro outperformed other models, sometimes creating counterexamples and corrected proofs. However, AI models failed to independently find true errors without significant human input.

Conclusion: Current AI, while promising in assisting humans, is not yet capable of independently identifying and refuting errors in economic theory. Combining competent humans with advanced AI can enhance peer review processes.

Abstract: Can artificial intelligence (AI) refute economic theory? I document experiments in which I asked several AI models (Gemini, Refine, Claude, and ChatGPT) to check the correctness of four published papers in economic theory, each containing an error that I helped identify or correct. ChatGPT Pro performed best, occasionally constructing counterexamples and corrected proofs, while other models fared worse. However, no model located a true error without substantial human guidance, and data contamination complicates interpretation. I argue that a competent human paired with a frontier model can outperform current peer review, but AI cannot yet refute economic theory on its own.

</details>


<div id='physics.comp-ph'></div>

# physics.comp-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [588] [Finite Element-Based Material Learning via Automatic Differentiation: Learning constitutive neural network models from full-field deformation data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05199)
*Matthias Knipper,Chenyi Ji,Malte Brand,Kevin Linka*

Main category: physics.comp-ph

TL;DR: This paper introduces FE-MAD, a framework for identifying neural network-based constitutive models directly from full-field deformation data, leveraging automatic differentiation within finite element solvers to overcome limitations of traditional calibration methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for identifying constitutive models from deformation data are either too computationally expensive, sensitive to noise, or require large datasets. This work aims to develop a robust, efficient alternative addressing these limitations.

Method: The authors integrate constitutive neural network models into a finite element solver (JAX-FEM) and use gradient-based loss minimization via forward- and reverse-mode automatic differentiation. Two neural architectures are tested: a grey-box CANN (flexible polyconvex model) and a white-box CANN (phenomenologically interpretable).

Result: The framework demonstrates success on three datasets, including both full digital image correlation data and reduced data scenarios, effectively identifying constitutive laws and generalizing to previously unseen cases.

Conclusion: FE-MAD offers a computationally efficient, robust, and generalizable approach for learning constitutive models directly from heterogeneous data, combining automatic differentiation with finite element solvers for high accuracy and versatility.

Abstract: The identification of constitutive neural network models from heterogeneous full-field deformation data provides a robust alternative to traditional calibration methods based on homogeneous stress-strain experiments, particularly given the high dimensionality of trainable parameters. Existing approaches must balance generality, robustness, and computational efficiency: Conventional finite element model updating is broadly applicable but computationally demanding; weak-form methods offer efficiency but are sensitive to noise and data scarcity; neural operator models are highly expressive but require extensive training datasets. This work presents FE-MAD (Finite Element-Based Material learning via Automatic Differentiation), an end-to-end differentiable framework that integrates a constitutive neural network model within a JAX-FEM nonlinear solver and identifies its parameters through gradient-based minimization of a measurement-mismatch loss. Newton tangent stiffness and loss gradients are computed automatically using forward- and reverse-mode automatic differentiation throughout the entire pipeline, thereby removing the need for analytic adjoints or offline surrogate models. FE-MAD is demonstrated for two architectures: a grey-box Constitutive Artificial Neural Network (CANN), a polyconvex, fully connected model with high flexibility, and a white-box CANN, an expert-system network with phenomenologically interpretable strain-energy terms. Focusing on incompressible isotropic hyperelasticity, FE-MAD is evaluated on three open experimental datasets: (1) full digital image correlation (DIC) of a perforated tensile specimen, (2) a reduced-data scenario with a one-dimensional stretch profile and global force-displacement curve, and (3) a heterogeneous matrix-inclusion system in which both phases constitutive laws are identified and generalized to twenty-two previously unseen samples.

</details>


### [589] [A differentiable machine learning small-angle X-ray scattering analysis framework for structure elucidation of lipid nanoparticles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05200)
*Maria Bånkestad,Sandra Barman,Magnus Röding,Erik Kaunisto,Viktoriia Meklesh,Audrey Gallud,Marco Mendez,Marianna Yanez Arteta,Stefan Norberg,Ann Terry,Smita Chakraborty,Shun Yu,Jerk Rönnols,Sepideh Pashami*

Main category: physics.comp-ph

TL;DR: A new machine-learning-enhanced framework is introduced for SAXS analysis of lipid nanoparticles, solving issues of complexity and ambiguity in structural analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional SAXS methods for analyzing lipid nanoparticles face challenges such as non-unique solutions and high computational costs in modeling internal structure and size distributions.

Method: Developed a machine-learning-accelerated framework with a differentiable forward model combining core-shell particle geometry, Gaussian random-field interiors, and a neural surrogate for SAXS map predictions; enabled large-scale fitting and ensemble analysis.

Result: The surrogate model reduced prediction costs by 10,000 times, and the framework revealed that similar SAXS fits might arise from distinct internal structural models, dominated by size-distribution and structure trade-offs.

Conclusion: The framework enhances SAXS analysis accuracy while addressing non-uniqueness in solving internal structures of lipid nanoparticles, with significant computational efficiency.

Abstract: Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) are efficient delivery systems for negatively charged nucleic acids. Their multi-component architecture yields a core-shell structure. Small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) is an important characterization technique for LNPs, but recovering internal structure and size distribution from SAXS is an inverse problem with non-unique solutions. Realistic models are often too expensive for systematic exploration. We introduce a machine-learning-accelerated, differentiable framework for SAXS analysis of heterogeneous, polydisperse LNPs. The forward model combines a core-shell particle with a Gaussian random-field interior, a neural surrogate for the monodisperse SAXS map, and a differentiable layer integrating over particle-size distributions. The surrogate reduces prediction cost by four orders of magnitude, while differentiability enables large-scale multi-start fitting and ensemble identifiability analysis. Applied to synthetic and experimental MC3 LNP data, the framework shows that near-identical SAXS fits can arise from distinct parameter modes, with the experimental fits dominated by a trade-off between size-distribution and interior-structure parameters.

</details>


### [590] [Multi-Fidelity Learning with Shallow Recurrent Decoders for Reactor Physics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05202)
*Stefano Riva,Carolina Introini,J. Nathan Kutz,Antonio Cammi*

Main category: physics.comp-ph

TL;DR: The paper explores a new machine learning framework, "Shallow Recurrent Decoders," to efficiently map low-fidelity neutronic data (e.g., point kinetics models) to high-fidelity reactor models (e.g., multi-group diffusion solutions).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Computational modeling of neutronics in reactor physics often requires balancing between accuracy and efficiency. Solving the Boltzmann transport equation for high-fidelity results is computationally expensive, while low-fidelity models like point kinetics are efficient but less precise. This motivates the need for a method to bridge the gap between these fidelity levels.

Method: The authors propose the use of a novel machine learning architecture called Shallow Recurrent Decoders. This approach leverages multi-fidelity input data (e.g., local or global measurements or simpler lumped models) to predict the high-dimensional, high-fidelity reactor states efficiently.

Result: The study successfully applied the proposed approach to a benchmark reactor geometry, demonstrating its ability to map outputs of a low-fidelity point kinetics model to high-fidelity diffusion equation solutions under varying input conditions with significantly lower computational cost.

Conclusion: Shallow Recurrent Decoders offer a promising approach to effectively and efficiently bridge the gap between low-fidelity and high-fidelity reactor physics models, reducing computational burdens while maintaining high accuracy.

Abstract: In reactor physics, neutronics can be treated with different fidelity levels, according to the needs of the user. On one hand, the precise modeling of neutrons' behaviour in reactor physics is often expensive and time-consuming due to the high computational costs to numerically solve the Boltzmann transport equation. Conversely, by adopting suitable assumptions, such as the SP$_N$, diffusion theory, and point kinetics, it is possible to generate efficiently low-fidelity data. From the perspective of surrogate models, this computational limitation translates into a scarcity of high-fidelity data and a significant amount of low-fidelity data. Given this difference in fidelity levels, it would be interesting to develop a suitable procedure to map low-fidelity models towards higher fidelity models; for instance, one could obtain the solution to a multi-group diffusion equation starting from time-series data obtained from a point kinetics model. Indeed, this work investigates this possibility by leveraging multi-fidelity information with Shallow Recurrent Decoders, a novel machine learning architecture able to map time-series observations to the full state of the reactor. This technique has been designed to use local or global measurements as input and map their temporal trajectories to the high-dimensional state; by the same logic, in principle, this architecture can also be used when the input is formed by the solution of a lumped model. This work applies this idea to a benchmark reactor geometry, mapping the point kinetics model to the diffusion solution under various input conditions, with much less computational costs.

</details>


<div id='cs.DB'></div>

# cs.DB [[Back]](#toc)

### [591] [Data Flow Control: Data Safety Policies for AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05679)
*Charlie Summers,Eugene Wu*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: The paper introduces Data Flow Control (DFC), a framework to ensure data safety by enforcing regulatory, privacy, or business constraints on SQL queries within a DBMS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the gap between query correctness in automated data analysis and ensuring compliance with safety regulations and constraints, which they argue is a critical infrastructure problem.

Method: They formalized data safety using aggregate predicates over provenance monomials and developed Passant, a query rewriting layer that enforces DFC policies efficiently across different database engines without materializing provenance.

Result: Passant demonstrated ~0% overhead and significant performance improvements when tested across five DBMS engines, outperforming alternatives by orders of magnitude.

Conclusion: Data Flow Control provides a robust mechanism to embed safety enforcement directly into the data infrastructure, moving beyond surface-level validation or post-hoc checks.

Abstract: Agents increasingly generate SQL, orchestrate pipelines, and automate data analysis on behalf of users. While recent work improves query correctness, correctness is not safety. A query may be semantically valid yet violate regulatory, privacy, or business constraints that govern how data may be combined and released. We argue that enforcing such constraints is fundamentally a data infrastructure problem.
  This paper introduces Data Flow Control (DFC), a framework to declaratively specify and guarantee policy enforcement over tuple-level data flows within a DBMS query. A key challenge is defining a policy language that is optimizer-invariant yet efficient to enforce at scale. We formalize data safety as aggregate predicates over provenance monomials and present Passant, a portable query rewriting layer that enforces DFC policies without materializing provenance. Across five DBMS engines -- DuckDB, Umbra, PostgreSQL, DataFusion, and SQLServer -- Passant achieves ~0% overhead and outperforms alternatives by orders of magnitude. As a result, Data Flow Control is the first step towards moving data safety from prompts and post-hoc checks into the data infrastructure. Data Flow Control is available open source at https://github.com/dataflowcontrol/data-flow-control.

</details>


### [592] [Causal Scaffolding for Physical Reasoning: A Benchmark for Causally-Informed Physical World Understanding in VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05966)
*Tianyi Tang,Zhuoyi Lin,Zeyu Feng,Tianyi Ma,Yew-Soon Ong,Ivor Tsang,Haiyan Yin*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: The paper introduces CausalPhys, a benchmark dataset and methodology to enhance and evaluate causal reasoning in vision-language models (VLMs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite advancements in vision-language models, they often fail at causal physical reasoning, producing plausible but incorrect outcomes. This paper aims to address this gap in capability.

Method: The researchers developed CausalPhys, a dataset with over 3,000 video- and image-based questions accompanied by expert-annotated causal graphs. They also introduced a causal-graph-grounded metric for evaluating how well VLMs understand causal reasoning and proposed a fine-tuning approach called CRFT to align models' reasoning with causal structures.

Result: The study uncovered systematic weaknesses in current VLMs' ability to capture causal dependencies. The proposed CRFT methodology significantly improved both reasoning accuracy and the interpretability of these models.

Conclusion: CausalPhys integrates dataset design, evaluation metrics, and learning strategies to provide a comprehensive framework for advancing VLMs' causal reasoning capabilities. This work highlights the importance of causality-aware learning for advancing artificial intelligence.

Abstract: Understanding and reasoning about the physical world is the foundation of intelligent behavior, yet state-of-the-art vision-language models (VLMs) still fail at causal physical reasoning, often producing plausible but incorrect answers. To address this gap, we introduce CausalPhys, a benchmark of over 3,000 carefully curated video- and image-based questions spanning four domains: Perception, Anticipation, Intervention, and Goal Orientation. Each question is paired with an expert-annotated causal graph capturing object-attribute-event dependencies, enabling interpretable and fine-grained evaluation of causal understanding. Building on this, we formulate a causal-graph-grounded metric that quantitatively measures how well a model's chain-of-thought reasoning aligns with the correct causal relations, moving beyond answer-only accuracy and enabling systematic diagnosis of VLMs' causal reasoning failures. Using this metric, we conduct a comprehensive analysis of leading VLMs, revealing systematic gaps in capturing causal dependencies and underscoring the need for causality-aware learning. To address these limitations, we further propose Causal Rationale-informed Fine-Tuning (CRFT), which explicitly aligns VLM reasoning with causal structures. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CRFT substantially enhances both reasoning accuracy and interpretability across multiple model backbones. By unifying dataset curation, causal evaluation, and causality-informed learning, CausalPhys establishes a strong foundation for advancing modern VLMs toward causally grounded physical reasoning.

</details>


### [593] [TOKI: A Bitemporal Operator Algebra for Contradiction Resolution in LLM-Agent Persistent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06240)
*Ziming Wang*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: This paper introduces TOKI, a framework to formalize and address anomalies in persistent memory systems of large language model (LLM) agents, offering a correctness specification for write-time heuristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address and formalize the issue of write-time anomalies and lack of explicit isolation-level declarations in existing contradiction resolution techniques for persistent memories in LLM agents.

Method: The paper introduces TOKI, a family of bitemporal operators designed to handle resolution heuristics with an explicit correctness contract. The framework uses dual-row schemas and provenance annotations to track anomalies while preserving reliability and reproducibility.

Result: TOKI was shown to exclude all three major anomalies (replay inconsistency, belief-drift skew, and audit erasure) while preserving the language model's role in resolution. Tests on the LoCoMo workload demonstrated accuracy gains but noted limitations in cross-system comparisons.

Conclusion: TOKI provides a write-time correctness specification that standardizes and strengthens the resolution process for LLM persistent memories, balancing consistency and functionality gaps left by existing systems.

Abstract: Persistent memory for an LLM agent is a write-heavy substrate: every belief update is a versioned write, and a new claim may contradict a stored one. Production systems use four resolution heuristics (last-writer-wins, evidence-weighted merge, await-confirmation, per-rule policy), yet none declares the isolation level it assumes or the write-time anomalies it admits. We show that contradiction resolution is write-time concurrency control and make the missing contract explicit. TOKI types the four heuristics as one family of bitemporal operators over a dual-row schema, each with an isolation precondition and a provenance annotation that preserves the losing fact in an audit row. Four soundness theorems close the contract across isolation, schema, and provenance, lift the guarantees to operator pipelines, and extend the fold operators to n-ary conflict sets. A tightness companion proves that, within the relational schedule model, keyed logging of the adjudicating judge is necessary for replay consistency, which every audited baseline omits. A verdict matrix over eight systems localizes the gap: every baseline that keeps a language-model judge on the write path admits at least one of three write-time anomalies (replay inconsistency, belief-drift skew, audit erasure); a content-addressed engine-layer comparator avoids them only by removing the judge, and TOKI alone excludes all three while keeping it. On its one natural-workload slice the audit-row defence moves LoCoMo by 0.86, and ablating the typed memory layer removes 0.49 accuracy on 1,444 answerable LoCoMo questions; the cross-system comparison stays underpowered and claims no superiority. The contribution is the contract: a write-time correctness specification, proved sound across isolation, schema, and provenance, pinning the guarantee every production heuristic assumes but no deployed system makes explicit.

</details>


<div id='math-ph'></div>

# math-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [594] [The Score Hamiltonian: Mapping Diffusion Models to Adiabatic Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05217)
*Peter Halmos,Boris Hanin*

Main category: math-ph

TL;DR: The paper establishes a precise relationship between sampling using score-based diffusion models and adiabatic ground state transport in quantum mechanics through "Score Hamiltonians."


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need to better understand the theoretical foundations of score-based diffusion models used in machine learning, particularly for sampling tasks.

Method: The authors construct "Score Hamiltonians" from the learned score's quantum potential and use adiabatic transport principles and Fokker-Planck equations to derive novel bounds and schedules for density reconstruction.

Result: The fundamental limit of sampling is determined by the ratio of squared score-matching error to the spectral gap of the Score Hamiltonian, which is related to the data density's inverse Poincaré constant.

Conclusion: Adiabatic theorems and quantum mechanics inform both the accuracy and limits of score-based sampling algorithms, centering these results around Score Hamiltonian properties.

Abstract: We exhibit an exact correspondence between sampling with score-based diffusion models and adiabatic transport of ground states for a family of Schrödinger operators we call Score Hamiltonians, built from the learned score's quantum potential. We obtain novel density reconstruction bounds and principled annealing schedules via adiabatic theorems for Fokker-Planck equations with time-varying potentials. We find the fundamental limit of sampling is set by the ratio of squared score-matching error to Score Hamiltonian spectral gap - the inverse Poincaré constant of the data density.

</details>


<div id='cond-mat.dis-nn'></div>

# cond-mat.dis-nn [[Back]](#toc)

### [595] [Nonreversible Gauge Fields in Fokker--Planck Dynamics: Supersymmetric Hamiltonians and Learned Finite Forces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06412)
*Masayuki Ohzeki*

Main category: cond-mat.dis-nn

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework to analyze and optimize Fokker--Planck dynamics through nonreversible perturbations (gauge fields), preserving invariant densities while modifying relaxation spectra. It develops tools for spectral gap optimization, applies this to Gaussian and nonconvex benchmarks, and links the method to stochastic gradient algorithms like Adam.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Fokker--Planck dynamics are limited by challenges in accelerating relaxation to equilibrium and analyzing nonreversible systems. This paper seeks to provide a unifying operator framework to address these issues by formulating spectral gaps, transition rates, and gauge current representations in nonequilibrium systems.

Method: The authors use a similarity transformation of the Fokker--Planck operator to map it into a supersymmetric Hamiltonian framework. Nonreversible perturbations are modeled as antisymmetric tensor fields (gauge fields) and analyzed through spectral and Lyapunov-based optimization. Benchmarks and stochastic gradient methods are connected to this framework.

Result: The framework successfully separates spectral transitions from finite-time optimal solutions, as demonstrated in Gaussian Ornstein-Uhlenbeck and double-well model experiments. The learned gauge configurations match theoretically optimal values for both benchmarks.

Conclusion: This work not only advances theoretical understanding of relaxation and spectral modification in Fokker--Planck systems but also finds practical utility by linking stochastic gradients and adaptive optimizers to underlying physical dynamics and finite-time optimization.

Abstract: We formulate stationary-density-preserving nonreversible perturbations of Fokker--Planck dynamics as gauge fields that deform relaxation spectra while leaving the invariant state fixed. When detailed balance holds, a similarity transformation maps the reversible Fokker--Planck operator to a Witten-Laplacian-type supersymmetric Hamiltonian; nonreversible gauges then appear as non-Hermitian perturbations that preserve the zero mode but modify the excited spectrum. This operator viewpoint gives a common language for relaxation gaps, circulating probability currents, hypocoercive acceleration, and finite control costs. We represent admissible gauge currents by antisymmetric tensor fields and identify the detailed-balance-violating Ohzeki--Ichiki force as a constant symplectic example whose infinite-strength limit is Hamiltonian dynamics. The continuous-time spectral gap alone does not select a finite gauge strength, so we introduce a finite-time regularized objective and an actor--critic procedure for learning the gauge. An exactly solvable anisotropic Gaussian Ornstein--Uhlenbeck benchmark separates the spectral transition from the finite-time optimum and shows that the learned gauge recovers the Lyapunov-equation optimum. A double-well benchmark then illustrates the same constrained selection in a nonconvex metastable landscape. Stochastic gradient methods enter this framework as physically relevant Fokker--Planck systems: mini-batch noise acts as an effective diffusion tensor, and adaptive methods such as Adam correspond to metric choices with possible nonequilibrium currents.

</details>


<div id='math.HO'></div>

# math.HO [[Back]](#toc)

### [596] [Benchmarks in Leipzig](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05818)
*Andrei Balakin,Miklós Bóna,Marie-Charlotte Brandenburg,Clara Briand,Veronica Calvo Cortes,Shelby Cox,Jesus A. De Loera,Danai Deligeorgaki,Hannah Friedman,Tim Gehrunger,Chiara Giardino,Stephen Griffeth,Baran Hashemi,Elena Hoster,Alexander Ivanov,Nupur Jain,Aryaman Jal,Leonie Kayser,Joris Koefler,Kevin Kühn,Mario Kummer,Felix Lotter,René Marczinzik,Victor S. Miller,Alejandro Morales,Greta Panova,Gianni Petrella,Nathan Pflueger,Lakshmi Ramesh,Nikolas Rieke,Carlos Rodriguez,Andrea Rosana,Flavio Salizzoni,Otto T. P. Schmidt,Sven Ulf Schmitz,Lina Maria Simbaqueba Marin,Luca Sodomaco,Christian Stump,Bernd Sturmfels,Alexander Taveira Blomenhofer,Simon Telen,Philipp Tuchel,Emil Verkama,Carl Felix Waller,Julian Weigert,Annette Werner,Nathan Williams,Claudius Zibrowius*

Main category: math.HO

TL;DR: The paper documents the creation and assessment of a dataset of mathematics questions, testing LLMs' mathematical reasoning capabilities, which showed remarkable performance and improvement across evaluation stages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate and benchmark the mathematical reasoning capabilities of state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs) using challenging, research-level mathematics questions.

Method: A dataset of 100 research-level mathematics questions was created by 49 mathematicians. It was tested in three stages with state-of-the-art LLMs: single attempts by five models, 20-run evaluations by three models, and 3-run attempts by two intensive models.

Result: Through the three evaluation stages, the number of unsolved questions decreased from 41 to 16, and ultimately to only 2, showcasing the impressive reasoning capabilities of LLMs.

Conclusion: The findings highlight the evolving and significant potential of LLMs in handling complex mathematical reasoning tasks, with significant reductions in unsolved questions through advanced evaluations.

Abstract: Between April 1 and May 15, 2026, a group of 49 mathematicians compiled a dataset of research-level mathematics questions with known answers. Most of the work was done during the 3-day workshop *Benchmarks in Leipzig* with 35 participants at the Max Planck Institute for Mathematics in the Sciences in Leipzig, Germany. We present the resulting collection of 100 questions. We evaluated these questions in three stages: a single attempt by five state-of-the-art LLMs, followed by a 20-runs-per-model evaluation with three of these models, and finally a 3-run attempt with two heavy-thinking models. After Stage 1, 41 questions remained completely unsolved; after Stage 2, this count dropped to 16; and we concluded Stage 3 with only 2 unsolved questions. This demonstrates that the mathematical reasoning capabilities of LLMs are becoming impressive.

</details>


<div id='quant-ph'></div>

# quant-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [597] [Quantum enhanced rare event discovery and sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06316)
*Naixu Guo,Po-Wei Huang,Qisheng Wang,Jayne Thompson,Patrick Rebentrost,Mile Gu,Chengran Yang*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: The paper presents a quantum algorithm to efficiently discover and sample rare events occurring with extremely low probability, achieving optimal quantum scaling and quadratic speedup in certain scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rare events such as financial crashes, infrastructure failures, and critical AI errors have significant impacts despite occurring with very low probability, making efficient detection and sampling of these events crucial.

Method: The authors introduce a quantum algorithm designed to discover and sample rare events without prior knowledge of these events, employing optimal quantum scaling and robust techniques for heavy-tailed systems and stochastic processes.

Result: The algorithm achieves quadratic speedup for systems with heavy-tailed distributions and robust polynomial speedups for stationary stochastic processes, determined by their entropy-rate structure.

Conclusion: The proposed quantum algorithm offers an efficient and scalable solution for real-world problems involving rare events, leveraging the advantages of quantum computing to address challenges faced by classical methods.

Abstract: Financial crashes, cascading failures in infrastructure, and critical errors in AI systems are frequently triggered by events that occur with extremely small probability. Efficiently discovering and sampling events with probability below a threshold is therefore of critical interest. Yet this task is highly non-trivial using existing classical or quantum methods. Being rare, such events require an immense sampling overhead to collect sufficient data samples. Moreover, because the rare events are not known in advance, they cannot be flagged for amplification using standard techniques. Here, we introduce a quantum algorithm for rare-event discovery and sampling without first learning which events are rare. The algorithm achieves the optimal quantum scaling with the rarity threshold. We further demonstrate that this can achieve a quadratic speedup for heavy-tailed systems whose tail has nonvanishing total mass, and translates into a robust polynomial speedup for stationary stochastic processes, with the exponent determined by its entropy-rate structure.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.BM'></div>

# q-bio.BM [[Back]](#toc)

### [598] [An accurate nucleic acid-small molecule docking framework via geometric deep learning with large-scale pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05198)
*Shi Li,Xujun Zhang,Mingquan Liu,Hui Zhang,Shuoying Jia,Yu Kang,Tingjun Hou,Peichen Pan*

Main category: q-bio.BM

TL;DR: The paper introduces NucleoDock, a deep learning framework for docking small molecules to nucleic acids, addressing data scarcity and achieving improved accuracy and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Docking small molecules to nucleic acids is challenging due to limitations of physics-based methods and the scarcity of experimental nucleic acid-ligand complexes for deep learning methods.

Method: NucleoDock combines physics-guided pretraining on synthetic complexes with fine-tuning on experimental structures. It uses sequence- and structure-informed nucleotide representations and a mixture density network-based scoring mechanism for pose ranking.

Result: NucleoDock achieved a 56% success rate on a benchmark of 125 complexes at an RMSD cutoff of 2.0 Angstrom, outperforming a typical tool (rDock at 29%), while also demonstrating faster processing.

Conclusion: NucleoDock effectively bridges the gap between physics-based and deep learning methods for nucleic acid-directed drug discovery, with improved docking accuracy and efficiency.

Abstract: Nucleic acids are increasingly recognized as therapeutic targets beyond conventional protein-centered drug discovery, yet accurate and efficient docking of small molecules to nucleic acid structures remains challenging. Physics-based docking methods often show limited accuracy and efficiency, whereas deep learning approaches are constrained by the scarcity of experimentally resolved nucleic acid-ligand complexes. Here, we present NucleoDock, a deep learning framework for nucleic acid-small molecule docking. To address data scarcity, NucleoDock combines physics-guided large-scale pretraining on millions of docking-generated synthetic complexes with fine-tuning on curated experimental co-crystal structures. It further integrates sequence- and structure-informed nucleotide representations with atomistic three-dimensional features to capture both biological context and binding-site geometry. A mixture density network-based geometric scoring head is used to model conditional interaction-distance distributions for pose ranking. On an external benchmark of 125 nucleic acid-ligand complexes, NucleoDock achieved a top-1 success rate of 56 percent at an RMSD cutoff of 2.0 Angstrom, outperforming rDock with 29 percent, while generating 100 poses in approximately 5 seconds per complex. Retrospective virtual screening on the ROBIN benchmark further showed improved early enrichment. NucleoDock represents a step toward bridging the methodological gap between protein- and nucleic acid-directed computational drug discovery.

</details>


### [599] [AlloGen: Conformation-Selective Binder Generation with Differential State Scoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05474)
*Hanqun Cao,Zachary Quinn,Aastha Pal,Sumi Kimura,Jingjie Zhang,Pheng Ann Heng,Pranam Chatterjee*

Main category: q-bio.BM

TL;DR: This paper presents AlloGen, a computational framework for designing protein binders that are selective to specific structural states, addressing conformational selectivity issues in allosteric targets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Protein binders traditionally optimize for tight binding (affinity) but do not account for conformational selectivity, which is essential for functional specificity in allosteric targets like kinases and GPCRs.

Method: AlloGen decouples protein backbone generation from a state-selectivity scorer $Q_θ$, which uses an SE(3)-invariant graph transformer trained in two phases: learning interface geometry first, then incorporating conformational discrimination. It integrates seamlessly with backbone generators as either a reranker or a gradient-based guide.

Result: AlloGen successfully identifies selective protein binders for specific conformational states across diverse proteins. Experimental validation confirmed its effectiveness with de novo peptides binding the desired holo conformation without binding to the apo state.

Conclusion: AlloGen establishes conformational selectivity as a learnable property and offers a generalizable framework for designing state-selective protein binders.

Abstract: Protein binder design has largely optimized for affinity alone, leaving conformational selectivity unaddressed: for allosteric targets such as kinases, nuclear receptors, and GPCRs, a binder that engages both active and inactive states provides no functional specificity regardless of how tightly it binds. We introduce AlloGen, a modular framework that decouples backbone generation from a learned state-selectivity scorer $Q_θ$, an SE(3)-invariant interface graph transformer trained via a two-phase curriculum that first learns interface geometry before imposing conformational discrimination. Because $Q_θ$ is fully differentiable and generator-agnostic, it integrates with any backbone generator as a passive reranker or an active gradient-based guide without retraining. Across a diverse benchmark of proteins spanning multiple families and conformational mechanisms, AlloGen consistently identifies binders that preferentially recognize desired structural states while rejecting alternative conformations. Experimental validation on calmodulin further demonstrates that these computational selectivity signals translate to physical molecules, yielding de novo peptides that bind the desired holo conformation while exhibiting no detectable binding to the apo state. Together, these results establish conformational selectivity as a learnable property and provide a general framework for state-selective protein binder design.

</details>


<div id='math.OC'></div>

# math.OC [[Back]](#toc)

### [600] [Gradient descent at the Edge of Stability: free energy model and kinetic description of the two-layer network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05326)
*Antonin Chodron de Courcel*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: This paper studies gradient descent dynamics in the Edge of Stability regime and proposes a new continuous-time effective model to analyze and predict such behaviors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and model the effects of large learning rates in gradient descent, which induce persistent oscillations in loss and sharpness during optimization.

Method: A continuous-time effective model is formulated to track both the average trajectory and the time-averaged covariance of oscillations. For specific cases (e.g., wide two-layer networks), a mean-field limit leading to a novel kinetic equation is derived and interpreted as a Wasserstein-2 gradient flow.

Result: The model accurately captures the envelope of oscillations and predicts network training dynamics effectively. Numerical experiments on tasks like CIFAR-10 validate its performance.

Conclusion: The proposed effective free energy framework provides a robust understanding of unstable training regimes and offers predictive insights into training dynamics influenced by large learning rates.

Abstract: We study the dynamics of gradient descent in the Edge of Stability regime, where the learning rate is large enough to induce persistent oscillations in the loss and the sharpness. We propose a continuous-time effective model that tracks the evolution of the average trajectory coupled with the time-averaged covariance of its fast oscillations. Our analysis reveals that the natural quantity to monitor in such unstable regimes is an effective free energy, which combines the original risk functional with a curvature-related "entropic" term. Our model allows us to track the envelope of the oscillations even in situations where its dynamics evolve on similar timescales as the averaged weights. Otherwise stated, we can track the spikes that occur during the training of some neural network architectures. For wide two-layer neural networks optimized under stable non-vanishing oscillations, we derive a mean-field limit that results in a novel kinetic equation describing the joint distribution of weights and their fluctuations. We show that this equation can be interpreted as a Wasserstein-2 gradient flow of a macroscopic free energy. Finally, we provide numerical evidence on matrix factorization and deep learning tasks (CIFAR-10) to demonstrate the model's accuracy in capturing the envelope of the oscillations and the predictive power of the effective free energy.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.QM'></div>

# q-bio.QM [[Back]](#toc)

### [601] [The Language of Elution: Autoregressive Prediction of the Next Feature in Untargeted LC-HRMS Lipidomics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05225)
*Dayanjan S. Wijesinghe*

Main category: q-bio.QM

TL;DR: This paper addresses the limited structural annotation in untargeted LC-HRMS by reframing chromatographic elution as a sequence prediction task, training AI models to predict elution order.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the low annotation rates (2-20%) in untargeted LC-HRMS due to its reactive nature and inability to anticipate upcoming ions during tandem MS/MS acquisition.

Method: The study uses autoregressive models (LSTM and Transformer) trained on five features per molecular signal to predict the next eluting mass-to-charge (m/z) bin in reversed-phase chromatography, leveraging data from multiple clinical lipidomics cohorts.

Result: The LSTM achieves 98.4% top-1 accuracy, while the Transformer achieves 98.0%. Predictions generalize across instruments with similar methods but fail with different column chemistries or modes unless fine-tuned using minimal calibration.

Conclusion: Elution sequences are highly predictable, and predictive MS/MS acquisition could enhance the structural annotation coverage in untargeted metabolomics, though cross-condition deployment requires modest recalibration.

Abstract: Untargeted liquid chromatography-high-resolution mass spectrometry (LC-HRMS) detects thousands of molecular features per sample, yet only 2-20% receive confident structural annotations. A root cause of this "dark metabolome" is that tandem MS/MS acquisition is reactive: instruments select precursors only after ions appear, blind to what elutes next. We reframe chromatographic elution as an autoregressive sequence prediction task. Because reversed-phase elution order is governed by hydrophobicity, successive features form a physically constrained sequence, like tokens in language. We discretize the mass-to-charge (m/z) axis into 110 bins and train long short-term memory (LSTM) and Transformer models to predict the next eluting m/z bin from five annotation-free per-token features: m/z bin, mass defect, retention-time gap, polarity, and intensity rank. Trained on 15,242 features from four clinical lipidomics cohorts (342 plasma samples; SCIEX TripleTOF 6600+, Waters CSH C18), the LSTM reaches 98.4% top-1 accuracy (99.99% top-5; mean absolute error 3.6 Da) and the Transformer 98.0%. Ablation shows autoregressive context accounts for 55.5 percentage points while no single feature contributes more than 0.2 pp: the sequential pattern, not molecular properties, drives prediction. Models transfer across instruments sharing the method (r=0.999 on an independent Agilent 6530 dataset) but fail under a different column chemistry (5.1% top-1) or polarity mode (2.6%), confirming method- and mode-specificity. Fine-tuning on as few as two to five quality-control injections recovers held-out accuracy from 2.6% to nearly 50%, so cross-condition deployment needs minimal calibration. These results establish that elution sequences are highly predictable and lay the groundwork for predictive MS/MS acquisition to improve annotation coverage in untargeted metabolomics.

</details>


### [602] [$p$-adic Bi-Filtrations for Topological Machine Learning on Genomic Sequences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06117)
*Tirtharaj Dash,Gunja Sachdeva*

Main category: q-bio.QM

TL;DR: pVR is a novel machine learning framework combining $p$-adic mathematics and topological data analysis for alignment-free DNA sequence classification, achieving competitive results on genomic benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address challenges in alignment-free DNA sequence classification by introducing a novel method leveraging topological insights and $p$-adic metrics to improve performance, especially in low-sample scenarios.

Method: The method encodes DNA sequences using $p$-adic distances for capturing hierarchical structures and $L_1$ compositional distances for local content. It uses bi-filtered Vietoris—Rips complexes to generate topological summaries as input features for machine learning models.

Result: pVR outperformed existing alignment-free methods on low-sample genomic datasets, showing gains up to 21 percentage points on specific benchmarks. It also outperformed embeddings from advanced models like the Nucleotide Transformer v2 in select cases.

Conclusion: pVR demonstrates the utility of incorporating $p$-adic and topological data for DNA sequence classification, particularly under low-sample conditions. However, its assumptions may limit performance in datasets with point-mutation divergence, and traditional methods remain competitive in larger datasets.

Abstract: We introduce pVR, a topological machine learning framework for alignment-free genomic sequence classification that combines $p$-adic numbers with topological data analysis. Each DNA sequence is encoded along two complementary axes: a $p$-adic distance on $k$-mer prefixes, which captures hierarchical positional structure, and a compositional $L_1$ distance on $k$-mer frequencies, which captures local sequence content. The two distances jointly parameterise a bi-filtered Vietoris--Rips complex, and per-sequence topological summaries from this bi-filtration serve as features for standard machine learning classifiers. We establish theoretical guarantees for the construction: stability under metric perturbations and invariance to the choice of prime, alongside a result that explains why a single $p$-adic axis is topologically uninformative and why the bi-filtration recovers nontrivial homology. On twelve genomic benchmarks ($28$ to $500$ sequences, $3$ to $7$ classes), pVR outperforms four established alignment-free baselines on three of six low-sample datasets, with gains of up to $21$ percentage points; it underperforms only on a SARS-CoV-2 variant benchmark whose point-mutation divergence violates the hierarchical assumption, and all methods saturate in the large-sample regime. pVR also outperforms zero-shot frozen embeddings from the 500M-parameter Nucleotide Transformer v2 by $6.7$ to $11.4$ percentage points on three low-sample benchmarks. The pVR codebase is publicly available at https://github.com/MAHI-Group/pVR.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.GN'></div>

# q-bio.GN [[Back]](#toc)

### [603] [rsx: A high-performance streaming toolkit for RAD-seq sex determination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06434)
*Rohit Goswami,Ruhila Goswami*

Main category: q-bio.GN

TL;DR: The paper presents rsx, a robust and memory-efficient Rust implementation of RADSex for discovering sex-linked markers in large datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address limitations in RADSex, particularly its high memory usage and lack of posterior evidence reporting, as well as the absence of Python and C integration.

Method: rsx employs techniques like 2-bit DNA encoding, parallel ingestion, and memory-mapped tables, adding Bayesian statistical approaches to enhance sex-marker analysis. It ensures compatibility with RADSex workflows and improves computational efficiency.

Result: On four large RAD-seq datasets, rsx was significantly faster than RADSex (8.38x speedup in most cases), reproduced previous calls, identified novel W-linked markers in Danio albolineatus, and improved statistical robustness through posterior analysis.

Conclusion: rsx enhances RADSex, offering better speed, resource efficiency, and analytical depth. It is well-suited for sex-marker discovery and comes with Python and C integration for broader usability.

Abstract: Restriction site-associated DNA sequencing (RAD-seq) is widely used to discover sex-linked markers in non-model organisms, but large studies produce marker tables with millions of RAD tags. RADSex provides the reference workflow for building marker-by-individual depth tables and testing sex-biased marker distributions, but its depth, merge, and related table-building commands grow memory-hungry, and its standard output reports frequentist calls with no posterior evidence and no direct Python or C integration. We present rsx, a Rust implementation of the complete RADSex command set that preserves marker-table semantics and command-line compatibility. rsx combines 2-bit DNA keys, parallel ingestion, memory-mapped marker tables, external sorting, bitset group counts, and streamed Gram-matrix PCA so that memory stays bounded by the number of individuals or by explicit buffers. It adds conjugate Beta-Binomial Bayes factors and posterior probabilities under XY and ZW hypotheses, returning strict, posterior-supported, and Bayes-factor-only evidence grades. A portable, libm-independent minimax approximation of the error function keeps the chi-squared tail reproducible across platforms without changing the underlying Yates test. On four real RAD-seq datasets comprising 41.9 billion bases and 29 million markers, rsx reproduced published RADSex v1.2.0 calls, achieved an 8.38-fold geometric-mean speedup across 56 paired timings (2.77-fold for FASTQ processing), and recovered every Bonferroni-significant positive-control marker. In Danio albolineatus, treated as null in the source publication, the posterior layer surfaced 30 W-linked marker hypotheses; in Notothenia rossii it withheld 400 Bayes-factor-only rows compatible with a low-prevalence null. Python bindings, a C API, and a reproducibility archive provide the workflows used for all reported numbers. rsx is released under GPL-3.0-or-later.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [604] [X-Band UAV-enabled Integrated Sensing and Communications for Vehicular Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05262)
*Remon Polus,Soumaya Cherkaoui*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This study explores optimal time allocation for UAVs serving as platforms for both sensing and communication in vehicular networks, focusing on balancing these functions under realistic constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges in optimizing the dual functionalities of sensing and communication for UAV-enabled systems in intelligent transportation, taking into account constraints and channel effects in real-world deployments.

Method: Developed an optimization framework to allocate time between UAV-based sensing and communication. The framework considers practical UAV constraints, fading effects, and target distances using single- and double-shadowing channel models.

Result: Simulation results show adaptive time allocation strategies, demonstrating the influence of channel conditions and target distances on achieving a balance between sensing and communication.

Conclusion: The study presents effective time allocation strategies for UAVs in smart mobility scenarios, advancing the integration of sensing and communication for intelligent transportation systems.

Abstract: Uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) are increasingly considered as aerial platforms capable of providing both sensing and communication services, representing a promising paradigm for intelligent transportation systems. This paper investigates the optimal time allocation for a UAV-enabled integrated sensing and communication (ISaC) system operating in the X-band for vehicular networks. We analyze the trade-off between sensing accuracy and communication performance under practical UAV constraints and fading effects, considering both single-shadowing and double-shadowing channel models. An optimization framework is developed to allocate time between sensing and communication while guaranteeing minimum communication rates and sufficient sensing reliability. Simulation results demonstrate adaptive time allocation strategies, highlighting how UAV-to-ground channel conditions and target distances influence the balance between sensing and communication in smart mobility scenarios.

</details>


### [605] [Automated Proving of Shannon-Type Entropy Inequalities via Fine-Tuned Language Models and Guided Tree Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05729)
*Shing Yin Wong,Shaocheng Liu,Linqi Song,Amin Gohari,Cheuk Ting Li*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper addresses automating the proof of Shannon-type entropy inequalities using small-scale fine-tuned language models combined with a guided beam search, achieving an 85% success rate on a test set.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Shannon-type entropy inequalities are critical in information theory, but proving them involves complex combinatorial searches that scale poorly. This paper explores automated solutions using language models.

Method: Small-scale large language models (0.6B–1.7B parameters) were fine-tuned on atomic proof steps and coupled with guided beam search to explore automation of proving inequalities.

Result: The 0.6B model achieved an 85% success rate on a test set with tree search, outperforming larger models like GPT-5.5 (1.7% success) and Psitip (33.3% success). Optimal training configuration was identified as having 4096 tokens and non-skewed data distribution.

Conclusion: Fine-tuned small-scale language models paired with beam scoring provide an effective approach for automating entropy inequality proofs, with highlighted failure modes and insights into optimal configurations.

Abstract: Proving Shannon-type entropy inequalities is a fundamental task in information theory that often requires constructing non-trivial linear combinations of known constraints, which is a combinatorial search problem that scales poorly with the number of random variables. We investigate whether small-scale large language models (0.6B--1.7B parameters), fine-tuned on atomic proof steps and combined with guided beam search, can automate this process. On a held-out test set of 60 inequalities spanning n=10 to 15 variables, our 0.6B fine-tuned model achieves an 85\% proof success rate with tree search. GPT-5.5 solves 1.7\% samples under zero-shot prompting while Psitip solves 33.3\% samples. A systematic ablation study across training context length (4096 vs.\ 8192 tokens) and data distribution (n=9-skewed vs not skewed) reveals that a 4096-token not skewed training distribution yields the best performance, with extended context and skewed data providing no marginal benefit. We further identify two dominant failure modes -- format failures and step quality degradation -- and verify that the beam-scoring heuristic is essential via a controlled ablation (random scoring reduces success from 83\% to 23\%).

</details>


### [606] [Compositional Boundaries for Density Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05871)
*Ratan Bahadur Thapa,Ali Darijani,Jürgen Beyerer,Steffen Staab*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper examines how distributed uncertainty-management systems can hierarchically fuse probability densities in an order-invariant manner, identifying compositional constraints and algebraic conditions for this process.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The research seeks to ensure that distributed systems using probabilistic models aggregate information in a way that is independent of the order of combination, addressing concerns about communication, privacy, and scheduling constraints.

Method: The authors investigate algebraic compositionality for the hierarchical fusion of density functions, focusing on binary fusion rules, additive weights, and geometric and divergence-based considerations. Mathematical frameworks like $f$-divergence balancing and Gaussian mixtures are analyzed.

Result: The study establishes when binary fusion rules allow order-invariant hierarchical execution, with normalized weighted linear pooling emerging as a central method. It also highlights the limitations of local balancing methods in achieving global compositional objectives.

Conclusion: Compositional, order-invariant fusion can be precisely achieved under specific conditions, with implications for reconciling local heuristics and global aggregation goals in uncertainty-management systems.

Abstract: Distributed uncertainty-management systems often combine local probabilistic models along aggregation trees chosen by communication, privacy, or scheduling constraints. The final density should depend on the weighted sources, not on the particular order in which intermediate nodes combine them. We study this requirement as an algebraic compositionality problem for binary fusion of weighted probability densities. The central question is when a local fusion rule can be executed hierarchically while remaining order-invariant. We establish a compositional boundary for local segment-valued fusion rules. Within the class of continuous binary rules with additive output weights and weight-only coefficients, order-invariant hierarchical execution characterizes normalized weighted linear pooling; norm-induced segment balancing realizes the corresponding coefficient. Smooth endpoint-to-candidate $f$-divergence balancing has a different local geometry: its quadratic expansion induces square-root effective weights, showing why pairwise solvability alone is insufficient for schedule-independent fusion. We show that this obstruction is local to endpoint-to-candidate binary balancing, whereas global divergence barycenters retain additive-weight local limits. Finally, Gaussian mixtures show how the same issue appears in finite model classes: exact fusion is compositional, whereas stepwise compression is compositional only under a congruence condition on unnormalized component measures. These results distinguish exact schedule-independent fusion from global aggregation objectives and local approximation heuristics.

</details>


### [607] [Adapting Diffusion Language Models for Lossless Pixel-Level Image Transmission](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06273)
*Tianqi Ren,Rongpeng Li,Xianfu Chen,Yingyu Li,Zhifeng Zhao*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper presents DDM-SSCC, a novel framework using discrete diffusion models for lossless image transmission, achieving state-of-the-art performance over noisy channels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of lossless pixel-level image recovery, which requires both accurate symbol probability modeling and reliable communication over noisy channels.

Method: Proposes a unique source-channel coding framework (DDM-SSCC) that uses a diffusion language model for progressive pixel-token restoration, synchronized reverse arithmetic coding, bidirectional attention, and introduces specific enhancements like Halton-guided denoising order, mask-ratio-aware cosine schedules, and temperature calibration.

Result: The proposed method outperforms existing lossless and semantic communication approaches in exact image recovery across various datasets and noise channels. Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of the introduced enhancements.

Conclusion: DDM-SSCC demonstrates that discrete diffusion models, with tailored enhancements, offer significant advancements in lossless image transmission under noisy channel conditions.

Abstract: Lossless pixel-level image transmission is a fundamental regime beyond semantic communications, because exact recovery requires both accurate symbol probability modeling and reliable delivery over noisy channels. This paper proposes DDM-SSCC, a discrete-diffusion-model-based separate source-channel coding framework for lossless image transmission. Different from raster-order autoregressive coding, the proposed source codec adapts a diffusion language model to pixel-token restoration and performs synchronized reverse arithmetic coding under bidirectional attention, allowing multiple masked tokens to be coded within one reverse denoising step. This progressive restoration process also yields a more favorable source representation for noisy transmission, since newly restored tokens can serve as bidirectional context in subsequent denoising steps. To bridge the gap between generation-oriented masked denoising and lossless arithmetic coding, we further introduce a Halton-guided denoising order, a mask-ratio-aware cosine schedule, and a lightweight temperature calibration module. These designs respectively improve spatial coverage, adapt the denoising pace to context reliability, and calibrate the probability tables used by arithmetic coding. Experiments on CIFAR10, DIV2K-LR-X4, and Kodak over additive white Gaussian noise and Rayleigh fading channels show that DDM-SSCC achieves better exact-recovery performance than representative lossless and semantic communication baselines, while ablation studies verify the effectiveness of the proposed denoising order, schedule, and calibration modules.

</details>


<div id='cs.GT'></div>

# cs.GT [[Back]](#toc)

### [608] [Should Demand Models Incorporate Competitor Prices? Oblivious Learning and Algorithmic Collusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05363)
*Yuhang Wu,Assaf Zeevi*

Main category: cs.GT

TL;DR: The paper examines the strategic decision of whether pricing algorithms should explicitly model competitors' prices in competitive markets, highlighting that incorporating competitor information leads to better profits and efficient competition, while ignoring competitor prices results in temporary collusion-like phenomena but is not robust.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation arises from the conflicting strategies in algorithmic pricing: classical belief in incorporating competitors' prices for efficiency versus recent literature suggesting that ignoring competitors may enhance collusion and profits.

Method: The authors study this issue in a stylized competitive market with multiple sellers who use iterated least squares to learn demand under two conditions: incorporating competitor prices (informed) or ignoring them (oblivious). They explore market dynamics and price evolution based on these strategies.

Result: Oblivious sellers must explore prices more aggressively to compensate for lacking competitor insights. Collusive behavior emerges temporarily but dissipates as learning progresses. Markets with informed sellers out-perform those with oblivious sellers. The all-informed strategy becomes the unique Nash equilibrium, driving efficient competitive pricing.

Conclusion: Collusive patterns are transient and not sustainable under oblivious modeling. Incorporating competitor information, coupled with sufficient price exploration, remains the optimal strategy for sellers seeking long-term success in competitive markets.

Abstract: On a platform with many sellers, should a pricing algorithm explicitly model competitors' prices when learning demand?
