Classical learning arguments suggest an affirmative answer: ignoring competitors induces model misspecification and inefficiency. In contrast, recent work on algorithmic collusion suggests that strategic obliviousness -- deliberately ignoring competitor prices -- may facilitate collusive outcomes and improve profits. We study this modeling choice in a stylized competitive market with unknown noisy demand, in which multiple sellers repeatedly set prices and estimate demand via iterated least squares, and either incorporate competitors' prices into their demand models (informed) or ignore them (oblivious). We first show that, relative to a monopolist, an oblivious seller in a competitive market must explore more aggressively to compensate for the loss of dynamic competitor information. Building on this insight, we characterize market dynamics when all sellers are oblivious and show that prices converge to the competitive outcome under sufficient exploration, while a continuum of pseudo-equilibria arises when exploration decays. Analyzing the resulting price trajectories, we uncover an excursion phenomenon that gives rise to transient collusive patterns that dissipate as learning progresses. In markets with both oblivious and informed sellers, the informed strictly out-earn the oblivious. Read as a strategy game, the modeling choice has a unique Nash equilibrium: the all-informed market, in which prices converge to the competitive outcome efficiently. Overall, our results indicate that collusive patterns are not robust and are not sustained by oblivious modeling; therefore, incorporating competitor information, together with sufficient price exploration, remains a reliable strategy for sellers in competitive markets.

</details>


### [609] [DNQ: Deep Nash Q-Network for Partially Observable n-Player Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06480)
*Qintong Xie,Edward Koh,Xavier Cadet,Peter Chin*

Main category: cs.GT

TL;DR: The paper introduces DNQ, a framework for training agents in multi-turn simultaneous bidding through equilibrium-based supervision, focusing on scalability and computational efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of training decision-making agents in competitive scenarios involving shared constraints, limited information, and repeated interaction, such as auctions and resource allocation.

Method: The DNQ framework alternates between trajectory collection, critic-based payoff estimation, equilibrium computation, and policy imitation. It uses a shared critic for payoff evaluation and proposes a scalable pairwise formulation to reduce computational costs.

Result: The pairwise method enables scalability to a larger number of agents while the exact method becomes computationally infeasible in larger games. Comparisons based on critic loss, policy entropy, and training cost demonstrate this trade-off.

Conclusion: The DNQ framework is effective for training agents in competitive, repeated game environments, balancing strategic fidelity with scalability by leveraging the pairwise approach.

Abstract: Many real-world competitive systems require multiple decision-makers to act simultaneously under shared constraints, limited information, and repeated interaction, as in auctions, resource allocation, and security competition. We study multi-turn simultaneous bidding as a controlled testbed for such problems and propose DNQ, a solver-in-the-loop equilibrium supervision framework for training bidding agents. DNQ alternates between trajectory collection, critic-based payoff estimation, equilibrium computation, and policy imitation. At each visited state, a shared critic predicts either pairwise payoff matrices or an exact N-player payoff tensor, an external solver computes equilibrium strategies, and the agents are trained by minimizing the KL divergence between their masked policies and the solver-derived equilibrium targets. We focus on a scalable pairwise formulation that greatly reduces equilibrium-solving cost and training time compared with the exact formulation, while the shared critic amortizes payoff learning across agents and states. Experiments compare the pairwise and exact variants using critic loss, policy entropy, bidding resource usage, and training cost, showing that the pairwise method scales to larger numbers of agents, whereas the exact method becomes computationally impractical as the joint game grows. These results illustrate the trade-off between strategic fidelity and scalability in repeated competitive environments.

</details>


<div id='cs.ET'></div>

# cs.ET [[Back]](#toc)

### [610] [Wave Focusing in Metamaterials: Tactile Displays Beyond the Diffraction Limit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05572)
*Gregory Reardon,Max Linnander,Dustin Goetz,Neeli Tummala,Yon Visell*

Main category: cs.ET

TL;DR: This paper presents a technique for creating high-resolution distributed haptic displays using mechanical wave focusing on a metamaterial plate with resonators. It achieves precise tactile feedback for multi-point interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to overcome the challenge of producing high-resolution distributed haptic feedback for multi-digit touch interactions, which is limited by wave diffraction at tactile frequencies.

Method: The authors utilize a metamaterial approach by embedding resonators into a flexural plate. This alters the wave dispersion properties, allowing for slow-wave focusing beyond the diffraction limit. Numerical simulations are used to design the system, and a prototype tactile display is fabricated for experimental validation.

Result: The fabricated metamaterial display reduces the virtual pixel area by 10 times compared to systems without resonators. It provides localized and independent tactile feedback across multiple points with high precision.

Conclusion: The proposed technique demonstrates a robust methodology for achieving perceptually high-resolution haptic feedback with fewer actuators. This paves the way for advanced haptic interfaces with diverse applications.

Abstract: We address the challenge of engineering distributed haptic displays capable of reproducing multiple localized, independently addressable vibrations -- representing virtual tactile pixels -- at arbitrary locations on a surface. Our technique is based on the focusing of mechanical waves in a flexural plate using a sparse set of actuators. At tactile frequencies, wave diffraction prevents the formation of localized virtual tactile pixels at spatial scales relevant for multi-digit touch interactions. We overcome this limitation by augmenting the plate with a lattice of mechanical resonators, forming a locally resonant metamaterial plate. Coupling between the plate's dynamic modes and those of the resonators alters the dispersion relation governing wave transmission, introducing a slow-wave branch that enables focusing beyond the diffraction limit imposed by the unmodified plate. We use numerical simulations to engineer the dispersion relation of the metamaterial system for high-resolution focusing at tactile frequencies. We then fabricate a metamaterial tactile display and experimentally demonstrate virtual pixels that are far more localized than those generated on an otherwise identical plate without resonators, resulting in a tenfold reduction in virtual-pixel area. In behavioral experiments, we show that this system can deliver perceptually localized single- and multi-point tactile feedback and moving tactile sources while maintaining independent control over temporal waveforms at multiple display locations. The methods reported here can enable high-resolution haptic displays for widespread applications using a small number of actuated degrees of freedom.

</details>


<div id='math.ST'></div>

# math.ST [[Back]](#toc)

### [611] [Bentkus-type asymptotic e-values](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06332)
*Diego Martinez-Taboada,Ben Chugg,Aaditya Ramdas*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: This paper introduces Bentkus-type asymptotic e-values to improve efficiency in statistical inference, addressing limitations in existing e-values.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing asymptotic e-values are limited by a "missing factor," which causes them to be overly conservative in statistical inference.

Method: The authors utilize near-optimal concentration inequalities developed by Bentkus to eliminate the missing factor and develop Bentkus-type asymptotic e-values.

Result: Theoretical and empirical evaluations show that Bentkus-type e-values outperform existing methods by providing sharper inference, tighter confidence intervals, and higher rejection rates in multiple testing.

Conclusion: Bentkus-type asymptotic e-values represent a significant improvement in statistical inference, particularly for post-hoc analysis and multiple testing, and address key inefficiencies in prior approaches.

Abstract: Asymptotic e-values are emerging as a powerful alternative to asymptotic p-values, particularly in post-hoc inference and multiple testing, where significance levels may be data-dependent. Existing asymptotic e-values, however, suffer from the ``missing factor,'' a scaling inefficiency resulting in overly conservative inference. Drawing on the framework of near-optimal concentration inequalities developed by Bentkus in the 2000s, we introduce Bentkus-type asymptotic e-values and prove that they successfully eliminate the missing factor. We also demonstrate both theoretically and empirically that Bentkus-type e-values consistently deliver sharper inference than existing alternatives, leading to tighter post-hoc confidence intervals and higher rejection rates in multiple testing procedures.

</details>


### [612] [Optimally taming biases in black-box models for efficient semiparametric estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06368)
*Yihong Gu,Qishuo Yin,Tianxi Cai,Jianqing Fan*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: This paper challenges the optimality of prevalent error bounds in double machine learning (DML) for semiparametric estimation and proposes sharper estimation rates for improving results, especially when nuisance function estimation struggles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to understand and improve how errors from nuisance function estimation affect inference for low-dimensional parameters in semiparametric estimation, particularly in scenarios using black-box machine learning models.

Method: The paper revisits the partial linear model and introduces a new estimator for the target parameter β_0, achieving improved error rates. It proposes a novel tuning strategy to minimize stochastic error without classical bias-variance trade-offs.

Result: The proposed estimator achieves a rate of $n^{-1/2}+δ^a_μ+(δ_μ^s)^2$, which is sharper compared to the standard DML rate. Authors establish its optimality via a matching lower bound and demonstrate asymptotic normality with minimal variance.

Conclusion: The study reveals that first-order stochastic errors in nuisance estimation can be mitigated under structure-agnostic settings, enabling improved semiparametric inference and setting a new benchmark for tuning strategies and estimation methods.

Abstract: Modern semiparametric estimation often relies on flexible black-box machine learning methods to estimate nuisance functions, raising a fundamental question: how do nuisance estimation errors propagate into inference for low-dimensional target parameters? The dominant paradigm, exemplified by double machine learning (DML), yields error bounds in which nuisance estimation errors enter multiplicatively. While widely adopted, it remains unclear whether this multiplicative-rate dependence is optimal for black-box models. In this paper, we start by revisiting the partial linear model $Y = μ_0(X)+T\cdotβ_0+\varepsilon$ under a structure-agnostic setting, where the nuisance function $μ_0$ is estimated using a generic machine learning model, with approximation error $δ^a_μ$ and stochastic error $δ_μ^s$. We show that the standard DML rate is not optimal in the regime where the auxiliary function $\mathbb{E}[T|X=x]$ cannot be consistently estimated. We propose a new estimator for $β_0$ that achieves a sharper rate of $n^{-1/2}+δ^a_μ+(δ_μ^s)^2$ and establish a matching lower bound demonstrating its optimality. Our results reveal a new principle: the first-order stochastic error of nuisance estimation can be eliminated without imposing any additional assumptions. This also leads to a revised tuning strategy favoring under-smoothing, where $δ^a_μ\asymp(δ_μ^s)^2$, rather than the classical bias-variance trade-off $δ^a_μ\asymp δ_μ^s$. Under mild additional conditions, the estimator is asymptotically normal with minimal asymptotic variance. The proposed method extends to a broad class of semi-parametric linear functional estimation problems, including average treatment effect estimation. Our results imply that popular orthogonal score methods in semiparametric estimation with black-box nuisance learners can be substantially improved.

</details>


### [613] [Estimation of the sub-Gaussian parameter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06384)
*Jason Liu,Min Xu,Jinchuan Xing*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: The paper develops and analyzes an estimator for the sub-Gaussian parameter $ξ^2_*$. The estimator is consistent with provable convergence rates under certain assumptions, and its limitations are explored. Finally, the method is applied in a real-world Gene Ontology study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Estimating the sub-Gaussian parameter $ξ^2_*$ is crucial for working with sub-Gaussian random variables, yet prior research on this topic is scarce, highlighting the need for a deeper understanding and practical estimators.

Method: The authors introduce an estimator for $ξ^2_*$ based on constrained maximization of an empirical cumulant generating function. They analyze its statistical properties, establish bounds on convergence rates under different assumptions, and explore theoretical limitations.

Result: The estimator is shown to be consistent with convergence rates of $O_p(n^{-1/2 + ε})$ under general conditions and $O_p(n^{-1/2})$ under stricter conditions. Lower bounds on minimax risk are derived, showing the necessity of certain assumptions. The estimator also diverges predictably for non-sub-Gaussian distributions.

Conclusion: The proposed estimator is reliable for sub-Gaussian parameter estimation, achieving minimax optimality under bounded maximal assumptions. It also has practical applicability, as demonstrated in a Gene Ontology enrichment study, where it outperforms alternative methods in certain scenarios.

Abstract: The sub-Gaussian parameter (also called the variance proxy) of a mean-zero random variable $X$ is defined as $ξ^2_* = \sup_{λ\in \mathbb{R}} L(λ)$ where $L(λ) = \frac{2}{λ^2} \log \mathbb{E} e^{λX}$ is a weighted cumulant generating function. Despite the ubiquity of sub-Gaussian random variables, the estimation of $ξ^2_*$ has received little attention and is not yet well understood. In this work, we study a natural estimator of $ξ^2_*$ based on constrained maximization of the empirical analogue of $L$. We prove that the estimator is consistent bound the rates of convergence under assumptions on $L$: if $L$ has an maximizer, then our bound is $O_p(n^{-1/2 + \varepsilon})$ for any $\varepsilon > 0$; if the argmax of $L$ is also bounded, then the bound improves to $O_p(n^{-1/2})$. We show that our assumptions on $L$ are necessary by proving that the minimax risk over all sub-Gaussian distributions is $Ω(1)$; imposing increasingly strong assumptions on the tail growth of $L$ yields a continuum of classes whose minimax lower bound interpolates between $Ω(1/\log n)$ and $Ω(1)$. Root-n rate is possible if we restrict to a subclass of distributions where $L$ attains its supremum in a bounded region, in which case our estimator is minimax optimal. If the underlying distribution is not sub-Gaussian, we show that our estimator goes to infinity with a divergence rate controlled by the tail of the distribution. Finally, we apply our estimator in a Gene Ontology (GO) enrichment study to construct p-values for a large-scale permutation test, showing that it can serve as a reliable alternative to the peaks-over-threshold approach, particularly in regimes where the peaks-over-threshold method is of uncertain validity.

</details>


### [614] [How abundant are good interpolators?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06469)
*August Y. Chen,Ahmed El Alaoui*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: This paper presents a probabilistic analysis of large deviations in generalization error achieved by interpolating classifiers under certain theoretical models, providing insights into the typical performance of such classifiers in high-dimensional, over-parameterized regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to better understand the generalization properties of interpolating classifiers, particularly in the context of over-parameterized models where the number of data points is small relative to the feature dimensions.

Method: Using a large deviation principle, the study models interpolating classifiers under Gaussian mixture and logistic data distributions in the proportional regime (when the number of data points relative to dimensions is small). This provides a deterministic rate function describing the generalization performance distribution.

Result: The study reveals that the majority of interpolating classifiers' generalization performance concentrates around a specific value determined by the rate function, with exponentially small deviations. Efficient methods like gradient descent outperform the typical interpolators in this regime.

Conclusion: In the over-parameterized regime, efficient classifier training techniques lead to better generalization than the vast majority of uniformly random interpolators, suggesting these methods achieve benign overfitting.

Abstract: Let $S$ be the set of unit norm linear classifiers $θ\in \mathbb{R}^d$ which correctly classify every point of a labeled dataset $(X_i,y_i)_{i=1}^n$, $X_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$, $y_i \in \{-1,+1\}$, with a possibly negative margin $κ$ fixed in advance. Under two natural data-generating distributions of the $(X,y)$ pairs -- a Gaussian mixture model and a logistic model with Gaussian features -- and in the proportional regime $n/d \to α$ with small enough $α$, we establish a large deviation principle on the event that a point $θ$ chosen uniformly at random from $S$ achieves a given generalization error, with high probability over the choice of the data. The associated large deviation rate function is deterministic and describes the proportion, at the exponential scale in $d$, of interpolating classifiers having a given desired performance. As a consequence, we establish the following concentration phenomenon: all but an exponentially small fraction of interpolating classifiers have approximately the same generalization performance given by the unique maximizer of this rate function.
  We numerically compare this maximizer to the performance of empirical risk minimization by gradient descent and to the performance of a natural linear program, both finding a point in $S$, and deduce that in the overparametrized regime of small $α$, these efficient procedures outperform the vast majority of interpolators, pointing to their nontrivial benign overfitting in this setting.

</details>


<div id='cs.GR'></div>

# cs.GR [[Back]](#toc)

### [615] [The Invisible Hand of Physics: When Video Diffusion Models Know More Than They Show](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05328)
*Parsa Esmati,Somjit Nath,Katja Hofmann,Derek Nowrouzezahrai,Samira Ebrahimi Kahou,Majid Mirmehdi*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether modern video diffusion models inherently encode physical structure or simply replicate motion patterns by studying latent trajectories from real videos with known physical plausibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore if video diffusion models encode inherent physical structures instead of merely mimicking motion patterns observed during training.

Method: The authors invert the deterministic sampling process of video diffusion models using a backward integration of their velocity fields, analyze intermediate states, and assess physical plausibility through decodable features.

Result: Physical plausibility representations are decodable from model states with around 81.27% accuracy, surpassing representation-learning baselines, and this signal emerges solely within the denoising transformer.

Conclusion: Physically meaningful representations can naturally emerge during generative denoising, even without explicit predictive training objectives.

Abstract: Modern video diffusion models generate increasingly realistic and temporally coherent videos, motivating their use as candidate world simulators. Yet it remains unclear whether these models internally encode physical structure, or merely reproduce motion patterns seen during training. We study this question by probing video diffusion models along latent trajectories corresponding to real videos with known physical plausibility. To obtain such trajectories, we approximately invert the deterministic sampling process by integrating the learned velocity field backward from a clean video latent to noise, giving access to the model's intermediate states and attention maps. Using these recovered trajectories, we show that physical plausibility is linearly decodable from diffusion transformer states across IntPhys and InfLevel, reaching around 81.27% average accuracy and outperforming dedicated representation-learning baselines such as V-JEPA and VideoMAE. Surprisingly, this signal is absent from the VAE latent input and emerges inside the denoising transformer itself, despite the model not being trained with a self-supervised predictive objective. These findings suggest that physically meaningful representations can arise as a byproduct of generative denoising.

</details>


### [616] [Monte Carlo Steklov Operators for Large-Scale Geometry Processing in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05581)
*Arman Maesumi,Tanish Makadia,Aruna Anderson,Oras Phongpanangam,Justin Solomon,Daniel Ritchie*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: This paper introduces a fast and robust Monte Carlo method for computing the Dirichlet-to-Neumann operator and its eigenmodes, enabling scalable and isometry-invariant geometry processing for meshes with challenging characteristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Intrinsic geometry processing methods struggle under real-world conditions where mesh quality is poor or geometries have multiple disconnected components. There is a need to develop more robust methods using volumetric constructions.

Method: The authors propose a Monte Carlo method to compute the Dirichlet-to-Neumann operator and its eigenmodes (Steklov spectra), enabling it to handle poor triangulations, high-resolution meshes, and multi-component geometries. They generalize the operator to external domains for broader applicability.

Result: The method is several orders of magnitude faster than traditional boundary-element methods for Steklov spectral computation. It is demonstrated on approximately 450,000 shapes in the Objaverse dataset, showcasing its scalability and robustness.

Conclusion: The proposed Monte Carlo method enables practical and scalable volumetric spectral geometry processing. Integration into the Steklov-CLIP neural network highlights its utility for robust 3D representation learning across large modern datasets.

Abstract: Intrinsic methods fill the default toolbox for geometry processing on meshes. Intrinsic operators, in particular the Laplacian, underlie methods that require invariance to isometry and have hence been employed in many algorithms for shape analysis, learning, and editing. However, intrinsic methods are predicated on assumptions that quickly become brittle when working with in-the-wild geometry, where (i) mesh quality is not guaranteed, and (ii) many meshes are modeled with multiple connected components. In such settings, volumetric constructions are better-defined, since restrictions on surface topology can be relaxed. This paper presents a Monte Carlo method for estimating the Dirichlet-to-Neumann (DtN) operator -- a boundary-to-boundary volumetric operator -- and its associated Steklov eigenmodes. We build on recent developments in Monte Carlo geometry processing by casting this boundary operator itself as the subject of estimation. The DtN operator, defined through a volumetric stochastic process, is then generalized to the exterior domain, where it couples disconnected components through the surrounding ambient space. We show that our method is orders of magnitude faster than existing boundary-element approaches for computing Steklov spectra while remaining robust to poor triangulations, high-resolution meshes, and multi-component geometry. To demonstrate this scalability, we compute interior and exterior Steklov eigenspectra for approximately 450,000 shapes from the uncurated Objaverse dataset. We incorporate these operators into Steklov-CLIP, a mesh-based neural network that uses volumetric spectral operators for large-scale contrastive 3D representation learning. The resulting network learns semantically meaningful global and dense shape representations, illustrating that geometrically-principled volumetric operators can be made practical at the scale of modern 3D datasets.

</details>


### [617] [Aggregating LLM-Based Weak Verifiers for Spatial Layout Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05268)
*Sharon Zhang,R. Kenny Jones,Jiajun Wu,Maneesh Agrawala*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: The paper introduces a pipeline for creating strong verifiers by aggregating weak, task-specific verifiers generated by Large Language Models (LLMs), significantly improving spatial layout task accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to overcome the limitations of imperfect LLM-generated verifiers by employing weak learning methods to aggregate them into stronger verifiers for spatial layout tasks.

Method: The pipeline generates weak verifier programs using a layout-specific DSL via LLMs, and these verifiers are aggregated using weak learning techniques, leveraging minimal human-labeled examples (around 10).

Result: The strong verifiers built with this pipeline drastically outperformed existing LLM-based judging approaches, boosting F1-scores by up to 7X and improving layout generation from natural language feedback by up to 66.2%.

Conclusion: By combining weak verifier outputs and leveraging sparse human labels, the pipeline provides a scalable and effective solution for spatial layout verification and significantly enhances layout quality.

Abstract: We present a pipeline for building and aggregating task-specific, LLM-generated weak (imperfect) verifiers into a strong verifier for spatial layout domains. Given a task description, our pipeline asks an LLM to synthesize a collection of verifier programs using a layout verification DSL. Each individual LLM-generated verifier usually provides an imperfect check for a match between the layout and the corresponding task description. We show that by aggregating the responses of many such verifiers we can produce a stronger verifier. Moreover, by applying techniques from weak learning, our pipeline can learn how to aggregate the weak verifiers from a very sparse set of human labeled example layouts (about 10). We find that the strong verifiers produced by our pipeline outperform the status-quo approach of using a set of LLM judges to directly check whether a layout matches a task description, raising F1-scores by up to 7X across a variety of 3D room layout and 2D poster design tasks. We also demonstrate that verifier-guided layout generation using natural language feedback from our strong verifiers improves layout quality of a base layout generator by up to 66.2% according to a human evaluator.

</details>


<div id='physics.optics'></div>

# physics.optics [[Back]](#toc)

### [618] [Inverse Design of Realizable Metasurface based Absorbers using Improved Conditioning and Diversity Enhanced Progressively Growing GANs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05849)
*Vineetha Joy,Mohammad Abdullah,Pramit Pal,Anshuman Kumar,Amit Sethi,Hema Singh*

Main category: physics.optics

TL;DR: This paper introduces a novel generative inverse design framework for metasurfaces, achieving high accuracy and diversity under continuous spectral constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Controlling electromagnetic (EM) waves with metasurfaces is transformative for applications like beam steering and sensing, but generating designs that meet precise EM responses is challenging due to computational costs, fidelity issues, and lack of diversity in current methods.

Method: The approach uses a progressively growing Wasserstein GAN with gradient penalty and feature-wise linear modulation to enforce spectral and fabrication consistency, alongside surrogate-assisted spectral alignment and determinantal point process for diversity regulation.

Result: The framework generates diverse, electromagnetically consistent metasurface absorbers for the frequency range of 2-18 GHz, achieving an average mean squared error of 0.0052 and an EM design generation validity of 89.57%.

Conclusion: This method enables accurate, diverse, and fabrication-realizable metasurface designs, effectively addressing limitations of existing inverse design techniques.

Abstract: Metasurfaces enable precise manipulation of electromagnetic waves for applications such as beam steering, sensing, and stealth technology. However, inverse design of metasurfaces with targeted EM responses remains challenging due to the computational expense of iterative full wave simulation driven optimization and the limited conditioning fidelity and diversity of existing generative approaches. To address these challenges, this paper presents a generative inverse design framework for controllable and physically consistent metasurface synthesis under continuous spectral constraints. The proposed approach employs a progressively growing Wasserstein generative adversarial network with gradient penalty integrated with feature wise linear modulation based conditioning for stable propagation of continuous spectral and fabrication constraints. EM consistency is embedded directly into the generative learning process through a surrogate assisted spectral alignment loss, enabling physics constrained generation during training. Further, a determinantal point process based diversity regularization strategy is incorporated to generate geometrically diverse yet spectrally consistent realizations for the same target response. The effectiveness of the proposed framework is demonstrated through the generation of practically realizable metasurface absorbers exhibiting diverse reflection characteristics in the frequency range of 2 to 18 GHz. EM simulations validate that the generated designs meet the target specifications with high accuracy. The final proposed framework achieved an average mean squared error of 0.0052, diversity score of 0.8730, band alignment accuracy of 0.8533, and a valid EM design generation percentage of 89.57, clearly demonstrating its capability to generate highly accurate, diverse, electromagnetically consistent and fabrication realizable metasurface configurations.

</details>


<div id='math.DS'></div>

# math.DS [[Back]](#toc)

### [619] [A Two-Channel F-Transform Representation for Early Trajectory Characterization in Iterated Correlation Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05462)
*Ishrak Alhajj Hassan*

Main category: math.DS

TL;DR: The paper presents a method to transform short early segments of nonlinear iterative trajectories into compact, interpretable fuzzy coordinates for predicting convergence and trajectory geometry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of comparing and interpreting high-dimensional trajectories from nonlinear iterative processes, especially for their convergence and geometry.

Method: The authors used a new descriptor based on logarithmic signals (step-size and contraction ratio) projected onto fuzzy partitions using zero- and first-degree F-transform coefficients. This yields an eight-dimensional representation tested for predicting convergence-length approximation.

Result: The descriptor achieves a mean R^2 score of 0.6480, close to raw trajectories (0.6518) and is shown to be stable across experiments. PCA explains 84.26% variance, and clustering supports reproducible low-dimensional organization.

Conclusion: The proposed fuzzy coordinate system provides a compact, interpretable, and effective representation of trajectory dynamics that compares favorably with raw data and statistical summaries.

Abstract: Many nonlinear iterative procedures generate high-dimensional trajectories whose early behavior is informative but difficult to compare directly. This paper studies a soft-computing representation problem: how to convert a short early trajectory segment into compact, interpretable, fixed-dimensional fuzzy coordinates that preserve information about subsequent convergence and trajectory geometry. The problem is investigated for iterated Pearson correlation matrices, a nonlinear matrix iteration historically connected with CONCOR-type blockmodeling and repeated-correlation methods. The proposed descriptor uses two logarithmic signals from the early post-transient regime: a step-size signal, measuring contraction magnitude, and a contraction-ratio signal, measuring local contraction evolution. Each signal is projected onto a three-node triangular fuzzy partition using zero-degree F-transform coefficients and one centered first-degree coefficient. This yields an eight-dimensional two-channel representation separating local level from local trend and contraction magnitude from contraction evolution. Across 22 matrix dimensions with 1000 trajectories per dimension, the descriptor is compared with raw trajectory samples, statistical summaries, and PCA-compressed raw features using Random Forest regression for convergence-length approximation. It achieves mean R^2 = 0.6480, close to raw trajectories (0.6518) and statistical summaries (0.6528), while improving over the step-size-only F-transform descriptor (0.5001). Repeated random-split and shifted-window experiments confirm stability. PCA and clustering further show reproducible low-dimensional organization, with the first two principal components explaining 84.26% of variance and k = 3 favored by the mean silhouette criterion.

</details>


<div id='math.NA'></div>

# math.NA [[Back]](#toc)

### [620] [DAS-PINNs for high-dimensional partial differential equations: extending deep adaptive sampling to spacetime domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06314)
*Anshima Singh,David J. Silvester*

Main category: math.NA

TL;DR: This paper addresses the challenge of solving time-dependent, high-dimensional PDEs with spatially localized, dynamic solutions using PINNs. It introduces an adaptive sampling framework that relies on a normalizing flow network to focus on high-residual regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Solving high-dimensional PDEs with spatially localized, dynamic solutions with traditional uniform sampling fails in effectively capturing solution dynamics, especially in high-dimensional spatiotemporal domains.

Method: The proposed approach treats space and time as a unified domain, abandoning explicit time marching. A normalizing flow neural network predicts PDE residual distributions, enabling focused sampling in areas where the solution is hardest to approximate.

Result: The strategy demonstrated effectiveness in solving benchmark PDE problems with features like sharp gradients, moving structures, and localized dynamics in up to eight spatial dimensions.

Conclusion: The study provides a generalized and adaptive framework for addressing spatiotemporal dynamics, demonstrating improvements in the capacity of PINNs to resolve complex, high-dimensional PDE solutions.

Abstract: Time-dependent high-dimensional partial differential equations (PDEs) with spatially localised and dynamically evolving solutions pose a fundamental challenge for physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), as uniform collocation sampling becomes increasingly ineffective in high-dimensional spatiotemporal domains. In this work, a deep adaptive sampling framework for PINNs is extended to the time-dependent setting by treating space and time as a unified domain without any explicit time marching. A normalising flow neural network model effectively learns the distribution induced by the PDE residual and generates new collocation points concentrated in regions where the solution is most difficult to learn. Unlike conventional adaptive strategies that require explicit time stepping or moving meshes, high-residual regions are automatically identified and tracked across both space and time, driven purely by the PDE residual distribution. The effectiveness of the proposed strategy is assessed on a range of benchmark problems, from sharp and moving features in two spatial dimensions to localised structures in up to eight spatial dimensions.

</details>


### [621] [Learning solution operators of PDEs with sparse approximation methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06046)
*Sebastian Neumayer,Daniel Potts,Fabian Taubert*

Main category: math.NA

TL;DR: The paper proposes a sparse high-dimensional technique using orthogonal matching pursuit (OMP) for approximating PDE solution operators, reducing sample size while maintaining competitive accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the efficiency and reduce sample size for approximating PDE solution operators while providing interpretable results.

Method: Combines dimension-incremental framework, product basis expansions, and orthogonal matching pursuit (OMP) for sparse recovery.

Result: Numerical experiments demonstrate reduced PDE solves compared to previous cubature-based methods and competitive accuracy, especially for solutions with sparse basis representation.

Conclusion: This approach enhances efficiency, interprets variable interactions, and reduces computational resource requirements, making it a promising alternative for PDE approximation.

Abstract: We investigate the approximation of solution operators for partial differential equations (PDEs) using sparse high-dimensional techniques. Building on a dimension-incremental framework, we combine product basis expansions with sparse recovery methods, specifically orthogonal matching pursuit (OMP), to substantially reduce the required sample size compared with a previously considered cubature-based approach. We evaluate the resulting method numerically on several examples, comparing it against both cubature-based sparse approximation and Fourier neural operators in terms of accuracy, runtime, and sample size. The experiments show that our approach considerably reduces the number of required PDE solves relative to its predecessor while maintaining competitive accuracy, particularly when the solution admits a sparse representation in the chosen basis. Furthermore, the recovered sparse index sets yield interpretable insights into the relevant variables and parameter interactions.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [622] [Transformer-Enhanced Reinforcement Learning: Fundamentals and Applications in Communication Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05208)
*Nguyen Cong Luong,Shaohan Feng,Nguyen Duc Hai,Zeping Sui,Bo Ma,Min Xu,Zhihao Dong,Qiushi Zhao,Nguyen Duc Duy Anh,Nguyen Quoc Khanh,Ngoc Hung Nguyen,Zitian Zhang,Jie Cao*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper surveys the use of Transformer-based reinforcement learning (RL) in communication networks, highlighting its advantages in addressing traditional RL limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the limitations of traditional RL models, such as reliance on extensive environment interactions, difficulties in modeling long-term dependencies, and handling partial observability.

Method: It reviews the integration of Transformer models into RL frameworks for applications in communication networks, focusing on mathematical background, key challenges, and practical applications in network systems.

Result: Transformer-enhanced RL provides solutions to resource allocation, routing, network security, and other issues in communication networks while addressing traditional RL limitations.

Conclusion: Transformer models significantly enhance RL's capabilities by addressing long-term dependencies and partial observability, paving the way for efficient applications in communication networks and opening up future research opportunities in network optimization.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has long been a powerful solution to various problems in communication networks. However, traditional RL models still face with several limitations. Not only do they rely on large numbers of interactions with the environment, but they are also limited in terms of modeling long-term relationships and tackling partial observability. In recent years, the Transformer model has demonstrated the ability to enhance RL models, allowing them to overcome these issues. Particularly, the self-attention mechanism within the Transformer enables efficient modeling of long-range dependencies and global correlations, as well as accelerates training processes and handles heterogeneous data modalities. In this paper, we present a comprehensive survey of Transformer-based RL algorithms and their applications in communication networks. Specifically, the paper provides the mathematical background of RL and Transformer architectures, along with insights into key issues such as resource allocation, computation offloading, routing, and trajectory control, and network security. We conclude the paper by discussing challenges, open issues, and notable future research directions, including Transformer-enhanced DRL algorithms for semantic communication and network optimization.

</details>


### [623] [LatentWave: JEPA Pretraining for Wireless Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06373)
*Ahmed Mohamed,Ahmed Aboulfotouh,Hatem Abou-Zeid*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: LatentWave is a wireless foundation model that outperforms masked-modeling baselines by leveraging a novel Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture to pretrain on diverse wireless data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing wireless models are task-specific or rely on masked input reconstruction, which biases them toward low-level signal details. There's a need for a more generalizable wireless model.

Method: The authors proposed LatentWave, which uses a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) and pretrains on wireless spectrograms and CSI. It employs per-channel patch embeddings and stochastic channel sampling for versatile usage.

Result: LatentWave was evaluated on tasks like signal classification, positioning, beam prediction, and LoS/NLoS classification, showing better transferability and performance compared to the baseline (WavesFM).

Conclusion: LatentWave demonstrates the value of latent-space pretraining and masking geometry, which differently impacts downstream task performance, and offers a highly adaptable solution for diverse wireless tasks.

Abstract: Wireless foundation models have emerged as a promising alternative to building separate models for each wireless task. However, existing approaches rely on masked input reconstruction, which can bias representations toward low-level signal details. In this paper, we propose LatentWave, a wireless foundation model pretrained using a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) on diverse wireless spectrograms and channel state information (CSI). By predicting masked regions in latent space, LatentWave learns representations that are more transferable out of the box across diverse downstream tasks. The proposed architecture employs per-channel patch embeddings with stochastic channel sampling during pretraining, allowing it to process variable antenna counts and improving usability across heterogeneous wireless configurations. We evaluate LatentWave on four downstream tasks: RF signal classification, 5G NR positioning, beam prediction, and LoS/NLoS classification, comparing against a masked-modeling baseline (WavesFM) pretrained on the same data. Additionally, we show that the masking geometry introduces a task-dependent inductive bias: frequency masking strongly favors channel-related tasks such as positioning and beam prediction, while region masking better preserves discriminability for signal classification.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [624] [AppAgent-Claw: CLI Is All You Need for GUI Automation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05171)
*Zhixue Song,Zhiheng Zhang,Yi Song,Chi Zhang*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: AppAgent-Claw converts GUI workflows into reusable skills for the OpenClaw platform using a record-once, replay-many approach with contextual metadata and robust execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the inefficiency of existing GUI automation solutions relying on repeated live inferences, which hampers OpenClaw’s goal of practical automation.

Method: A demonstration-driven system capturing contextual metadata, layered localization to handle visual shifts, and validation-coupled execution for reliable replay of recorded GUI workflows.

Result: The system enables efficient, reusable GUI-bound skills without the need for runtime inference, enhancing the OpenClaw platform's scope.

Conclusion: AppAgent-Claw effectively integrates GUI-based tasks into the OpenClaw ecosystem, offering a robust and diagnosable automation framework.

Abstract: The OpenClaw platform provides a practical foundation for automation through its skill-oriented architecture, organizing external capabilities into lightweight, reusable components that can be invoked efficiently through a command-line interface (CLI). However, a significant bottleneck remains: many real-world tasks are confined to graphical user interfaces (GUIs) with no stable API available. While LLM-based GUI agents offer generality, their reliance on repeated live model inference makes them too slow, costly, and inconsistent to serve as efficient OpenClaw skills. In this paper, we present AppAgent-Claw, a demonstration-driven system that converts GUI workflows into reliable, reusable skills without runtime inference. By following a ``record-once, replay-many'' paradigm, the system captures rich contextual metadata to facilitate robust execution. It employs a layered localization strategy to handle visual shifts and a validation-coupled execution model to ensure intended on-screen effects. AppAgent-Claw provides a practical, efficient, and diagnosable solution for integrating GUI-bound tasks into the OpenClaw ecosystem.

</details>


### [625] [Is This Edit Correct? A Multi-Dimensional Benchmark for Reasoning-Aware Image Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05172)
*Yixuan Ding,Wei Huang,Ruijie Quan,Xiaojuan Qi,Yi Yang*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper discusses the limitation of diffusion-based image editing models in reasoning-based tasks and introduces RE-Edit benchmark to address logical and contextual reasoning challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in current image editing systems that produce visually plausible edits but fail to reason about implicit contextual constraints embedded in user instructions.

Method: Introduced RE-Edit, a benchmark with 1,000 samples across five reasoning dimensions, and evaluated existing models. Also proposed a reasoning-guided post-edit method as a baseline.

Result: Most systems struggle with multi-dimensional reasoning despite creating high-quality visuals. Reasoning-guided methods show promise in addressing these shortcomings.

Conclusion: Reasoning-aware models are crucial to achieve logically consistent image edits, and explicit reasoning mechanisms can significantly enhance system performance.

Abstract: Diffusion-based image editing has achieved strong visual fidelity under natural language instructions, yet most existing systems still operate at the level of surface instruction following, without reasoning about the implicit contextual constraints embedded in real user requests. This often leads to visually plausible but logically inconsistent edits. In this work, we introduce RE-Edit, a benchmark for REasoning-aware image Editing that evaluates image editing systems across five complementary reasoning dimensions: physical, environmental, cultural, causal, and referential. RE-Edit comprises 1,000 carefully curated samples, each designed such that visual plausibility alone is insufficient and correct editing requires satisfying implicit logical constraints. To support fine-grained analysis, we establish dimension-aligned evaluation criteria and conduct a comprehensive study of ten open-source and two commercial image editing models. Our results show that even advanced systems frequently struggle with implicit multi-dimensional reasoning despite producing high-quality visuals. We further present a lightweight reasoning-guided post-edit baseline as an initial exploration, illustrating how inserting explicit reasoning can help mitigate such failures in a model-agnostic manner.

</details>


### [626] [The Virtual Roundtable: Multi-Agent Personas Simulating the Dynamics of Human Brainstorming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05178)
*Tim Dorn,Saara A. Khan,Julie Mumford*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper introduces a multi-agent AI-driven brainstorming system that tackles traditional human brainstorming biases to generate and rank diverse ideas.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations like groupthink and lack of diversity in traditional human brainstorming.

Method: The paper describes a two-phase system employing diverse AI personas for roundtable discussions, facilitated by an agentic guide, enforcing idea quotas and tracking idea lineage.

Result: In a case study on AI smart glasses, the system effectively produced diverse, relevant ideas while deepening discussion quality.

Conclusion: The architecture achieves a more effective and unbiased brainstorming output, fostering better idea generation and evaluation through simulated collaboration.

Abstract: As AI-driven product development accelerates, the bottleneck is shifting from how we build to what we build. Traditional human brainstorming faces challenges including groupthink, echo chambers, and limited diversity. To address this, we present a multi-agentic architecture that simulates roundtable brainstorming through two phases: divergent thinking to generate diverse ideas, and convergent thinking to evaluate and rank the most promising ones. The system employs diverse AI personas that engage in roundtable discussions, guided by an agentic facilitator that steers the discussion toward productive outcomes. Personas maintain private thoughts while commenting publicly, with ideas emerging organically throughout the discussion. Per-persona quotas on idea submissions and votes promote balanced participation while producing natural rankings. Throughout the session, the system tracks each idea's lineage, capturing how concepts originate and cross-pollinate over time. We demonstrate this approach through a case study generating consumer ideas for AI smart glasses, showing (i) it produces diverse, relevant ideas with insights into their evolution; (ii) the cumulative exchange of perspectives across personas cultivates a shared context that progressively deepens the quality of discussion and the ideas produced.

</details>


### [627] [The Role of Instructional Guidance in Generative AI-Assisted Learning: Empirical Evidence from Construction Engineering Education](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05509)
*Xiaoyu Hou,Bo Xiao,Hexu Liu,Shane Mueller*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The study looks at how structured interaction, guided by a five-step framework, can enhance AI-supported learning in construction education, showing improved open-ended task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore ways to improve student engagement and deeper learning processes when interacting with generative AI systems in self-directed construction education.

Method: A five-step prompting framework based on Generative Learning Theory (GLT) was developed and tested through a controlled experiment comparing slide-based, unprompted AI-supported, and prompted AI-supported learning conditions.

Result: Prompted AI-supported learning outperformed the other conditions in open-ended tasks, improving scores by about 2-3 points (p < 0.01), though no difference was found in multiple-choice tasks. Unprompted AI-supported learning had similar performance to slide-based learning.

Conclusion: The structure of interaction is critical to the effectiveness of AI-supported learning, with the proposed framework demonstrating potential for integrating educational principles into generative AI for construction education.

Abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly used to support self-directed learning, yet student interaction with such systems often remains unstructured, limiting engagement in deeper cognitive processes. This study examines how instructional guidance shapes student and AI interaction in construction education. A five-step prompting framework grounded in Generative Learning Theory (GLT) is introduced to guide learner interaction during review activities. A controlled experiment compares three learning conditions: slide-based learning, unprompted AI-supported learning, and prompted AI-supported learning. Learning performance is assessed using multiple-choice and open-ended tasks, and user experience is measured using the User Experience Questionnaire (UEQ). Performance differences are concentrated on tasks requiring explanation and reasoning. The prompted condition achieves higher open-ended scores, with an improvement of approximately 2 or 3 points on a scale of 18 (p < 0.01), while no significant differences are observed in multiple-choice performance. The unprompted condition remains comparable to slide-based learning. These findings indicate that the effectiveness of AI-supported learning depends on how interaction is structured. The proposed framework provides a basis for integrating learning science principles into generative AI systems for construction education.

</details>


### [628] [EEGDancer: Dynamic Emotion Latent Space Masked Modeling with Reinforcement Learning for EEG Continuous Emotion Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05855)
*Zhihao Zhou,Weishan Ye,Li Zhang,Gan Huang,Zhen Liang*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper presents EEGDancer, a framework for improving continuous emotion prediction from EEG data by learning structured emotional representations and optimizing prediction trajectories using reinforcement learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods fail to effectively capture long-range temporal dependencies, structured emotional dynamics, and often struggle with noisy high-dimensional EEG data, leading to challenges in continuous emotion prediction.

Method: EEGDancer employs a causal spatiotemporal VQ-VAE for structured emotional representation learning, Transformer-based masked modeling for temporal dependencies, and Soft Actor-Critic reinforcement learning for sequence-level trajectory optimization.

Result: EEGDancer outperforms traditional machine learning and deep learning approaches on SEED, SEED-IV, and Long-Term Naturalistic Emotion datasets, demonstrating its superiority in accuracy and emotional dynamics modeling.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses the challenges of continuous emotion prediction by integrating emotional latent space learning and trajectory optimization, laying the groundwork for improved EEG-based emotion modeling.

Abstract: Continuous electroencephalography (EEG) emotion prediction aims to model the temporal evolution of human emotional states from EEG signals. Unlike conventional discrete emotion recognition, continuous prediction requires capturing long-range temporal dependencies and coherent emotional dynamics. However, existing methods mainly rely on point-wise regression and directly model noisy high-dimensional EEG features, limiting their ability to characterize continuous emotional evolution.To address these challenges, we propose EEGDancer, a dynamic emotional latent space learning framework for continuous EEG emotion prediction. The framework integrates vector-quantized representation learning, masked temporal modeling, and reinforcement learning-based trajectory optimization into a unified architecture.Specifically, a causal spatiotemporal Vector-Quantization Variational Autoencoder (VQ-VAE) is designed to learn structured emotional prototypes and construct a discrete-continuous emotional latent space from EEG signals. Based on the learned latent representations, a Transformer-based masked dynamic modeling strategy captures long-range emotional dependencies and temporal evolution patterns. Furthermore, continuous emotion prediction is formulated as a sequential decision-making problem, and a Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) framework is introduced to optimize emotional prediction trajectories at the sequence level instead of frame-wise local fitting.Extensive experiments on the SEED, SEED-IV, and Long-Term Naturalistic Emotion datasets demonstrate that EEGDancer consistently outperforms existing machine learning and deep learning methods. Ablation studies further verify the effectiveness of the proposed latent space and reinforcement learning-based trajectory optimization for modeling continuous EEG emotional dynamics.

</details>


<div id='eess.SY'></div>

# eess.SY [[Back]](#toc)

### [629] [Development of a Structured Approach for Establishing Mission Engineering Requirements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05651)
*Taylor C. Fazzini,Daniel R. Herber*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper introduces a systematic framework to define or approximate mission effectiveness in scenarios lacking clear customer requirements, demonstrated through a military mission example.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to fill the gap left by traditional requirements engineering frameworks, which depend on customer input, making them unsuitable for scenarios like rapid development programs where requirements evolve or are absent.

Method: The paper proposes a framework that breaks down mission intent into components like context, functions, constraints, and critical dimensions, conducts feasibility assessments, prioritizes dimensions using Best-Worst Scaling, and incorporates a mission complexity factor.

Result: The proposed method demonstrates an ability to derive traceable mission requirements and facilitates integration with frameworks like Unified Architecture Framework (UAF) and Systems Modeling Language (SysML).

Conclusion: The approach provides a structured, adaptable methodology for evolving or undefined requirements, ensuring traceability and applicability to complex systems and missions.

Abstract: This paper addresses the question: How can mission effectiveness be systematically defined or approximated in the absence of customer requirements? Legacy requirements engineering frameworks presuppose customer input to define specifications but leave a gap in the process when stakeholder input is ill-defined or missing. Rapid build and development programs (such as military acquisition, space assets, infrastructure projects, etc.) often see requirement and objective evolutions throughout the proposal process, so a more adaptive method is needed. To address this gap, a structured approach is proposed that decomposes mission intent into mission context, functions, constraints, critical dimensions, effectiveness attributes, and architecture alternatives. This method conducts a mission feasibility assessment, prioritizes mission-critical dimensions using Best-Worst Scaling, and introduces a mission complexity factor to quantitatively understand the impacts of external mission difficulties, technology maturity, evidence and confidence standards, and mission utility. The resulting method provides a traceable basis for deriving Tier 1 and 2 requirements. The approach is structured to support future Unified Architecture Framework (UAF) and Systems Modeling Language (SysML) artifact integration. The proposed framework is demonstrated using a notional close air support mission example.

</details>


### [630] [Amortized Nonlinear Model Predictive Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05840)
*Francesco Pillitteri,Alberto Bemporad*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method to solve Nonlinear Model Predictive Control (NMPC) problems more efficiently using a state-dependent quadratic program augmented by a residual-correcting neural network.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current real-time NMPC algorithms are computationally intensive, limiting their applicability in resource-constrained hardware or high sampling-rate systems.

Method: The authors develop a single-network residual-corrector architecture that approximates the NMPC solution by combining an analytic baseline for QP parameters with a neural network to account for corrections. Training uses offline supervised learning and KKT-residual losses, and the QP is solved with a differentiable interior-point layer.

Result: The proposed method achieves orders-of-magnitude computational speedup compared to a conventional NLP solver while maintaining comparable tracking performance, tested on a three-link planar robotic arm.

Conclusion: This approach allows for real-time NMPC implementation in hardware-constrained environments or high sampling rates without significant loss in control performance.

Abstract: Nonlinear Model Predictive Control requires solving a constrained nonlinear program (NLP) in real-time at every sampling instant, a computational bottleneck that limits deployment on resource-constrained hardware or at high sampling rates. We address this challenge for the broad class of input-affine nonlinear systems to show that the optimal control move can be approximated by a state-dependent quadratic program (QP) whose cost parameters depend on the current state and reference. We propose a single-network residual-corrector architecture: a state-dependent analytic baseline provides initial QP parameters, and the network learns only the corrections needed to match the full NLP solution; the QP is solved by a differentiable interior-point layer, guaranteeing constraint satisfaction for the first control action. The network is trained offline on data generated by an NLP solver using a hybrid loss that combines supervised imitation and KKT-residual penalties. We validate the approach on a three-link planar robotic arm with Cartesian end-effector tracking, demonstrating orders-of-magnitude speedup over the NLP solver while maintaining comparable tracking performance.

</details>


### [631] [Attack Detection using Time Series Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.06347)
*Sribalaji C. Anand,Anh Tung Nguyen,George J. Pappas*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper develops a novel attack detection method for cyber-physical systems that bypasses the need for knowledge of the plant model, leveraging Google's TimesFM for efficient detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to enhance cyber-physical system security by solving the challenge of detecting model-free and model-based attacks without relying on inherent system models, which is critical as such systems are vulnerable to cyber threats.

Method: The authors derive optimal stealthy attack policies for non-linear and linear systems under a $χ^2$ detector and propose a model-agnostic attack detector using TimesFM as a surrogate for residual generation.

Result: Experimentation on the IEEE 14-bus power system demonstrates that the proposed TimesFM-based detector performs as well as or better than traditional methods, while also effectively substituting corrupted sensor measurements in scenarios lacking redundancy.

Conclusion: The TimesFM-based detector is a feasible, model-agnostic solution with practical applications in detecting and mitigating attacks in cyber-physical systems, providing enhanced system resilience.

Abstract: This paper addresses the problem of attack detection in cyber-physical systems without any knowledge of the plant model or its structure. A remotely located plant transmits sensor measurements to an operator over a network that is assumed to be under attack. We consider two classes of attacks: model-free replay attacks and model-based stealthy attacks. For the latter, we derive closed-form expressions for the optimal stealthy attack policy against a $χ^2$ detector, for both linear and nonlinear systems. We then propose a model-structure-free detector based on TimesFM, a time-series foundation model developed by Google Research, which serves as a surrogate residual generator operating in a zero-shot fashion. We show empirically that the TimesFM-based detector achieves a comparable or superior attack detection performance. The efficacy of the proposed approach is demonstrated numerically on the IEEE 14-bus power system. We also demonstrate that TimesFM predictions can serve as a substitute for corrupted measurements, a practical mitigation technique when classical redundancy assumptions fail.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [632] [RAINO: Anchoring Agents in Reality, A Systematic Review and Conceptual Framework for Realism in Agent-Based Modelling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05167)
*Loïs Vanhée,Melania Borit*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: This paper identifies inconsistencies in the operationalization of realism in Agent-Based Modelling and introduces the RAINO framework to address the issue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of a consistent and well-defined conceptual framework for realism in Agent-Based Models.

Method: The paper conducts a Systematic Literature Review to examine how realism is currently defined, achieved, and demonstrated in Agent-Based Modelling.

Result: The review finds that realism is often poorly defined, and existing methods lack clear justifications. The RAINO framework is developed to categorize and better understand realism through Reality Anchors and their application as Inputs or Outputs.

Conclusion: The RAINO framework broadens how realism is conceptualized, highlighting diverse interpretations and their impact on model development and evaluation.

Abstract: Realism is a central yet seemingly under-theorized concept in Agent-Based Modelling. This paper presents a Systematic Literature Review, aiming to identify how realism is currently operationalized and demonstrated. The results show that realism is often poorly defined and lacks a consistent conceptual framework. A wide variety of methods are used to achieve and demonstrate realism, but explanations of whether and why these methods are appropriate for their intended purposes are generally limited. Building on this review, we introduce the Reality Anchor, Input, Output (RAINO) framework. RAINO identifies the key structures used to argue for realism in Agent-Based Models, consisting of Reality Anchors (e.g., empirical data, formal theory, expert knowledge, common-sense expectations) and their application as model Input or Output. RAINO broadens existing perspectives on how realism is framed. It explains why different assessors may evaluate the realism of a model in different ways, and it shows how this broader framing can lead to significantly different approaches to model development.

</details>


<div id='econ.EM'></div>

# econ.EM [[Back]](#toc)

### [633] [Econstellar: An Open-Source AI-Augmented Research Engine for Computational Financial Econometrics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05705)
*Avishek Bhandari*

Main category: econ.EM

TL;DR: Econstellar is an open-source research engine that enables users to re-run and verify publication-grade econometric analyses directly from their web browsers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the challenges of verifying complex economic research findings, which often require specialised computation and are rarely available in a reproducible format.

Method: The platform leverages heavy computation on appropriate processors, incorporates an AI assistant for selection and interpretation of analyses, and ensures reproducibility by using the same code for both research and public access.

Result: The system provides 17 econometric methods, with verified live values accessible at a public endpoint. It also allows live regeneration of headline results from a financial contagion study, which is open-source and freely available.

Conclusion: Econstellar significantly reduces the gap between research claims and independent verification by making complex economic analyses accessible, reproducible, and interpretable for anyone.

Abstract: Turning a promising economic idea into a credible empirical finding is, in practice, an expensive undertaking: it demands a great deal of specialised computation, and the results are seldom released in a form that others can check or build upon. Econstellar is our response. It is an open, publicly served research engine that runs publication-grade financial econometrics from an ordinary web browser and explains what the results mean, so that a reader does not merely read a finding but can re-run it, vary its inputs, and trace exactly how it was produced. Three choices give the system its character. The heavy computation is placed on the processor that suits it, rather than forced onto hardware ill-matched to the task, which is much of the reason analysis of this kind is so rarely served to the public. An artificial-intelligence assistant selects and interprets the analyses but never originates a number, so every quantity it reports is a real computation the reader can reproduce. And the engine a visitor exercises is the same code that produced the figures in our published research. We expose seventeen econometric methods, each reported with a verified live value and reproducible at the public endpoint, computed under a single discipline: prices are treated as non-stationary and all methods are applied to returns. The system also regenerates, on demand, the headline result of an accompanying study of financial contagion, from the package that generated it. The platform is the working core of an active research programme spanning three software releases and three preprints, and it is available now, free and open-source, at a live public address. Our aim is a simple one: to shorten the distance between a research claim and the moment another person can independently verify it.

</details>


<div id='cs.DS'></div>

# cs.DS [[Back]](#toc)

### [634] [PivCo-Huffman](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05765)
*Marcin Zukowski*

Main category: cs.DS

TL;DR: The paper introduces PivCo-Huffman, a new variant of Huffman coding leveraging wavelet tree structures for improved performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance the efficiency of the traditional Huffman coding, making it more SIMD-friendly and achieving higher performance while maintaining competitive compression ratios.

Method: The authors propose a technique called PivCo-Huffman, based on wavelet tree data structures, and integrate ANS-coding for selective nodes to further refine compression ratios.

Result: PivCo-Huffman outperforms current state-of-the-art Huffman codecs in decoding speed while maintaining high compression efficiency close to ANS-based methods.

Conclusion: The proposed method offers a significant advancement in both decoding performance and flexibility, marking a potential improvement in compression algorithms leveraging Huffman coding.

Abstract: Huffman encoding has been an enduring technique for 70+ years, ubiquitous in compression algorithms since its invention. In this paper we propose a new approach to Huffman coding, based on a data structure from wavelet trees. The resulting pivot-coded Huffman (PivCo-Huffman) enables high-performance SIMD-friendly encoding and decoding operations. In our tests PivCo-Huffman consistently outperforms state-of-the-art Huffman codecs in decoding throughput. Additionally, we show how ANS-coding can be selectively applied to skewed nodes in this structure, yielding compression ratios approaching those of ANS-based codecs while preserving very high decompression speeds.

</details>


### [635] [Learning-Augmented Online Minimization with Dual Predictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05380)
*Christian Coester,Alexa Tudose,Alexander Turoczy*

Main category: cs.DS

TL;DR: This study introduces learning-augmented algorithms for metrical task systems and laminar set cover problems, leveraging machine-learned predictions of dual linear programs for improved online minimization performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the need for algorithms in online minimization that can utilize machine-learned predictions effectively while addressing the instability of primal solutions under small instance perturbations.

Method: The proposed approach uses machine learning predictions of stable dual linear program solutions to design learning-augmented algorithms for metrical task systems and laminar set cover.

Result: The algorithms achieve improved guarantees theoretically, extending the utility of dual-based predictions from offline and maximization to online minimization problems. Experimental validation was performed on the $k$-server problem and the parking permit problem.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates, for the first time, that dual predictions are stable and effective for online minimization problems, showing both theoretical and empirical promise.

Abstract: We present learning-augmented algorithms for two general classes of online minimization problems: metrical task systems and laminar set cover. Both algorithms achieve improved theoretical guarantees using machine-learned predictions of an optimal solution to the dual linear program. Unlike optimal primal solutions, which can change drastically under tiny instance perturbations, these dual solutions are much more stable, which ensures the existence of good (and learnable) predictions for families of similar instances. While previous work has used dual predictions in offline settings and for online maximization problems, our algorithms are, to the best of our knowledge, the first demonstration that such dual predictions can be effective for online minimization. Our theoretical results are complemented by experiments on the $k$-server problem and the parking permit problem.

</details>


<div id='cs.SD'></div>

# cs.SD [[Back]](#toc)

### [636] [GLASS: GRPO-Trained LoRA for Acoustic Style Steering in Zero-Shot Text-to-Speech](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05889)
*Jaehoon Kang,Yejin Lee,Kyuhong Shim*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper introduces GLASS, a framework for controlling acoustic styles in zero-shot text-to-speech using post-generation rewards instead of style labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to tackle the challenge in zero-shot TTS where style control is limited because speaker prompts entangle speaker identity with prosodic attributes, making modifications complex.

Method: GLASS uses LoRA-based adapters trained with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and style rewards like speech-token length and pitch, allowing modular and composable controls without retraining the main TTS backbone.

Result: Experiments showcased targeted style modifications (e.g., pitch and speaking rate), smooth interpolations, multi-axis composition through LoRA arithmetic, and preserved naturalness, intelligibility, and speaker similarity.

Conclusion: GLASS enables flexible, effective, and modular style control in zero-shot TTS systems, improving customizability without compromising quality or requiring extensive retraining.

Abstract: We propose GLASS, a framework for composable acoustic style control in zero-shot autoregressive text-to-speech (TTS) that learns controls from post-generation rewards rather than style labels. In zero-shot TTS, a speaker prompt often entangles speaker identity with prosodic attributes such as speaking rate and pitch, making it difficult to change style without changing the prompt itself. GLASS instead treats each acoustic attribute as a reward-defined control direction. For each control axis, GLASS freezes the TTS backbone and trains one lightweight LoRA adapter with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), using speech-token length and mean F0 as style rewards and WER as an intelligibility anchor. Because each control is represented as a LoRA weight update, independently trained adapters can be swapped, interpolated, and composed through linear LoRA arithmetic without retraining the backbone. Experiments on speaking rate and pitch control show targeted style shifts while preserving naturalness, speaker similarity, and intelligibility, and demonstrate smooth interpolation and multi-axis composition across independently trained adapters.

</details>


### [637] [Exploring LLMs for South Asian Music Understanding and Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.05522)
*Faria Binte Kader,Mohtasim Hadi Rafi,Shah Wasif Sajjad,Santu Karmaker*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper evaluates the performance of Large Language Models (LLMs) on South Asian classical music understanding and generation, identifying significant limitations in handling structurally distinct musical traditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address whether modern LLMs, which have shown advancements in Western music, can handle less explored but structurally complex music traditions like South Asian classical music.

Method: The authors introduce a 504-question benchmark for music understanding and a controlled prompting framework for music generation. They evaluate 33 LLMs focusing on Hindustani and Bengali classical music traditions.

Result: Frontier models achieve 85-90% accuracy in music understanding tasks, while most open-source models perform significantly worse. Music generation accuracy remains low, with stylistically faithful outputs only 40% of the time.

Conclusion: The study highlights the limitations in current LLMs' ability to model structurally distinct and culturally specific music, pinpointing an open challenge in culturally grounded music AI development.

Abstract: Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown promising results in music understanding and generation tasks.
